$$f(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \le 0 \end{cases}$$

**Derivative:**
$$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } x > 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } x \le 0 \end{cases}$$

**Comparison with Leaky ReLU:**

| Aspect | ReLU | Leaky ReLU |
|---|---|---|
| Negative side output | $0$ | $\alpha x$ (small slope) |
| Negative side gradient | $0$ | $\alpha$ (small but non-zero) |
| Dying neuron problem | **Yes** (irrecoverable) | **No** (weights keep updating) |
| Computational cost | Trivial $\max$ | Trivial + multiply |
| Used in | AlexNet, VGG, ResNet | YOLO, GANs |

**Gradient Flow Explanation:**
- In ReLU, if $z < 0$, gradient is exactly 0 → no learning on that path → neuron dies.
- In Leaky ReLU, even for $z < 0$, gradient is $\alpha = 0.01$ → learning continues at a slow rate → neuron can recover.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the **Softmax activation function** for a $K$-class classification problem. Prove that the sum of all Softmax outputs equals 1. Explain the **numerical stability issue** and derive the stable form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Hard Tanh** activation function. State its derivative, range, and compare it with the standard **Tanh** function. Why is Hard Tanh preferred in edge-AI / embedded systems? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Define Softmax for a $K$-dimensional logit vector $\mathbf{z} = [z_1, z_2, \dots, z_K]^T$:**
$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, K$$

**Step 2 — Prove that the outputs sum to 1:**

Compute the sum explicitly:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(z_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

Pull out the constant denominator:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(z_i) = \frac{1}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}$$

The numerator is the same sum as the denominator:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{K} \sigma(z_i) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} e^{z_i}} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Numerical stability issue:**

When logits are large (e.g., $z_i = 1000$ or $z_i = -1000$), the exponentials $e^{z_i}$ can overflow to $\infty$ or underflow to $0$, causing:
- **NaN** values in the output.
- **Division by zero** if the sum underflows to $0$.

**Step 4 — Derive the stable form (let $C = \max_j z_j$):**

$$\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} = \frac{e^{z_i - C} \cdot e^C}{\sum_j e^{z_j - C} \cdot e^C} = \frac{e^{z_i - C}}{\sum_j e^{z_j - C}}$$

Since $z_i - C \le 0$ for all $i$, the exponentials are bounded in $(0, 1]$. **No overflow or underflow can occur.**

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Hard Tanh Definition:**
$$f(x) = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{if } x < -1 \\ x & \text{if } -1 \le x \le 1 \\ 1 & \text{if } x > 1 \end{cases}$$

**Derivative:**
$$f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } -1 < x < 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Range:** $[-1, 1]$ (clipped).

**Comparison with Tanh:**

| Aspect | Tanh | Hard Tanh |
|---|---|---|
| Smoothness | Infinitely differentiable | Piecewise linear |
| Computation | Requires $\exp$ operations | Simple comparisons and multiplications |
| Approximation error | Exact | Linear approximation in $[-1, 1]$ |
| Use case | Standard deep learning | Embedded / edge inference |
| Non-differentiable points | None | At $x = \pm 1$ |

**Why Hard Tanh is preferred in edge-AI:**
1. **No floating-point exponential**: Microcontrollers and DSP chips often lack hardware $\exp$ units. Hard Tanh uses only `if-else` and multiplications.
2. **Deterministic latency**: Piecewise linear functions have predictable execution time, critical for real-time control systems.
3. **Lower power consumption**: Integer arithmetic (clip) is far cheaper than floating-point $\exp$.
4. **Memory efficiency**: No lookup tables for $\exp$ are needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the numerical stability constant** $C = \max_j z_j$ in Softmax derivations → **2 marks lost** in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing Sigmoid derivative** as $\sigma'(x) = e^{-x}(1 + e^{-x})^{-2}$ instead of the elegant $\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$ form → examiner expects the elegant form.
> 3. **Stating ReLU is differentiable at $x = 0$** — it is **not**. Use the subgradient $[0, 1]$ convention.
> 4. **Forgetting that Softmax is a generalization of Sigmoid**: For $K = 2$, $\text{Softmax}(z_1) = \sigma(z_1 - z_2)$.
> 5. **Not stating the zero-centeredness** of Tanh vs. Sigmoid — this is a **favourite KTU question** worth 2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing "dying ReLU" with "vanishing gradient"** — they are related but distinct: dying ReLU is **per-neuron permanent failure**; vanishing gradient is **exponential shrinkage** across layers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sigmoid** outputs in $(0, 1)$ — use for **binary output** layers; its derivative max is **$0.25$** → vanishing gradient in deep nets.
- **Tanh** outputs in $(-1, 1)$ — **zero-centered**; relation $\tanh(x) = 2\sigma(2x) - 1$ is a must-remember identity.
- **ReLU** is $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ — the **default hidden-layer activation**; sparse, fast, and gradient-preserving in the positive regime.
- **Leaky ReLU** uses slope $\alpha \approx 0.01$ on the negative side to **prevent the dying neuron problem** in GANs and deep CNNs.
- **Hard Tanh** is a **piecewise linear approximation** of Tanh; used in **edge / embedded AI** where $\exp$ is too expensive.
- **Softmax** generalizes Sigmoid to $K$ classes; outputs form a **probability distribution** (sum to 1); always use the **$\max$-subtraction** trick for numerical stability.
- The **derivative of Sigmoid** in terms of its own output ($\sigma(x)(1 - \sigma(x))$) is the canonical KTU form for backprop.
- **Vanishing gradient** is bounded by $(0.25)^L$ for $L$-layer Sigmoid networks; ReLU's derivative is exactly 1 in the active region, **breaking this bound**.
- All activation functions must be **non-linear** to enable universal approximation; a stack of linear layers is equivalent to a single linear layer.
- **Softmax + Cross-Entropy** gradient simplifies elegantly to $\hat{y} - y$, which is why this pair is the standard multi-class loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark derivation question, KTU expects: (1) the function definition, (2) step-by-step differentiation, (3) a final boxed equation, and (4) a 2-3 sentence engineering interpretation. Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.2 — it covers ~70% of Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loss Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loss Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Loss Function** (also known as a **Cost Function** or **Objective Function**) is a mathematical function $L(\hat{y}, y)$ that quantifies the discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{y}$ produced by a neural network model and the true target value $y$ from the training dataset. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the loss function serves as the *primary scalar metric* that the optimization algorithm (typically Gradient Descent or its variants) attempts to minimize during the training process.

Mathematically, for a dataset containing $N$ samples, the empirical risk is expressed as:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\bigl(f(x_i; \theta), y_i\bigr)$$

where $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters of the network, and $f(x_i; \theta)$ is the model's prediction for input $x_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The loss function is the *cornerstone* of supervised learning. Without a well-defined loss function, backpropagation cannot compute meaningful gradients, and the network weights cannot be updated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **student preparing for an exam** with an answer key:
- Your answer $\hat{y}$ = what you wrote
- The correct answer $y$ = the answer key
- The **Loss Function** is like the **marks deducted for each wrong answer**

If you lose only 1 mark per wrong answer (low loss), you are nearly perfect. If you lose 10 marks per wrong answer (high loss), you are far from the answer key. The goal of "studying" (training) is to **reduce these deducted marks to as close to zero as possible**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The loss function is essentially a "compass" that tells the neural network *which direction* and *how far* it is from the correct answer. The optimizer uses this compass to navigate toward better predictions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bold

The following metrics are universally referenced in the KTU 2024 Scheme Deep Learning module:
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: Average of squared differences
- **Cross-Entropy Loss**: Information-theoretic divergence measure
- **Hinge Loss**: Margin-based loss for classification
- **Epoch**: One complete pass through the training data
- **Convex Function**: A function where any line segment lies above the function graph
- **Gradient**: Vector of partial derivatives indicating steepest ascent
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: How Loss Functions Drive Learning

The training process follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Forward Propagation**: Input $x$ passes through the network layers, producing prediction $\hat{y} = f(x; \theta)$.
2. **Loss Computation**: The loss function $L(\hat{y}, y)$ computes a scalar error value.
3. **Backward Propagation (Backprop)**: Using the chain rule, the gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$ is computed with respect to every parameter.
4. **Parameter Update**: The optimizer updates weights as $\theta_{new} = \theta_{old} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}$, where $\eta$ is the learning rate.
5. **Iteration**: Steps 1-4 repeat for many epochs until the loss converges to a minimum.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why Forward Propagation?** Because we need an actual prediction to compare with the ground truth.
- **Why a Scalar Output?** Because comparing two numbers (scalars) is mathematically tractable; vector comparisons require aggregation.
- **Why Gradient Computation?** Because the gradient tells us the direction of steepest *ascent*; negating it gives the direction of steepest *descent*, which is what we need to minimize loss.
- **Why Learning Rate $\eta$?** Because we cannot take infinite steps; $\eta$ controls the step size in the parameter space.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Loss Function** | **Formula** | **Use Case** | **Domain** | **Key Property** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$ | Regression | $\mathbb{R}$ | Penalizes large errors heavily (quadratic penalty) |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert \hat{y}_i - y_i \vert$ | Regression (robust) | $\mathbb{R}$ | Linear penalty; robust to outliers |
| Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\bigr]$ | Binary Classification | $[0, 1]$ | Probabilistic interpretation |
| Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE) | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$ | Multi-class Classification | Probability simplex | One-hot encoded targets |
| Hinge Loss | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\max(0, 1 - y_i \cdot \hat{y}_i)$ | SVM / Margin Classification | $\{-1, +1\}$ | Encourages correct classification with margin |
| Huber Loss | $L_\delta(y, \hat{y}) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}(\hat{y}-y)^2 & \text{for } \vert \hat{y}-y \vert \le \delta \\ \delta \vert \hat{y}-y \vert - \frac{1}{2}\delta^2 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Robust Regression | $\mathbb{R}$ | Combines MSE and MAE; $\delta$ is a hyperparameter |
| Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_{i} P(i) \log\bigl(\frac{P(i)}{Q(i)}\bigr)$ | Distribution Matching | Probabilities | Asymmetric measure; $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) \ge 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION NOTE:** For KTU examinations, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to denote absolute value in formulas to avoid LaTeX table-breaking issues.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Loss functions are not limited to academic exercises; they drive critical engineering applications:
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: MSE loss in steering angle regression ensures smooth driving trajectories.
- **Medical Diagnosis**: Cross-entropy loss in tumor classification minimizes false negatives, which are life-critical.
- **Financial Forecasting**: Huber loss in stock price prediction balances sensitivity and robustness to market shocks.
- **Natural Language Processing**: CCE loss trains language models like transformers for next-token prediction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gradient of Mean Squared Error

The Mean Squared Error (MSE) for a single training example is given by:

$$L(\hat{y}, y) = (\hat{y} - y)^2$$

For a linear model where $\hat{y} = wx + b$, we need to compute the partial derivatives with respect to $w$ and $b$.

**Step 1: Express the loss explicitly in terms of model parameters.**

$$L(w, b) = (wx + b - y)^2$$

This step substitutes the model's prediction $\hat{y} = wx + b$ into the loss function, making the dependency on parameters explicit.

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial}{\partial w} (wx + b - y)^2$$

We use the chain rule: if $L = u^2$ where $u = wx + b - y$, then $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = 2u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial w}$.

$$= 2(wx + b - y) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial w}(wx + b - y)$$

**Step 3: Compute the inner derivative.**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w}(wx + b - y) = x$$

This is because $b$ and $y$ are constants with respect to $w$, and the derivative of $wx$ is $x$.

**Step 4: Combine to obtain the final gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = 2(wx + b - y) \cdot x = 2x(\hat{y} - y)$$

This gradient tells us that the update direction for $w$ is proportional to the prediction error and the input value $x$.

**Step 5: Similarly derive the gradient with respect to $b$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 2(wx + b - y) \cdot 1 = 2(\hat{y} - y)$$

The gradient with respect to the bias $b$ is simply twice the error, independent of the input.

**Step 6: Formulate the parameter update rule.**

$$w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = w_{old} - 2\eta x(\hat{y} - y)$$

$$b_{new} = b_{old} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = b_{old} - 2\eta(\hat{y} - y)$$

This update rule is the foundation of gradient descent; subtracting the gradient moves parameters toward the minimum.

### 3.2 Derivation: Binary Cross-Entropy Gradient

For a single sample, the Binary Cross-Entropy loss is:

$$L = -\bigl[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\bigr]$$

where $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ is the sigmoid output.

**Step 1: Recognize the elegant combined form.**

We can write the BCE loss in a unified form using the property that for $y \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$L = -\log(\hat{y}^y \cdot (1-\hat{y})^{1-y})$$

This rewriting highlights that BCE is essentially the negative log-likelihood under a Bernoulli distribution.

**Step 2: Compute the derivative of the sigmoid function.**

The sigmoid function has a special property: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z))$$

**Step 3: Apply the chain rule to find $\frac{\partial L}{\partial z}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left[\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right] = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$.**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 6: Multiply to obtain the beautifully simple final gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

This is why sigmoid + BCE is the **gold standard combination**: the gradient reduces to simply the difference between prediction and target, eliminating the vanishing gradient problem for this final layer.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Loss Functions

| **Property** | **MSE** | **MAE** | **BCE** | **Hinge** | **Huber** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Differentiable everywhere | Yes | No (at 0) | Yes (for $\hat{y} \in (0,1)$) | No (at margin) | Yes |
| Convex | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Robust to outliers | No | Yes | N/A | Somewhat | Yes |
| Output range | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |
| Gradient behavior | Large near minimum | Constant | Disappears at extremes | Zero at correct side | Small near min |
| Typical use | Regression | Robust regression | Binary classification | SVMs | Robust regression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "justify the choice of loss function," explicitly mention differentiability (needed for backprop), convexity (ensures global minimum), and robustness (handles noise/outliers).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Loss Function in the Training Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input Batch X] --> forwardA[Forward Propagation]
    forwardA --> predictA[Model Prediction yhat]
    labelA[True Label y] --> lossA
    predictA --> lossA{Loss Function L yhat, y}
    lossA --> scalarA[Scalar Loss Value]
    scalarA --> gradA[Compute Gradient dL dtheta]
    gradA --> optA[Optimizer Update theta]
    optA --> forwardA
    optA --> checkA{Converged?}
    checkA -- No --> forwardA
    checkA -- Yes --> endA[Trained Model]
```

### 4.2 Loss Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[Define Problem Type] --> branchB{Output Type?}
    branchB -- Continuous Values --> regB[Regression Problem]
    branchB -- Discrete Classes --> clsB[Classification Problem]
    regB --> outlierB{Outliers Present?}
    outlierB -- Yes --> hubB[Use Huber Loss]
    outlierB -- No --> mseB[Use MSE Loss]
    clsB --> binaryB{Binary or Multi-class?}
    binaryB -- Binary --> bceB[Use Binary Cross Entropy]
    binaryB -- Multi-class --> cceB[Use Categorical Cross Entropy]
    hubB --> endB[End Selection]
    mseB --> endB
    bceB --> endB
    cceB --> endB
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Loss Computation Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputX[Input Tensor X] --> modelBlock[Neural Network fX theta]
    modelBlock --> predOut[Prediction yhat]
    trueY[Ground Truth y] --> lossBlock[Loss Function L]
    predOut --> lossBlock
    lossBlock --> lossValue[Scalar Loss J]
    lossValue --> backprop[Backpropagation Engine]
    backprop --> gradOut[Gradients dJ dtheta]
    gradOut --> weightUpdate[Weight Update Rule]
    weightUpdate --> modelBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three diagrams above illustrate (1) where the loss function sits in the iterative training loop, (2) how to choose an appropriate loss function based on problem type, and (3) the modular architecture of loss computation within a deep learning system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a loss function. Why is it essential in training a neural network?**

**Model Answer:**
A loss function $L(\hat{y}, y)$ is a mathematical function that measures the discrepancy between the predicted output $\hat{y}$ of a neural network and the true target $y$. It is essential because:
1. It provides a **single scalar metric** to evaluate model performance.
2. It is **differentiable**, enabling gradient-based optimization.
3. It **guides weight updates** through backpropagation, allowing the network to learn meaningful representations.
4. Without a loss function, there is no way to determine *how* or *in which direction* to update the model parameters.

*(Expected length: 3-4 sentences with formula; Total marks: 3)*

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) loss.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **MSE** | **MAE** |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $(\hat{y} - y)^2$ | $\vert \hat{y} - y \vert$ |
| Penalty type | Quadratic (heavy) | Linear (uniform) |
| Robustness to outliers | Poor | Good |
| Differentiability | Smooth everywhere | Not differentiable at zero |
| Gradient magnitude | Proportional to error | Constant |

MSE penalizes large errors more severely, making it suitable when large deviations are particularly undesirable. MAE is more robust to outliers because the gradient does not grow with the magnitude of the error.

*(Expected length: Comparison table with brief explanation; Total marks: 3)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

---

**Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the role of the loss function in training a deep neural network. Discuss any three commonly used loss functions for regression and classification tasks, stating their mathematical formulations and the scenarios in which they are most appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The loss function is the **optimization target** in supervised learning. It converts the abstract goal of "good predictions" into a concrete, computable scalar. The optimizer minimizes this scalar by iteratively adjusting the network's parameters.

**Three Commonly Used Loss Functions:**

1. **Mean Squared Error (MSE) for Regression:**
$$L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$$
Used when: Continuous output is expected, and large errors should be penalized heavily (e.g., predicting house prices).

2. **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) for Binary Classification:**
$$L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\bigr]$$
Used when: Output is a probability for two classes (e.g., spam detection, disease diagnosis).

3. **Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE) for Multi-class Classification:**
$$L_{CCE} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{y}_{i,c})$$
Used when: There are more than two mutually exclusive classes (e.g., digit recognition, image classification with 10 classes).

---

**(b)** Derive the gradient of the Binary Cross-Entropy loss with respect to the logits $z$ when the output is passed through a sigmoid activation $\hat{y} = \sigma(z)$. Show that the gradient simplifies to $\hat{y} - y$, and explain why this is computationally advantageous. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Binary Cross-Entropy loss is:

$$L = -\bigl[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1 - y)\log(1 - \hat{y})\bigr]$$

The sigmoid function is $\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

**Step 1: Compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}$.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\left[\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right]$$

**Step 2: Combine the fractions over a common denominator.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}$ using the sigmoid derivative property.**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})$$

**Step 4: Apply the chain rule to obtain the final gradient.**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \frac{\hat{y} - y}{\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})} \cdot \hat{y}(1-\hat{y}) = \hat{y} - y$$

**Computational Advantage:**
- The gradient $\hat{y} - y$ is **simple and efficient** to compute, requiring only a subtraction.
- It **eliminates the vanishing gradient problem** for the final layer because the gradient is linear in the error.
- It allows **faster convergence** since the gradient magnitude is bounded and meaningful even when $\hat{y}$ is near 0 or 1.

---

**Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the role of the loss function in the deep learning training loop. Describe how backpropagation uses the loss to update network weights, and explain why convexity of the loss function is a desirable property. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Training Loop Diagram (described textually):**

Input $X$ → Neural Network $f(X; \theta)$ → Prediction $\hat{y}$ → Loss Function $L(\hat{y}, y)$ → Scalar Loss $J$ → Backpropagation → Gradients $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$ → Optimizer → Updated Weights $\theta_{new}$ → (Loop back to Neural Network).

**Backpropagation and Weight Updates:**

Backpropagation uses the chain rule to compute gradients of the loss with respect to each weight. For a weight $w$ in layer $l$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(L)}}{\partial a^{(L-1)}} \cdots \frac{\partial a^{(l+1)}}{\partial z^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial w^{(l)}}$$

The update rule is:

$$w^{(l)}_{new} = w^{(l)}_{old} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}}$$

**Why Convexity is Desirable:**
- A convex loss function has a **single global minimum**, ensuring that gradient descent converges to the optimal solution.
- Non-convex losses (common in deep networks) can have multiple local minima, potentially leading to suboptimal solutions.
- Convexity also guarantees that any local minimum is a global minimum, providing **convergence guarantees** for the optimization process.

---

**(b)** Consider a regression problem where the true value is $y = 5$ and the model predicts $\hat{y} = 3$. Compute the MSE loss and the MAE loss. If the model now predicts $\hat{y} = 7$, recompute both losses. Based on these calculations, explain how MSE and MAE differ in their sensitivity to error magnitude. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: $y = 5$, $\hat{y} = 3$**

**MSE Loss:**
$$L_{MSE} = (3 - 5)^2 = (-2)^2 = 4$$

**MAE Loss:**
$$L_{MAE} = \vert 3 - 5 \vert = \vert -2 \vert = 2$$

**Case 2: $y = 5$, $\hat{y} = 7$**

**MSE Loss:**
$$L_{MSE} = (7 - 5)^2 = 4$$

**MAE Loss:**
$$L_{MAE} = \vert 7 - 5 \vert = 2$$

**Analysis of Sensitivity:**

For an error of magnitude 2, both MSE and MAE yield losses of 4 and 2 respectively, regardless of the direction of the error. This demonstrates the **symmetry** of both losses.

**Key Difference for Larger Errors:**

Now consider an error of magnitude 4 ($\hat{y} = 9$):
- MSE: $(9 - 5)^2 = 16$ (quadratic growth)
- MAE: $\vert 9 - 5 \vert = 4$ (linear growth)

**Conclusion:** MSE grows **quadratically** with error magnitude, making it highly sensitive to large errors. MAE grows **linearly**, treating all errors with equal proportional weight. Therefore:
- **MSE** is preferred when large errors are particularly undesirable and should be heavily penalized (e.g., in control systems).
- **MAE** is preferred when robustness to outliers is required (e.g., in financial forecasting with occasional extreme events).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula Errors:** Students often write MSE as $(\hat{y} - y)$ instead of $(\hat{y} - y)^2$. Always include the **square**.
> 2. **Logarithm Mistakes:** In cross-entropy, students sometimes forget the **negative sign** in front. The loss must be non-negative, so the negative sign is crucial.
> 3. **Confusing Loss and Cost:** The "loss" applies to a single sample, while the "cost" is the average over all samples. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the Derivation:** In 7-mark derivation questions, you MUST show every step of the chain rule. Skipping the sigmoid derivative ($\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$) is a common reason for losing 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Differentiability:** When asked "why this loss function?", always mention that it must be **differentiable** for backpropagation to work.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Loss Function Definition:** A scalar function $L(\hat{y}, y)$ measuring prediction error; minimized via gradient descent.
- **MSE** = $\frac{1}{N}\sum(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$; used for regression; sensitive to outliers due to squaring.
- **MAE** = $\frac{1}{N}\sum\vert \hat{y}_i - y_i \vert$; robust to outliers; not differentiable at zero.
- **BCE** = $-\frac{1}{N}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$; for binary classification with sigmoid output.
- **CCE** = $-\sum\sum y_{i,c}\log\hat{y}_{i,c}$; for multi-class classification with softmax output.
- **Hinge Loss** = $\max(0, 1 - y\hat{y})$; for SVM-style margin classification.
- **Huber Loss** = Combines MSE (for small errors) and MAE (for large errors); robust regression.
- **Gradient of BCE with sigmoid** simplifies elegantly to $\hat{y} - y$.
- **Convexity** ensures a single global minimum; non-convex losses may have local minima.
- **Differentiability** is mandatory for backpropagation; non-differentiable points can be handled with subgradients.
- **Loss vs. Cost:** Loss = single sample error; Cost = average loss over the entire dataset.
- **Symmetry property:** Both MSE and MAE are symmetric (sign of error does not matter).
- **The chain rule** is the mathematical backbone connecting the loss function to weight updates across all layers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Training a Neural Network with Backpropagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Training a Neural Network with Backpropagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Backpropagation (Backward Propagation of Errors)** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs). It is a two-phase iterative process consisting of a **forward pass** (where input signals propagate through the network to produce an output) and a **backward pass** (where the error at the output is propagated backward through the network using the calculus **chain rule** to compute the gradient of the loss function with respect to every weight and bias).

The weights are then updated using a **gradient descent** optimization rule:
$$w_{ij} \leftarrow w_{ij} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{ij}}$$

where $w_{ij}$ is the weight connecting neuron $i$ to neuron $j$, $\eta$ is the **learning rate**, and $L$ is the loss function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Backpropagation is the foundational algorithm of Deep Learning. It is the engine that makes the training of deep, multi-layer networks computationally feasible by exploiting the modular structure of the chain rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Terminology:** The official KTU terminology requires you to refer to this as the **"Backpropagation Training Algorithm"** or **"Error Backpropagation Algorithm"**. Memorize both the equations and the order of operations: Forward Pass → Loss Computation → Backward Pass → Weight Update.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The Blaming Game"

Imagine a student taking a difficult exam and getting a wrong answer. The student thinks: *"I got it wrong, but which topic caused the mistake?"*

- **Forward Pass:** Solving the exam question step-by-step, starting from the first topic and moving forward.
- **Loss Function:** The total number of marks lost.
- **Backward Pass:** Tracing the mistake back to figure out **how much each preceding topic (each neuron/weight) contributed to the error**.
- **Weight Update:** Studying harder on the topics that caused the mistake (strengthening correct connections and weakening wrong ones).

In a neural network, **the loss at the output is "blamed" on each weight in proportion to its responsibility** for the error. This assignment of blame is precisely what the **chain rule of calculus** does.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Learning Rate ($\eta$)** | Typically $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Controls step size of weight updates |
| **Sigmoid activation** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Used in older networks; output in $(0,1)$ |
| **ReLU activation** | $\max(0, z)$ | Default for modern deep networks |
| **MSE Loss** | $L = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - t_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error for regression |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{i} t_i \log(y_i)$ | For classification tasks |
| **Epoch** | One full pass over training data | Hyperparameter |
| **Batch Size** | $1 \le B \le N$ | Number of samples per gradient update |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of Backpropagation Training

### Phase 1 — Forward Propagation
The input vector $\mathbf{x}$ is fed into the input layer, and activations are computed layer-by-layer until an output $\hat{y}$ is produced.

For a single neuron in layer $l$:
$$z^{(l)} = \mathbf{w}^{(l)} \cdot \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

$$a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the **activation function**, $z$ is the pre-activation (weighted sum + bias), and $a$ is the post-activation output.

### Phase 2 — Loss Computation
The predicted output $\hat{y}$ is compared with the true label $t$ using a loss function.

- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $L = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2$
- **Binary Cross-Entropy:** $L = -[t \log(\hat{y}) + (1-t)\log(1-\hat{y})]$

### Phase 3 — Backward Propagation (Error Signal)
The error (gradient) is propagated from the output layer back to the input layer. The fundamental local gradient at any neuron is defined as:
$$\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

This is computed recursively using the **chain rule**:
$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( \mathbf{w}^{(l+1)T} \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$$

The symbol $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**. This is the **single most important equation in backpropagation** — KTU examiners frequently ask students to derive or apply it.

### Phase 4 — Weight and Bias Update
The gradients with respect to weights and biases are then computed:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)} \cdot (a^{(l-1)})^T$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}} = \delta^{(l)}$$

And the parameters are updated using gradient descent:
$$w^{(l)} \leftarrow w^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}}$$

$$b^{(l)} \leftarrow b^{(l)} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Chain Rule

The chain rule is needed because $L$ depends on $w^{(l)}$ **indirectly**, through a chain of intermediate functions:
$$L \rightarrow \hat{y} \rightarrow z^{(L)} \rightarrow a^{(L-1)} \rightarrow z^{(L-1)} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow w^{(l)}$$

Without the chain rule, computing the gradient would require us to differentiate the loss with respect to **every weight independently**, which is computationally intractable for deep networks. The chain rule **recursively reuses previously computed gradients**, making the algorithm run in time proportional to the network size — a massive computational saving.

## 2.3 Variants of Gradient Descent

| Variant | Update Frequency | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Once per epoch (uses all $N$ samples) | Stable convergence | Slow for large $N$; can get stuck in local minima |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | Once per sample | Fast, escapes shallow local minima | Noisy updates |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Once per batch of $B$ samples | Balance of speed and stability | Requires tuning batch size $B$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Mini-Batch" is the default in practice:** It vectorizes computation (efficient on GPUs), provides stable gradient estimates, and fits in memory. The standard mini-batch sizes are $B \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $w \leftarrow w - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ |
| $z^{(l)}$ | Pre-activation at layer $l$ | $z^{(l)} = \mathbf{w}^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$ |
| $a^{(l)}$ | Post-activation at layer $l$ | $a^{(l)} = f(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\sigma'(z)$ | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z)) = a(1-a)$ |
| $L_{MSE}$ | Mean Squared Error | $L = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2$ |
| $\delta^{(L)}$ | Output-layer error (MSE) | $\delta^{(L)} = (\hat{y} - t) \odot f'(z^{(L)})$ |
| $\delta^{(l)}$ | Hidden-layer error | $\delta^{(l)} = (\mathbf{w}^{(l+1)T} \delta^{(l+1)}) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$ |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial w^{(l)}}$ | Weight gradient | $\delta^{(l)} (a^{(l-1)})^T$ |
| $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b^{(l)}}$ | Bias gradient | $\delta^{(l)}$ |
| $w_{new}$ | Updated weight | $w_{new} = w_{old} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Backpropagation powers virtually every deep learning system in production today — image recognition (CNNs), natural language processing (Transformers), speech synthesis, autonomous driving perception stacks, and medical diagnosis systems. Any improvement in optimization algorithms (Adam, RMSProp, etc.) builds on this same gradient-based foundation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output-Layer Error $\delta^{(L)}$

We start with the Mean Squared Error loss for a single training sample:
$$L = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2$$

The predicted output is $\hat{y} = f(z^{(L)})$, where $z^{(L)} = \mathbf{w}^{(L)} a^{(L-1)} + b^{(L)}$.

**Step 1:** Differentiate $L$ with respect to the predicted output $\hat{y}$:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \hat{y}} \left[ \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2 \right] = (t - \hat{y}) \cdot (-1) = (\hat{y} - t)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $\hat{y}$ with respect to the pre-activation $z^{(L)}$ (this is the activation derivative):
$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = f'(z^{(L)})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule to obtain $\delta^{(L)}$:
$$\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z^{(L)}} = (\hat{y} - t) \cdot f'(z^{(L)})$$

This is the **scalar error signal** at the output neuron. For vector outputs, the $\odot$ (Hadamard product) is used.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hidden-Layer Error $\delta^{(l)}$

For a hidden layer $l < L$, the loss depends on $z^{(l)}$ through the next layer's pre-activations:
$$L = L\left( \cdots, z^{(l+1)}, \cdots \right)$$

**Step 1:** Express $z^{(l+1)}$ in terms of $a^{(l)}$:
$$z^{(l+1)} = \mathbf{w}^{(l+1)} a^{(l)} + b^{(l+1)}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $z^{(l+1)}$ with respect to $a^{(l)}$:
$$\frac{\partial z^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} = \mathbf{w}^{(l+1)T}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $a^{(l)}$ with respect to $z^{(l)}$:
$$\frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}} = f'(z^{(l)})$$

**Step 4:** Apply the chain rule, summing over all neurons $k$ in layer $l+1$:
$$\delta^{(l)} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z^{(l)}} = \sum_k \frac{\partial L}{\partial z_k^{(l+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z_k^{(l+1)}}{\partial a^{(l)}} \cdot \frac{\partial a^{(l)}}{\partial z^{(l)}}$$

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( \mathbf{w}^{(l+1)T} \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot f'(z^{(l)})$$

This is the **backpropagation recursion** — once $\delta^{(L)}$ is known, all other $\delta^{(l)}$ can be computed by propagating the error signal backward.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

Consider a 2-2-1 fully connected network with **sigmoid** activation everywhere. Perform **one iteration of backpropagation** with the following setup:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Inputs | $x_1 = 0.5, \; x_2 = 0.3$ |
| Target output | $t = 0.8$ |
| Learning rate | $\eta = 0.5$ |
| Loss | $L = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2$ |

**Initial weights (input → hidden):**
$$w_{11} = 0.2, \quad w_{12} = 0.4, \quad w_{21} = 0.3, \quad w_{22} = 0.5$$

**Initial biases (hidden):** $b_1 = 0.1, \quad b_2 = 0.2$

**Initial weights (hidden → output):** $v_1 = 0.6, \quad v_2 = 0.7$

**Initial bias (output):** $b_3 = 0.15$

### Phase 1 — Forward Pass

**Step 1:** Compute pre-activations of the hidden layer:
$$z_1 = w_{11} x_1 + w_{21} x_2 + b_1 = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.3) + 0.1$$
$$z_1 = 0.10 + 0.09 + 0.10 = 0.29$$

$$z_2 = w_{12} x_1 + w_{22} x_2 + b_2 = (0.4)(0.5) + (0.5)(0.3) + 0.2$$
$$z_2 = 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 = 0.55$$

**Step 2:** Apply sigmoid activation to get hidden layer outputs:
$$h_1 = \sigma(0.29) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.29}} = \frac{1}{1.7483} \approx 0.5720$$

$$h_2 = \sigma(0.55) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.55}} = \frac{1}{1.5769} \approx 0.6341$$

**Step 3:** Compute pre-activation of the output neuron:
$$z_3 = v_1 h_1 + v_2 h_2 + b_3 = (0.6)(0.5720) + (0.7)(0.6341) + 0.15$$
$$z_3 = 0.3432 + 0.4439 + 0.15 = 0.9371$$

**Step 4:** Apply sigmoid to obtain the final prediction:
$$\hat{y} = \sigma(0.9371) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.9371}} = \frac{1}{1.5522} \approx 0.7185$$

**Step 5:** Compute the loss for this sample:
$$L = \frac{1}{2}(t - \hat{y})^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.8 - 0.7185)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.0815)^2 \approx 0.00332$$

### Phase 2 — Backward Pass (Output Layer)

**Step 6:** Compute the error signal at the output neuron. Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:
$$\sigma'(z_3) = \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) = (0.7185)(0.2815) \approx 0.2023$$

$$\delta_3 = (\hat{y} - t) \cdot \sigma'(z_3) = (0.7185 - 0.8)(0.2023) = (-0.0815)(0.2023) \approx -0.01649$$

**Step 7:** Compute gradients of weights connecting hidden layer to output:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial v_1} = \delta_3 \cdot h_1 = (-0.01649)(0.5720) \approx -0.00943$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial v_2} = \delta_3 \cdot h_2 = (-0.01649)(0.6341) \approx -0.01046$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b_3} = \delta_3 \approx -0.01649$$

**Step 8:** Update the output-layer parameters:
$$v_1^{new} = v_1 - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial v_1} = 0.6 - (0.5)(-0.00943) = 0.6 + 0.00472 = 0.6047$$

$$v_2^{new} = v_2 - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial v_2} = 0.7 - (0.5)(-0.01046) = 0.7 + 0.00523 = 0.7052$$

$$b_3^{new} = b_3 - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial b_3} = 0.15 - (0.5)(-0.01649) = 0.15 + 0.00825 = 0.1582$$

### Phase 3 — Backward Pass (Hidden Layer)

**Step 9:** Propagate the error to the hidden neurons:
$$\sigma'(z_1) = h_1(1 - h_1) = (0.5720)(0.4280) \approx 0.2448$$

$$\sigma'(z_2) = h_2(1 - h_2) = (0.6341)(0.3659) \approx 0.2320$$

$$\delta_1 = (v_1 \cdot \delta_3) \cdot \sigma'(z_1) = ((0.6)(-0.01649))(0.2448)$$
$$\delta_1 = (-0.00989)(0.2448) \approx -0.002422$$

$$\delta_2 = (v_2 \cdot \delta_3) \cdot \sigma'(z_2) = ((0.7)(-0.01649))(0.2320)$$
$$\delta_2 = (-0.01154)(0.2320) \approx -0.002677$$

**Step 10:** Compute gradients of input-to-hidden weights:
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{11}} = \delta_1 \cdot x_1 = (-0.002422)(0.5) = -0.001211$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{12}} = \delta_2 \cdot x_1 = (-0.002677)(0.5) = -0.001339$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{21}} = \delta_1 \cdot x_2 = (-0.002422)(0.3) = -0.000727$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{22}} = \delta_2 \cdot x_2 = (-0.002677)(0.3) = -0.000803$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial b_1} = \delta_1 \approx -0.002422, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial b_2} = \delta_2 \approx -0.002677$$

**Step 11:** Update the hidden-layer parameters:
$$w_{11}^{new} = 0.2 - (0.5)(-0.001211) = 0.2 + 0.000606 = 0.2006$$

$$w_{12}^{new} = 0.4 - (0.5)(-0.001339) = 0.4 + 0.000670 = 0.4007$$

$$w_{21}^{new} = 0.3 - (0.5)(-0.000727) = 0.3 + 0.000364 = 0.3004$$

$$w_{22}^{new} = 0.5 - (0.5)(-0.000803) = 0.5 + 0.000402 = 0.5004$$

$$b_1^{new} = 0.1 - (0.5)(-0.002422) = 0.1 + 0.001211 = 0.1012$$

$$b_2^{new} = 0.2 - (0.5)(-0.002677) = 0.2 + 0.001339 = 0.2013$$

### Final Summary Table (Post-Update Weights)

| Parameter | Old | New | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| $w_{11}$ | 0.2000 | 0.2006 | +0.0006 |
| $w_{12}$ | 0.4000 | 0.4007 | +0.0007 |
| $w_{21}$ | 0.3000 | 0.3004 | +0.0004 |
| $w_{22}$ | 0.5000 | 0.5004 | +0.0004 |
| $v_1$ | 0.6000 | 0.6047 | +0.0047 |
| $v_2$ | 0.7000 | 0.7052 | +0.0052 |
| $b_1$ | 0.1000 | 0.1012 | +0.0012 |
| $b_2$ | 0.2000 | 0.2013 | +0.0013 |
| $b_3$ | 0.1500 | 0.1582 | +0.0082 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the output-layer weights ($v_1, v_2$) changed by a larger magnitude ($\sim 0.005$) than the input-layer weights ($\sim 0.0006$). This is because the output-layer weights are **directly responsible** for the error, while the hidden-layer error is "diluted" by being multiplied by $v_1$ and $v_2$ in the chain rule. This phenomenon leads to the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep networks.

## 3.4 Learning Rate Sensitivity Analysis

| $\eta$ value | Effect | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Too small ($\eta \to 0$) | Slow convergence, may get stuck | Rare; only for fine-tuning |
| Moderate ($\eta \approx 0.01$) | Stable convergence | Default starting point |
| Too large ($\eta \to \infty$) | Overshooting, divergence, oscillation | Never |

The condition for stable convergence is $\eta < \frac{2}{\lambda_{max}}$, where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the Hessian of the loss function. In practice, adaptive optimizers like **Adam**, **RMSProp**, and **AdaGrad** automatically adjust $\eta$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Neural Network Topology (Input → Hidden → Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[/"Input x1 = 0.5"/]
    A2[/"Input x2 = 0.3"/]
    B1{{"Hidden h1\nBias b1=0.10\nsigma z1=0.29"}}
    B2{{"Hidden h2\nBias b2=0.20\nsigma z2=0.55"}}
    C1(["Output y_hat\nBias b3=0.15\nsigma z3=0.94"])
    D1>"Target t = 0.8"]

    A1 -- "w11 = 0.20" --> B1
    A1 -- "w12 = 0.40" --> B2
    A2 -- "w21 = 0.30" --> B1
    A2 -- "w22 = 0.50" --> B2
    B1 -- "v1 = 0.60" --> C1
    B2 -- "v2 = 0.70" --> C1
    C1 --> D1

    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style A2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style B1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38b00
    style B2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38b00
    style C1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style D1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

## 4.2 Backpropagation Data Flow (Forward + Backward)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1: Initialize weights w and biases b randomly"]
    S2["Step 2: FORWARD PASS - Compute z l = w l a l-1 + b l"]
    S3["Step 3: Apply activation a l = f of z l"]
    S4["Step 4: Compute Loss L = 0.5 times t minus y hat squared"]
    S5{"Step 5: Check convergence: Is L less than epsilon?"}
    S6["Step 6: BACKWARD PASS - Output error delta L = y hat minus t times f prime of z L"]
    S7["Step 7: Propagate error: delta l = w l+1 transpose times delta l+1 Hadamard f prime of z l"]
    S8["Step 8: Compute gradients: dL by dw l = delta l times a l-1 transpose"]
    S9["Step 9: Update weights: w new = w old minus eta times dL by dw"]
    S10["Step 10: Update biases: b new = b old minus eta times delta l"]
    S11["Step 11: Go to Step 2 for next epoch"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 -- "Yes - Converged" --> STOP(["STOP: Training Complete"])
    S5 -- "No - Continue" --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11
    S11 -.->|"Repeat"| S2

    style S1 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style S4 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style S5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style STOP fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#1a1a1a
```

## 4.3 Computational Graph of a Single Neuron

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[/"x1"/] --> M1(("Mult by w1"))
    N2[/"x2"/] --> M2(("Mult by w2"))
    N3[/"b"/] --> A3((("Add")))
    M1 --> A3
    M2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4(("Apply sigma"))
    A4 --> P1(["Output a"])

    P1 -.->|"Backward: dL/da"| A4b
    A4b -.->|"sigma prime"| A3b
    A3b -.->|"distribute"| M1b
    A3b -.->|"distribute"| M2b
    A3b -.->|"distribute"| N3b

    style N1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style N2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style N3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style P1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style A4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38b00
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Training System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Feature Vector x of dim n"]
    end

    subgraph HIDDEN["HIDDEN LAYER BLOCK"]
        H1["Linear Transformation: z1 = W1 x + b1"]
        H2["Nonlinear Activation: a1 = f1 of z1"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER BLOCK"]
        O1["Linear Transformation: z2 = W2 a1 + b2"]
        O2["Output Activation: y hat = f2 of z2"]
    end

    subgraph LOSS["LOSS COMPUTATION BLOCK"]
        L1["Compare y hat with target t"]
        L2["Compute scalar loss L of y hat, t"]
    end

    subgraph BACKPROP["BACKPROPAGATION BLOCK"]
        BP1["Output error: delta 2 = grad of L w.r.t. z2"]
        BP2["Hidden error: delta 1 = W2 transpose delta 2 Hadamard f1 prime of z1"]
        BP3["Weight gradient: dL by dW2 = delta 2 a1 transpose"]
        BP4["Weight gradient: dL by dW1 = delta 1 x transpose"]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE["PARAMETER UPDATE BLOCK"]
        U1["W2 new = W2 old minus eta times dL by dW2"]
        U2["W1 new = W1 old minus eta times dL by dW1"]
    end

    I1 --> H1 --> H2 --> O1 --> O2 --> L1 --> L2
    L2 --> BP1 --> BP2 --> BP3 --> BP4
    BP3 --> U1
    BP4 --> U2
    U1 -.->|"Updated weights for next iteration"| H1
    U2 -.->|"Updated weights for next iteration"| H1

    style INPUT fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style HIDDEN fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38b00
    style OUTPUT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style LOSS fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style BACKPROP fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style UPDATE fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
```
