$$\|\delta^{(1)}\| \leq (0.5 \times 0.25)^{2} \cdot \|\delta^{(3)}\| = (0.125)^{2} \cdot \|\delta^{(3)}\| = 0.0156 \cdot \|\delta^{(3)}\|$$

**Step 5.** For $L = 10$ layers, this scales to roughly $(0.125)^{9} \approx 7.6 \times 10^{-9}$. The gradient is effectively **zero** — no learning occurs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solutions Mapped to Root Causes

| Root Cause | Solution | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid/Tanh saturation | **ReLU / Leaky ReLU / GELU** | Derivative is $0$ or $1$ — no squashing. |
| Poor weight initialization | **Xavier / He Initialization** | Sets $\text{Var}(W) = 2/n_{\text{in}}$ to keep $\rho(W) \approx 1$. |
| Multiplicative chain decay | **Residual (Skip) Connections** | Creates an additive gradient path: $\delta \propto \delta + (\text{stuff})$, preserving magnitude. |
| Unstable batch statistics | **Batch Normalization** | Re-centers and re-scales activations per layer. |
| RNN-specific decay | **LSTM / GRU** | Additive cell-state update bypasses multiplicative gating. |
| Exploding in RNNs | **Gradient Clipping** | Caps gradient norm: $g \leftarrow g \cdot \min\!\left(1, \frac{c}{\|g\|}\right)$. |
| Plain SGD instability | **Adam / RMSProp** | Per-parameter adaptive learning rate dampens oscillations. |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — He Initialization Variance

For ReLU activation, we want $\text{Var}(\mathbf{a}^{(l)}) = \text{Var}(\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})$. Since $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \text{ReLU}(W \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)})$ and ReLU zeroes half the units, we need:

$$\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$$

This is the **He initialization** rule (He et al., 2015). The factor of $2$ compensates for ReLU killing half the variance.

**For tanh** (symmetric, no zeroing), the equivalent rule is **Xavier/Glorot**:

$$\text{Var}(W) = \frac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$$

The proof uses the identity $\text{Var}(\sum_i w_i x_i) = n_{\text{in}} \cdot \text{Var}(w) \cdot \text{Var}(x)$ under independence, with the $\text{ReLU}$ half-rectification contributing an extra factor of $2$ in the variance loss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Gradient Flow in Deep Networks

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputLayer[Input Layer x] --> layer1[Layer 1 W1, phi1]
    layer1 --> layer2[Layer 2 W2, phi2]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3 W3, phi3]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer L WL, phiL]
    layer4 --> lossCalc[Loss Function L]
    lossCalc --> bp[Backprop Engine]
    bp --> gradL[Gradient at Layer L]
    gradL --> gradL1[Gradient at Layer L-1]
    gradL1 --> gradL2[Gradient at Layer L-2]
    gradL2 --> grad1[Gradient at Layer 1]
    grad1 --> updateW[Weight Update Step]
    note1>Vanishing: gradient1 approx 0] -.-> grad1
    note2>Exploding: gradient1 approx INF] -.-> grad1
    style lossCalc fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style bp fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#003,stroke-width:2px
    style note1 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
    style note2 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Residual Connection Preserves Gradient

```mermaid
graph LR
    xIn[Input x to block] --> convA[Conv Layer W1]
    convA --> reluA[ReLU Activation]
    reluA --> convB[Conv Layer W2]
    convB --> addOp[Addition Node plus x]
    xIn -.-> addOp
    addOp --> reluOut[ReLU Output]
    reluOut --> nextBlock[Next Block]
    gradFlow[Backward Gradient delta] --> gradBranch[Main branch gradient]
    gradFlow --> skipBranch[Skip branch gradient equals delta]
    gradBranch --> mergeNode[Sum of gradients: large]
    skipBranch --> mergeNode
    mergeNode --> earlyLayer[Early Layer Receives Strong Signal]
    style addOp fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
    style skipBranch fill:#d0ffd0,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
    style mergeNode fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#006,stroke-width:2px
    style earlyLayer fill:#ffe0b0,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — LSTM Cell vs Vanilla RNN Gradient Path

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph vanillaRNN[Vanilla RNN Hidden Update]
        hPrevRNN[ht minus 1] --> whhRNN[Multiply by Whh]
        whhRNN --> actRNN[tanh]
        actRNN --> hCurrRNN[ht]
        lossRNN[Loss at time t] --> gradRNN[Gradient dL dht]
        gradRNN -.-> backRNN[Multiply by Whh transpose repeatedly]
        backRNN -.-> vanishRNN>Vanishing: Whh raised to power T]
    end
    subgraph lstmCell[LSTM Cell State Update]
        cPrevLSTM[ct minus 1] --> addLSTM[Additive update plus it times ct tilde]
        addLSTM --> cCurrLSTM[ct]
        lossLSTM[Loss at time t] --> gradLSTM[Gradient flows through addition]
        gradLSTM -.-> preserveLSTM>Unchanged gradient flows backward]
    end
    style vanishRNN fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style preserveLSTM fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Architectural Block — Mitigations Layered as a Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    rawInput[Raw Data] --> weightInit[Proper Weight Init: He or Xavier]
    weightInit --> activation[ReLU Family Activation]
    activation --> batchNorm[Batch Normalization Layer]
    batchNorm --> residual[Residual or Skip Connection]
    residual --> optimizer[Adam or RMSProp Optimizer]
    optimizer --> gradClip[Optional Gradient Clipping]
    gradClip --> stableTrain[Stable Deep Network Training]
    style rawInput fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style stableTrain fill:#c0ffc0,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 2]**
Define the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep neural networks. Why does it occur more severely in networks using sigmoid activation as compared to ReLU?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **vanishing gradient problem** refers to the phenomenon in deep neural networks where, during backpropagation, the gradients of the loss function with respect to the weights in the early (input-side) layers become extremely small (approaching zero), causing those weights to receive negligible updates and effectively halting learning in those layers.

It occurs more severely with sigmoid because the derivative of the sigmoid function is bounded above by $0.25$. During backpropagation, the chain rule multiplies these derivatives layer-by-layer. With $L$ layers, the effective gradient scaling is at most $(0.25)^{L-1}$ purely from the activation derivatives. For deep networks, this product collapses to near zero. ReLU, on the other hand, has a derivative of either $0$ or $1$, so the gradient magnitude is preserved across layers where neurons are active, avoiding the multiplicative squashing effect.

*(1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for chain-rule / layer-multiplication reasoning, 1 mark for sigmoid vs ReLU derivative comparison.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 2]**
List any **three architectural or training strategies** used to mitigate the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Use of ReLU (or its variants Leaky ReLU, PReLU, GELU) as activation function** — derivative is either $0$ or $1$, preventing multiplicative shrinkage across layers.
2. **He / Xavier weight initialization** — sets the initial weight variance to $\frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (He) or $\frac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (Xavier) so that the spectral norm of the layer transition stays close to $1$.
3. **Residual (skip) connections** — provides a direct additive path for gradients to flow from later layers to earlier ones, bypassing the chain of multiplications.
4. *(Bonus valid alternative)* **Batch Normalization** to re-center activations per layer.
5. *(Bonus valid alternative)* **LSTM cells** in RNNs to replace multiplicative hidden state updates with additive cell-state updates.

*(1 mark per correct strategy with brief justification; 3 strategies needed for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam Pattern]*

**(a)** With the help of a layered neural network diagram, explain the **vanishing and exploding gradient problems** in detail. Discuss the role of activation function derivatives in causing these phenomena. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain any **four solutions** to mitigate these problems. For each solution, briefly mention the underlying mathematical intuition. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a deep feedforward network with $L$ layers. The forward pass computes activations $\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = \phi(W^{(l)} \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$ for $l = 1, \ldots, L$. The loss is $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{a}^{(L)}, \mathbf{y})$.

**Step 2 — Backprop chain rule:** The gradient with respect to the first-layer weights $W^{(1)}$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{l=2}^{L} \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(1)}}{\partial W^{(1)}}$$

Each factor $\frac{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}} = \text{diag}(\phi'(z^{(l)})) \cdot W^{(l)T}$.

**Step 3 — Spectral analysis:** Taking the norm and assuming an average transition factor $\rho$:

$$\left\|\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W^{(1)}}\right\| \;\propto\; \rho^{\,L-1}$$

If $\rho < 1$, gradient **vanishes** exponentially. If $\rho > 1$, gradient **explodes** exponentially. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Activation derivative role:** Sigmoid satisfies $\phi'(x) \leq 0.25$, squashing the transition factor below $1$ even with unit-norm weights. Tanh has $\phi'(x) \leq 1$, slightly better. ReLU has $\phi'(x) \in \{0, 1\}$, eliminating the squashing. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Diagram description:** Draw a layered network (input → hidden 1 → hidden 2 → … → hidden L → output) with backward arrows labeled $\delta^{(l)}$, and annotate that the arrow magnitudes either shrink toward zero or grow toward infinity. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Practical symptoms:** Early layer weights barely update (vanishing) or loss diverges with `NaN` (exploding). **(1 mark)**

*(1 mark reserved for a clean, well-labeled diagram.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) ReLU Activation (2 marks):**
$$\phi_{\text{ReLU}}(x) = \max(0, x), \quad \phi'_{\text{ReLU}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & x \le 0 \\ 1 & x > 0 \end{cases}$$
The derivative is at most $1$, so the product of activation derivatives does not shrink across layers. Intuition: gradients pass through the network with no multiplicative decay along the active path.

**(ii) He / Xavier Initialization (2 marks):**
Sets $\text{Var}(W) = \frac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (He, for ReLU) or $\frac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$ (Xavier, for tanh). This balances the forward and backward variance, keeping the spectral norm $\rho \approx 1$. Intuition: prevents both the "everything collapses to zero" and "everything blows up" regimes from the start.

**(iii) Residual / Skip Connections (2 marks):**
A residual block computes $\mathbf{a}^{(l+1)} = \phi(\mathbf{a}^{(l)} + \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{a}^{(l)}))$. The backward gradient is $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l+1)}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(l)}}\right)$. The "$+1$" term ensures the gradient never vanishes. Intuition: an **additive** shortcut bypasses the multiplicative chain.

**(iv) Batch Normalization (1 mark):**
Normalizes activations to zero mean and unit variance per mini-batch, then applies a learnable scale and shift. Intuition: keeps activations in a stable regime where activation derivatives stay in healthy ranges, indirectly preventing gradient collapse.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the relationship showing how the **gradient magnitude scales as $\rho^{L-1}$** in a deep linear network, and explain why this leads to vanishing or exploding gradients based on the value of $\rho$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail how **LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) networks** solve the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs. Compare with the vanilla RNN update equation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a deep linear network with $L$ layers, each using weight matrix $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ and identity activation. Forward pass:
$$\mathbf{a}^{(l)} = W \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}, \quad l = 1, \ldots, L$$

**Step 2 — Unrolled output:**
$$\mathbf{a}^{(L)} = W^{L} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Loss gradient at output:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)} - \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 4 — Backward gradient at layer 1:** Using the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W} \supset (W^{T})^{L-1} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply spectral norm inequality** $\|\mathbf{M} \mathbf{v}\| \le \rho(\mathbf{M}) \|\mathbf{v}\|$ repeatedly:
$$\left\|(W^{T})^{L-1} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}}\right\| \le \rho(W)^{L-1} \cdot \left\|\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{a}^{(L)}}\right\|$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** The gradient magnitude scales as:
$$\left\|\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W}\right\| \;\propto\; \rho(W)^{L-1}$$
- $\rho(W) < 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient $\to 0$ as $L \to \infty$ → **vanishing**.
- $\rho(W) > 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient $\to \infty$ as $L \to \infty$ → **exploding**.
- $\rho(W) = 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient preserved across layers. **(2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for interpretation, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for concluding remark on choice of initialization)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Vanilla RNN update (1 mark):**
$$\mathbf{h}^{(t)} = \tanh\!\bigl(W_{hh} \mathbf{h}^{(t-1)} + W_{xh} \mathbf{x}^{(t)} + \mathbf{b}\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Vanilla RNN backward gradient (1 mark):**
The gradient of the loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{h}^{(t-k)}$ contains the term $(W_{hh}^{T})^{k}$. If $\rho(W_{hh}) < 1$, this product decays exponentially with $k$, causing long-range dependencies to be unlearnable. If $\rho(W_{hh}) > 1$, it explodes.

**Step 3 — LSTM cell state update (2 marks):**
$$\mathbf{c}^{(t)} = \mathbf{f}^{(t)} \odot \mathbf{c}^{(t-1)} + \mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}$$
where:
- $\mathbf{f}^{(t)} = \sigma(W_f [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ (forget gate)
- $\mathbf{i}^{(t)} = \sigma(W_i [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_i)$ (input gate)
- $\tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)} = \tanh(W_c [\mathbf{h}^{(t-1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(t)}] + \mathbf{b}_c)$ (candidate values)

**Step 4 — Why vanishing is solved (2 marks):**
The backward gradient of $\mathbf{c}^{(t-k)}$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{c}^{(t)}$ is the product of forget gates along the path:
$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{c}^{(t)}}{\partial \mathbf{c}^{(t-k)}} = \prod_{j=t-k+1}^{t} \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}^{(j)})$$
Crucially, this is a **product of forget gates** (each in $[0, 1]$), but the network *learns* the forget gate values. The **additive** input term $\mathbf{i}^{(t)} \odot \tilde{\mathbf{c}}^{(t)}$ provides a direct gradient path that does not require multiplications through the same gate chain — gradients can flow unchanged through the additive term, in contrast to the pure multiplicative vanilla RNN.

**Step 5 — Summary table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Vanilla RNN | LSTM |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden state update | Pure multiplicative | Additive (cell) + multiplicative (gate) |
| Long-range gradient | $\rho(W_{hh})^{T}$ — vanishes/explodes | $\prod \mathbf{f}^{(j)}$ — learnable & stable |
| Number of parameters | Low | ~4× higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Conflating "vanishing" with "saturation"**: Sigmoid *saturation* (output values pinned near $0$ or $1$) is a *cause*; the *problem* itself is the gradient being too small. State both clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting the matrix-norm step**: The bound $\|\delta^{(1)}\| \leq \rho^{L-1}\|\delta^{(L)}\|$ requires the use of spectral norm, not element-wise magnitude. Mentioning "matrix norm" earns the method mark.
> 3. **Confusing ReLU with sigmoid derivatives**: A common error is writing $\phi'_{\text{ReLU}}(x) = x$. ReLU's derivative is $0$ for $x \le 0$ and $1$ for $x > 0$.
> 4. **Omitting the role of initialization**: A 14-mark answer that lists ReLU, BN, and residuals but ignores **He/Xavier initialization** is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Not drawing/labeling a diagram**: The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests diagram-labeling. A poorly labeled or missing diagram typically costs $1$–$2$ marks per question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary):**

- **Definition triad:**
  - *Vanishing*: $\rho(W) < 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient shrinks to $\approx 0$ as $L \to \infty$.
  - *Exploding*: $\rho(W) > 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient grows to $\infty$ as $L \to \infty$.
  - *Healthy*: $\rho(W) = 1 \Rightarrow$ gradient magnitude preserved.

- **Activation derivatives (memorize):**
  - Sigmoid: $\phi'(x) \le 0.25$
  - Tanh: $\phi'(x) \le 1.0$
  - ReLU: $\phi'(x) \in \{0, 1\}$
  - Leaky ReLU: $\phi'(x) = \alpha$ for $x < 0$ (typically $\alpha = 0.01$), else $1$.

- **Key formula (board-favorite):**
  $$\left\|\delta^{(1)}\right\| \;\propto\; \rho(W)^{L-1} \cdot \left\|\delta^{(L)}\right\|$$

- **Initialization recipes:**
  - Xavier (tanh/sigmoid): $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{1}{n_{\text{in}}}$
  - He (ReLU): $\text{Var}(W) = \dfrac{2}{n_{\text{in}}}$

- **Architectural fixes (top 4 for KTU):**
  1. ReLU family activations
  2. He/Xavier initialization
  3. Residual (skip) connections
  4. Batch Normalization

- **Sequence-model fixes (RNN-specific):**
  1. LSTM / GRU cells (additive cell state)
  2. Gradient clipping (norm-based, value-based)

- **Optimizers that help:**
  - Adam, RMSProp, AdaGrad — per-parameter adaptive scaling reduces oscillation.

- **Symptoms to recognize in a loss curve:**
  - *Vanishing*: Loss plateaus at a high value; early-layer weights barely change.
  - *Exploding*: Loss becomes `NaN` or oscillates wildly; weight matrices overflow.

- **Must-cite historical anchor for theory credit:** Hochreiter (1991) — first formal identification of the vanishing gradient in RNNs; Bengio et al. (1994) — extended analysis.

- **Modern relevance:** Transformers, GPT, BERT, and ResNets all exist in their current form *because* of the gradient problem and its solutions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Techniques (Gradient Descent, Stochastic GD, GD with Momentum, GD with Nesterov Momentum)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Optimization Techniques (Gradient Descent, Stochastic GD, GD with Momentum, GD with Nesterov Momentum) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Deep Learning**, *optimization* refers to the mathematical process of **minimizing (or maximizing) an objective function** — typically the **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ — by iteratively updating the model's learnable parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The parameter set $\theta$ includes weights $\mathbf{W}$ and biases $\mathbf{b}$ of the neural network. The general iterative update rule that underpins every optimizer in this module is: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ where: - $\theta_t$ is the parameter vector at iteration $t$ - $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate** (a critical hyperparameter, typically in the range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$) - $\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ is the **gradient vector** of the loss w.r.t. the parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Optimization in deep learning is a *non-convex, high-dimensional, stochastic* problem. Unlike classical convex optimization, we settle for a *good local minimum* (or saddle point) because finding a global minimum is computationally intractable. ## 1.2 The Four Variants Covered in This Topic | Variant | Full Name | Core Idea | |---|---|---| | **GD** | Batch Gradient Descent | Uses the **entire dataset** to compute one update per epoch. | | **SGD** | Stochastic Gradient Descent | Uses **one sample** per update — high variance, fast iterations. | | **GD + M** | Gradient Descent with Momentum | Adds an *exponentially weighted moving average* of past gradients (a "velocity" term). | | **NAG** | Nesterov Accelerated Gradient | A *look-ahead* variant of momentum that evaluates the gradient at the *anticipated* future position. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker" 🏔️ Imagine you are a hiker **blindfolded** on a foggy mountain, and you want to reach the valley floor (minimum of the loss landscape). - **Gradient Descent (Batch):** You feel the slope only after surveying the **entire mountain** with a long measuring tape. Extremely accurate, but *agonizingly slow*. - **SGD:** You poke the ground with **one footstep** at a random spot and step downhill. Fast and cheap, but you *stagger and zig-zag* like a drunk person. - **Momentum:** You are placed inside a **heavy ball** that accumulates downhill velocity. You can roll past small bumps (local minima) and reach the valley faster. - **Nesterov Momentum:** You are the same heavy ball, but you are a *smarter* one — you peek at the slope **slightly ahead** of where momentum would carry you, then correct the course. This *anticipatory* correction reduces overshooting. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Checkpoint:** The *learning rate* $\eta$ is the "step size" of the blindfolded hiker. Too small → takes forever. Too large → steps off a cliff (diverges). Momentum $\gamma$ is the "ball's mass" — it remembers past direction. ## 1.4 Why Optimization is the Heart of Deep Learning Deep learning models may have **millions to billions** of parameters (e.g., GPT-scale models have $\sim 1.76 \times 10^{11}$ parameters). Backpropagation only *computes* the gradient; the **optimizer decides how to use it**. A poor optimizer leads to: - Slow convergence (wasting GPU-hours) - Getting stuck in saddle points (very common in high-dim) - Unstable training (loss explosions) > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The loss landscape of deep networks is **non-convex**, but research (Choromanska et al., 2015 — KTU board-accepted reference) shows that for large networks, most local minima tend to have *similar* loss values, making practical convergence feasible. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Setup Let the empirical risk (loss) over a dataset of $N$ samples be: $$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell\big(f(x_i; \theta),\, y_i\big)$$ where $\ell(\cdot)$ is the per-sample loss (e.g., cross-entropy, MSE) and $f(x_i;\theta)$ is the network's prediction. The gradient is the vector of partial derivatives: $$\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_1},\ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_2},\ \ldots,\ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_n} \right]^T$$ ## 2.2 The Four Optimizers — Operational Logic ### (A) Batch Gradient Descent (GD) - **Logic Step 1:** Iterate over the *entire* training set $D$ of size $N$. - **Logic Step 2:** Compute the gradient as the average loss gradient over all $N$ samples. - **Logic Step 3:** Perform a single parameter update. - **Logic Step 4:** One update = **one epoch**. **Update Equation:** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla_{\theta} \ell\big(f(x_i;\theta_t), y_i\big)$$ **Pros:** Deterministic, stable convergence path. **Cons:** Very slow for large $N$ (e.g., $N = 1.4 \times 10^6$ for ImageNet); memory-intensive; cannot update online. ### (B) Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) - **Logic Step 1:** Shuffle the dataset. - **Logic Step 2:** Pick **one** sample $(x_i, y_i)$ at a time. - **Logic Step 3:** Compute gradient using **only that one sample**. - **Logic Step 4:** Update immediately. - **Logic Step 5:** Repeat for all $N$ samples → completes one epoch with $N$ updates. **Update Equation:** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \ell\big(f(x_i;\theta_t), y_i\big)$$ **Pros:** Fast, supports online learning, can escape shallow local minima via noise. **Cons:** Noisy updates → high variance → oscillates near minimum. > [!NOTE] > **Mini-Batch SGD (KTU frequently asked):** The practical compromise — use a batch of size $m$ (commonly $m = 32, 64, 128, 256$). Each update uses $m$ samples, giving smoother but still fast updates. ### (C) Gradient Descent with Momentum (Classical Momentum) - **Logic Step 1:** Maintain a *velocity* vector $v_t$ initialized to zero. - **Logic Step 2:** Update velocity as a weighted sum of the current gradient and the previous velocity. - **Logic Step 3:** Use the velocity (not the raw gradient) to update parameters. **Update Equations:** $$v_{t+1} = \gamma \cdot v_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ where $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (typically $\gamma = 0.9$). **Pros:** Smooths out oscillations, accelerates convergence in ravines, escapes saddle points. **Cons:** Still may overshoot the minimum; the "look-ahead" correction of Nesterov is missing. ### (D) Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) - **Logic Step 1:** Compute the *interim* position $\tilde{\theta}_t = \theta_t - \gamma \cdot v_t$ (the "look-ahead" point). - **Logic Step 2:** Evaluate the gradient **at the look-ahead position** $\tilde{\theta}_t$, not at $\theta_t$. - **Logic Step 3:** Combine with momentum to form the final update. **Update Equations:** $$v_{t+1} = \gamma \cdot v_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma \cdot v_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Nesterov is "Smarter":** If the momentum is about to push us *uphill* (overshooting the minimum), the gradient at the look-ahead point will point *backward*, partially **canceling** the overshoot. This yields a *corrective* behavior that classical momentum lacks. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Optimizer | Update Rule | Key Hyperparameters | Memory Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Batch GD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \ell_i$ | $\eta$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | 2 | **SGD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla \ell_i$ | $\eta$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | 3 | **Mini-Batch SGD** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \nabla \ell_{i_j}$ | $\eta,\ m$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | 4 | **Momentum** | $v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$
$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$ | $\eta,\ \gamma$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | | 5 | **Nesterov** | $v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$
$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$ | $\eta,\ \gamma$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **Standard Hyperparameter Defaults (Board-Expected Values):** - Learning rate: $\eta = 0.01$ or $\eta = 0.001$ - Momentum coefficient: $\gamma = 0.9$ - Mini-batch size: $m = 32$ or $m = 64$ ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility These optimizers power virtually every modern AI system: - **Computer Vision (ResNet, YOLO):** SGD with momentum is the *de facto* standard for training image classifiers. - **Natural Language Processing (BERT, GPT):** Nesterov / Adam variants dominate. - **Reinforcement Learning:** SGD with momentum handles non-stationary objectives. - **Generative Models (GANs, Diffusion):** Adam (an adaptive variant) is preferred due to non-stationary discriminator losses. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the General Update Rule from First Principles We want to find $\theta^*$ such that $\mathcal{L}(\theta^*) \le \mathcal{L}(\theta)$ for all $\theta$ in a local neighborhood. **Step 1:** Use a first-order Taylor expansion of $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ around $\theta_t$: $$\mathcal{L}(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) \approx \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) + \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta\theta$$ **Step 2:** We want to choose $\Delta\theta$ such that the new loss is smaller, i.e., $\mathcal{L}(\theta_t + \Delta\theta) < \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. This requires: $$\nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)^T \cdot \Delta\theta < 0$$ **Step 3:** The most negative inner product occurs when $\Delta\theta$ is **anti-parallel** to the gradient. The smallest (most negative) directional derivative is achieved at: $$\Delta\theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) \quad \text{with } \eta > 0$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, the update is: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta\theta = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ This is the **canonical gradient descent rule**. > [!NOTE] > **Why a negative sign?** The gradient points *uphill* (direction of steepest ascent). To go *downhill*, we step in the *opposite* direction. Hence the minus sign. ## 3.2 Derivation of Momentum as an Exponentially Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) We begin from the recurrence $v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. Unrolling it for clarity: **Step 1:** Expand $v_{t+1}$: $$v_{t+1} = \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) + \gamma \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t-1}) + \gamma^2 \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_{t-2}) + \ldots + \gamma^t \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_0)$$ **Step 2:** Recognize this as a weighted sum of past gradients: $$v_{t+1} = \eta \sum_{\tau=0}^{t} \gamma^{t-\tau} \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_{\tau})$$ **Step 3:** The weights form a geometric series: $1 + \gamma + \gamma^2 + \ldots + \gamma^t = \dfrac{1 - \gamma^{t+1}}{1 - \gamma}$. The normalization factor is $1 - \gamma$. **Step 4:** For large $t$ (steady-state), the weight of a gradient $\tau$ steps ago is $\gamma^{t-\tau}(1-\gamma)$. Older gradients get exponentially decayed weight. **Step 5:** With $\gamma = 0.9$, the **effective number of past gradients** contributing significantly is roughly $\dfrac{1}{1-\gamma} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10$ steps. This is the *memory horizon* of momentum. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trick:** If asked "what is the effective horizon of momentum?" — Answer: $\approx \dfrac{1}{1-\gamma}$ steps. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Nesterov Correction **Step 1:** Classical momentum evaluates the gradient at the *current* position: $\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. **Step 2:** Nesterov's idea: evaluate the gradient at the *look-ahead* position: $$\theta_{\text{lookahead}} = \theta_t - \gamma v_t$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the velocity update: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$$ **Step 4:** The position update remains: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$. **Step 5:** **Geometric proof of the correction:** Decompose the Nesterov update as: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \gamma v_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$$ This can be rewritten as a *two-step* process: $$\text{(i) Jump ahead: } \theta_t^{\text{LA}} = \theta_t - \gamma v_t$$ $$\text{(ii) Correct: } \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t^{\text{LA}} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t^{\text{LA}})$$ The "jump ahead" is the momentum prediction; the gradient step from there is the *correction*. This is the foundation of the **convergence rate** improvement: Nesterov achieves $\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$ for convex functions vs. $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ for classical momentum. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite) | Property | Batch GD | SGD | Momentum | Nesterov | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Gradient Source** | Full dataset | 1 sample | Full / mini-batch | Full / mini-batch (look-ahead) | | **Updates per Epoch** | 1 | $N$ | 1 (batch) / $N/m$ (mini) | 1 (batch) / $N/m$ (mini) | | **Convergence Path** | Smooth, deterministic | Noisy, zig-zag | Smoother, faster | Smoothest, fastest | | **Local Minima Escape** | Poor | Good (via noise) | Excellent (via velocity) | Excellent (via velocity) | | **Overshoot Risk** | None | None | High | Reduced (corrected) | | **Convergence Rate (Convex)** | $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$ | | **Memory** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | | **Hyperparameters** | $\eta$ | $\eta$ | $\eta, \gamma$ | $\eta, \gamma$ | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Calculation Style) **Problem:** A loss function is $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = (\theta - 3)^2 + 1$. Starting at $\theta_0 = 10$, $\eta = 0.1$, and $\gamma = 0.9$ (for momentum). Run **two iterations** of (a) Batch GD and (b) Momentum GD. ### Part (a) — Batch GD **Iteration 1:** Compute gradient at $\theta_0 = 10$: $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_0) = 2(\theta_0 - 3) = 2(10 - 3) = 14$$ Update: $$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - \eta \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_0) = 10 - 0.1 \times 14 = 10 - 1.4 = 8.6$$ **Iteration 2:** Compute gradient at $\theta_1 = 8.6$: $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_1) = 2(8.6 - 3) = 2 \times 5.6 = 11.2$$ Update: $$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - \eta \cdot \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_1) = 8.6 - 0.1 \times 11.2 = 8.6 - 1.12 = 7.48$$ **Result after 2 iterations (Batch GD):** $\theta_2 = 7.48$ ### Part (b) — Momentum GD **Initialization:** $v_0 = 0$, $\theta_0 = 10$, $\gamma = 0.9$, $\eta = 0.1$. **Iteration 1:** $$v_1 = \gamma v_0 + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_0) = 0.9 \times 0 + 0.1 \times 14 = 1.4$$ $$\theta_1 = \theta_0 - v_1 = 10 - 1.4 = 8.6$$ **Iteration 2:** $$v_2 = \gamma v_1 + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_1) = 0.9 \times 1.4 + 0.1 \times 11.2 = 1.26 + 1.12 = 2.38$$ $$\theta_2 = \theta_1 - v_2 = 8.6 - 2.38 = 6.22$$ **Result after 2 iterations (Momentum):** $\theta_2 = 6.22$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Momentum accelerated the descent from $\theta = 7.48$ to $\theta = 6.22$ in the same two iterations. As more iterations proceed, the accumulated velocity continues to push $\theta$ faster toward the true minimum $\theta^* = 3$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Optimization Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Dataset D] --> B[Initialize Parameters theta0] B --> C[Initialize Velocity v0 equals 0] C --> D{Shuffle or Stream Samples} D --> E[Forward Pass f(xi, theta)] E --> F[Compute Loss L theta] F --> G[Backward Pass Backprop] G --> H[Compute Gradient grad L] H --> I{Choose Optimizer} I --> J1[Batch GD: full sum] I --> J2[SGD: single sample] I --> J3[Momentum: add gamma vt] I --> J4[Nesterov: lookahead grad] J1 --> K[Update Parameters theta t+1] J2 --> K J3 --> K J4 --> K K --> L{Convergence Met} L -->|No| D L -->|Yes| M[Trained Model] ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Comparison — Trajectory of Each Optimizer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Path1[Batch GD Trajectory] direction TB P1A[Start Point] --> P1B[Step 1] --> P1C[Step 2] --> P1D[Step 3] --> P1E[Minimum Smooth Path] end subgraph Path2[SGD Trajectory] direction TB P2A[Start] --> P2B[Zig] --> P2C[Zag] --> P2D[Zig] --> P2E[Zag Reaches Minimum Slowly] end subgraph Path3[Momentum Trajectory] direction TB P3A[Start] --> P3B[Accelerate] --> P3C[Build Velocity] --> P3D[Possible Overshoot] --> P3E[Settle Near Minimum] end subgraph Path4[Nesterov Trajectory] direction TB P4A[Start] --> P4B[Look Ahead] --> P4C[Corrected Step] --> P4D[Smoothed] --> P4E[Converges Fast and Accurately] end ``` ## 4.3 Momentum vs. Nesterov — Vector Diagram (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph M[Momentum Update Logic] M1[theta t] --> M2[Compute grad at theta t] M2 --> M3[v t+1 = gamma v t + eta grad theta t] M3 --> M4[theta t+1 = theta t - v t+1] end subgraph N[Nesterov Update Logic] N1[theta t] --> N2[Anticipate: theta t minus gamma v t] N2 --> N3[Compute grad at anticipated point] N3 --> N4[v t+1 = gamma v t + eta grad anticipated] N4 --> N5[theta t+1 = theta t - v t+1] end M4 -.Compared to.-> N5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The key difference is the *location* where the gradient is evaluated. Classical momentum evaluates at the *current* position $\theta_t$, while Nesterov evaluates at the *look-ahead* position $\theta_t - \gamma v_t$. This single relocation is the entire innovation of Nesterov. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Stochastic Gradient Descent. How does it differ from Batch Gradient Descent in terms of convergence stability and computational cost? [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Modeled)] **Model Answer:** **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** is an optimization algorithm that updates model parameters using the gradient of the loss computed on a **single training example** (or a small mini-batch) at each iteration. The update rule is: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \ell(f(x_i;\theta_t), y_i)$$ **Key Differences from Batch GD:** | Aspect | Batch GD | SGD | |---|---|---| | Data per update | Full dataset ($N$ samples) | 1 sample | | Updates per epoch | 1 | $N$ | | Convergence path | Smooth, stable | Noisy, oscillates | | Computational cost per update | High (processes all $N$) | Low (1 sample) | | Convergence near minimum | Steady | Wanders in a small region | | Can escape shallow local minima | No | Yes (via noise) | SGD performs **$N$ updates per epoch**, making it computationally cheap per update but introduces **high variance** in the parameter trajectory. Batch GD is **deterministic** but expensive for large datasets. --- ### Question 2: What is the role of the momentum coefficient $\gamma$ in Gradient Descent with Momentum? Why is a value around 0.9 commonly used? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modeled)] **Model Answer:** The **momentum coefficient** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ controls the contribution of past gradients to the current update via the velocity term: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma \cdot v_t + \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ **Role of $\gamma$:** - It acts as a *memory* parameter — higher $\gamma$ means longer memory of past gradients. - The effective memory horizon is approximately $\dfrac{1}{1-\gamma}$ steps. - It dampens oscillations in directions of high curvature and amplifies motion in consistent directions. **Why $\gamma = 0.9$?** - The effective horizon becomes $\dfrac{1}{1-0.9} = 10$ past iterations, which is empirically found to balance convergence speed and stability. - Values much higher (e.g., 0.99) cause excessive momentum buildup and overshooting. - Values much lower (e.g., 0.5) reduce momentum's benefit to almost that of plain SGD. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Part B, you are given an internal choice — answer EITHER (Or) option. Each long question has sub-parts worth 7 + 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** Compare and contrast Batch GD, SGD, GD with Momentum, and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient. Derive the update rules and discuss their convergence properties. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Modeled)] **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Update Rules (7 Marks) **Step 1: Batch Gradient Descent Update Rule** For a dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the total loss is: $$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell_i(\theta)$$ The gradient is: $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \ell_i(\theta_t)$$ The update is: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \nabla \ell_i(\theta_t)$$ **Step 2: Stochastic Gradient Descent Update Rule** SGD approximates the full gradient using a single sample $(x_i, y_i)$: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla \ell_i(\theta_t)$$ The expected value of this stochastic update equals the true gradient: $$\mathbb{E}\left[\nabla \ell_i(\theta_t)\right] = \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ **Step 3: Momentum Update Rule** A velocity term $v_t$ is introduced. The update equations are: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ Unrolling the velocity update: $$v_{t+1} = \eta \sum_{\tau=0}^{t} \gamma^{t-\tau} \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_\tau)$$ This shows momentum as an exponentially weighted moving average of past gradients with weight $(1-\gamma)\gamma^{t-\tau}$. **Step 4: Nesterov Update Rule** The gradient is evaluated at the *look-ahead* point $\theta_t - \gamma v_t$: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ The two-step decomposition: $$\theta_t^{\text{LA}} = \theta_t - \gamma v_t$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t^{\text{LA}} - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t^{\text{LA}})$$ #### Part (b) — Convergence Properties Comparison (7 Marks) | Optimizer | Convergence Rate (Convex) | Noise Level | Escapes Saddle Points | Memory | |---|---|---|---|---| | Batch GD | $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ | None | No | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | SGD | $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$ (in expectation) | Very High | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | Momentum | $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ | Low | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | | Nesterov | $\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$ | Lowest | Yes (best) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | **Discussion:** - **Batch GD** provides a smooth, monotonic decrease in loss, but it is *impractical* for large-scale deep learning since one update requires a full pass over the dataset. - **SGD** has high variance, leading to oscillation. However, this same noise helps escape **shallow local minima** and **saddle points**, which is critical in high-dimensional non-convex loss landscapes. - **Momentum** dampens the oscillations of SGD, builds up speed in consistent gradient directions, and is particularly effective in **ravine-shaped** loss surfaces. - **Nesterov** adds a *look-ahead* correction, providing the **fastest convergence rate** among the four ($\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$ for convex smooth functions). It is the most theoretically refined first-order optimizer in this module. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** Explain Gradient Descent with Momentum and Nesterov Accelerated Gradient in detail. Using the loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2$, starting from $\theta_0 = 20$ with $\eta = 0.1$ and $\gamma = 0.8$, perform 3 iterations of both methods and compare the results. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modeled)] **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Theory of Momentum and Nesterov (7 Marks) **Gradient Descent with Momentum** introduces a velocity vector that accumulates past gradients. The update equations are: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ **Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG)** modifies the above by computing the gradient at the *anticipated* future position. The equations are: $$v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_{t+1}$$ **Key Differences:** - Momentum evaluates the gradient at the *current* position $\theta_t$. - Nesterov evaluates the gradient at the *look-ahead* position $\theta_t - \gamma v_t$. - Nesterov achieves an improved convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$ for convex functions. - Nesterov reduces the *overshoot* that classical momentum experiences near minima. #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks) **Given:** $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = (\theta - 5)^2$, $\theta_0 = 20$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\gamma = 0.8$. **Gradient formula:** $\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta) = 2(\theta - 5)$. ### Method 1: Momentum GD **Iteration 1:** $v_0 = 0$ $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_0) = 2(20 - 5) = 30$$ $$v_1 = 0.8 \times 0 + 0.1 \times 30 = 3.0$$ $$\theta_1 = 20 - 3.0 = 17.0$$ **Iteration 2:** $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_1) = 2(17 - 5) = 24$$ $$v_2 = 0.8 \times 3.0 + 0.1 \times 24 = 2.4 + 2.4 = 4.8$$ $$\theta_2 = 17.0 - 4.8 = 12.2$$ **Iteration 3:** $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_2) = 2(12.2 - 5) = 14.4$$ $$v_3 = 0.8 \times 4.8 + 0.1 \times 14.4 = 3.84 + 1.44 = 5.28$$ $$\theta_3 = 12.2 - 5.28 = 6.92$$ **Final $\theta_3$ (Momentum) = 6.92** ### Method 2: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient **Iteration 1:** $v_0 = 0$, look-ahead position: $$\theta_0^{\text{LA}} = \theta_0 - \gamma v_0 = 20 - 0.8 \times 0 = 20$$ $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(20) = 30$$ $$v_1 = 0.8 \times 0 + 0.1 \times 30 = 3.0$$ $$\theta_1 = 20 - 3.0 = 17.0$$ **Iteration 2:** $$\theta_1^{\text{LA}} = 17.0 - 0.8 \times 3.0 = 17.0 - 2.4 = 14.6$$ $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(14.6) = 2(14.6 - 5) = 19.2$$ $$v_2 = 0.8 \times 3.0 + 0.1 \times 19.2 = 2.4 + 1.92 = 4.32$$ $$\theta_2 = 17.0 - 4.32 = 12.68$$ **Iteration 3:** $$\theta_2^{\text{LA}} = 12.68 - 0.8 \times 4.32 = 12.68 - 3.456 = 9.224$$ $$\nabla \mathcal{L}(9.224) = 2(9.224 - 5) = 8.448$$ $$v_3 = 0.8 \times 4.32 + 0.1 \times 8.448 = 3.456 + 0.8448 = 4.3008$$ $$\theta_3 = 12.68 - 4.3008 = 8.3792$$ **Final $\theta_3$ (Nesterov) = 8.3792** ### Comparison Table | Iteration | Momentum $\theta$ | Nesterov $\theta$ | Target | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 20.0 | 20.0 | 5.0 | | 1 | 17.0 | 17.0 | 5.0 | | 2 | 12.2 | 12.68 | 5.0 | | 3 | 6.92 | 8.3792 | 5.0 | **Conclusion:** In this particular 3-iteration example, **Momentum reached closer to the minimum (6.92) than Nesterov (8.38)**. This is because the *higher-order* curvature of this quadratic loss and the small number of iterations do not yet allow Nesterov's look-ahead correction to fully manifest. In practice, Nesterov's advantage becomes pronounced for **ill-conditioned, non-convex** loss surfaces (e.g., deep neural network training) over many thousands of iterations. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Sign error in update rule:** Students often write $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}$ — this is **gradient ASCENT**, not descent. Always use the **minus** sign. > > 2. **Confusing $v_{t+1}$ and $\theta_{t+1}$:** In momentum, students sometimes write $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - v_t$ (using *old* velocity). Correct: use the **freshly computed** $v_{t+1}$. > > 3. **Forgetting to evaluate Nesterov's gradient at the look-ahead point:** The single most common error. Nesterov's gradient is $\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t - \gamma v_t)$, **NOT** $\nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. > > 4. **Wrong initialization:** Velocity $v_0$ **must** be initialized to zero. Writing $v_0 = \theta_0$ is incorrect. > > 5. **Miscounting updates per epoch:** Batch GD = 1 update/epoch. SGD = $N$ updates/epoch. Mini-batch GD = $N/m$ updates/epoch. Many students interchange these. > > 6. **Not mentioning the variance/noise** when comparing SGD with Batch GD — this is a 2-mark item in "Compare and Contrast" questions. > > 7. **Missing the "look-ahead" intuition in Nesterov:** A complete answer must explain *why* Nesterov works (the corrective behavior), not just the formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Optimization Techniques (Module 2)** - **Optimization goal:** Find $\theta^*$ that minimizes $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ — a non-convex, high-dimensional problem. - **General update form:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta}\mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$. - **Batch GD** uses *all* $N$ samples per update — 1 update/epoch, smooth path, slow. - **SGD** uses *1* sample per update — $N$ updates/epoch, noisy path, can escape local minima. - **Mini-Batch SGD** is the *practical compromise* with batch size $m \in \{32, 64, 128, 256\}$. - **Momentum** adds a velocity term: $v_{t+1} = \gamma v_t + \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ with $\gamma \approx 0.9$. - **Effective momentum horizon** $\approx \dfrac{1}{1-\gamma}$ steps (e.g., 10 for $\gamma = 0.9$). - **Nesterov** evaluates the gradient at $\theta_t - \gamma v_t$ (the look-ahead position) for a *corrective* update. - **Convergence rate ranking (convex, smooth):** Nesterov ($\mathcal{O}(1/t^2)$) > Batch GD / Momentum ($\mathcal{O}(1/t)$) > SGD ($\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{t})$). - **Hyperparameter defaults:** $\eta = 0.01$, $\gamma = 0.9$, $m = 32$. - **Saddle points** (not local minima) are the *primary obstacle* in high-dim deep learning optimization — momentum and Nesterov help escape them. - **Always initialize** $v_0 = 0$ for momentum-based methods. - **Memory cost:** GD/SGD = $\mathcal{O}(1)$; Momentum/Nesterov = $\mathcal{O}(n)$ where $n$ is the number of parameters.

topics.title: Parameter-Specific Learning Rates (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Parameter-Specific Learning Rates (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) In deep neural network optimization, **Parameter-Specific Learning Rates** (also called *adaptive learning rate methods* or *per-parameter adaptive optimizers*) refer to a family of first-order stochastic optimization algorithms that dynamically adjust the learning rate **independently for each parameter** of the model, based on the historical gradient information observed during training. Unlike vanilla **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** which uses a single global learning rate $\eta$ for all parameters, these methods maintain a separate effective step size for every weight $\theta_i$, allowing rare but informative parameters to receive larger updates and frequently updated parameters to receive smaller updates. The three canonical algorithms under this category, namely **AdaGrad**, **RMSProp**, and **Adam**, are foundational to KTU Module 2 and form the backbone of modern deep learning training pipelines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Verbatim):** > *"Parameter-specific learning rates — Adaptive Gradient Algorithm (AdaGrad), Root Mean Square Propagation (RMSProp), Adam."* > Mastery of the exact update equations and their derivations is mandatory for ESE Part B questions. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Hiking Down a Foggy Mountain** > > Imagine you are a hiker trying to descend a fog-covered mountain (the loss landscape). You can only feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet (the local gradient). > > 1. **Vanilla SGD** is like taking steps of **the same length** everywhere. On a steep cliff, you might stumble; on flat terrain, you waste time. > 2. **AdaGrad** is a smart hiker who remembers *every* slope they have ever felt. The more they've felt a particular slope (a frequently updated parameter), the smaller the steps they take on it. *Problem:* Eventually, the hiker takes steps so small they stop moving entirely. > 3. **RMSProp** is the smarter cousin who uses a *sliding memory* (exponential moving average) of recent slopes only. The hiker forgets ancient terrain and adapts to current conditions. > 4. **Adam** is the most sophisticated hiker: it combines **momentum** (rolling downhill smoothly, ignoring micro-bumps) with **adaptive step sizes** (per-direction caution). It also wears *spectacles* called **bias correction** that fix its initial blurry vision. --- ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants (Standard Practice) The following standard hyperparameters are used across all three algorithms: - $\eta$ (eta): The **global learning rate**, typically **0.001** for Adam, **0.01** for AdaGrad/RMSProp. - $\epsilon$ (epsilon): A small smoothing constant, usually **$10^{-8}$**, used to prevent division by zero. - $\beta_1$: Decay rate for the first moment in Adam, default **0.9**. - $\beta_2$: Decay rate for the second moment in Adam/RMSProp, default **0.999**. - $\beta$ (in RMSProp): Decay rate, typically **0.9** or **0.99**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 AdaGrad — Adaptive Gradient Algorithm **Proposed by:** Duchi et al. (2011). The motivation is to scale the learning rate *inversely proportional to the square root of the sum of all past squared gradients*. ### Operational Logic (Why and How) - **Why?** Parameters that receive large gradients (e.g., weights for rare features) get their effective learning rate shrunk automatically. Parameters with small/infrequent gradients are allowed to move faster. - **How?** A running sum $r_t$ of squared gradients is maintained. The update is element-wise divided by $\sqrt{r_t}$, so directions with historically large gradients are dampened. ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Use `\vert` for absolute value) | Symbol | Formula / Update Rule | Purpose | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gradient | $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ | Compute gradient of loss at time $t$ | Standard backpropagation step | | Accumulator | $r_t = r_{t-1}1 + g_t \odot g_t$ | Element-wise sum of squared gradients | Penalizes frequently-updated params | | Parameter Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \dfrac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$ | Apply scaled update | Sparse data, NLP embeddings | | Initialization | $r_0 = 0$ | Zero-initialize accumulator | Standard convention | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall:** The accumulator $r_t$ is **monotonically non-decreasing**. Hence $\sqrt{r_t}$ grows without bound, and the effective learning rate $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_t}}$ decays to zero. Training prematurely stops — this is AdaGrad's fatal flaw. --- ## 2.2 RMSProp — Root Mean Square Propagation **Proposed by:** Geoff Hinton (Lecture 6e, Coursera, 2012). It was specifically designed to **fix AdaGrad's vanishing learning rate problem** by replacing the cumulative sum with an **exponential moving average (EMA)** of squared gradients. ### Operational Logic (Why and How) - **Why?** Old gradients should be *forgotten*; only recent gradient magnitudes should influence the adaptation. EMA gives higher weight to recent observations. - **How?** Introduce decay rate $\beta \in [0,1)$. The accumulator $r_t$ no longer grows unbounded; instead, it adapts to the recent *regime* of gradients. ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Update Rule | Purpose | Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gradient | $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ | Compute gradient | Backpropagation | | EMA Accumulator | $r_t = \beta \cdot r_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t \odot g_t$ | Forget old gradients | Stationary/non-stationary objectives | | Parameter Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \dfrac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$ | Apply scaled update | RNNs, GANs, general DL | | Typical $\beta$ | $\beta = 0.9$ | Balance memory | Standard value | > [!NOTE] > The effective "window" of remembered gradients is roughly $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ time steps. For $\beta=0.9$, this is **10 steps**; for $\beta=0.99$, it is **100 steps**. --- ## 2.3 Adam — Adaptive Moment Estimation **Proposed by:** Kingma & Ba (2015). Adam fuses the **momentum** concept (Polyak, 1964) with **RMSProp-style adaptation**, simultaneously maintaining: 1. The **first moment** $s_t$ (mean of gradients → like momentum). 2. The **second moment** $r_t$ (uncentered variance of gradients → like RMSProp). Additionally, **bias correction** compensates for the fact that both moments are initialized at zero, which biases them toward zero during early training. ### Operational Logic (Why and How) - **Why?** Momentum smooths oscillations and accelerates convergence in ravines; RMSProp adapts per-parameter step sizes; bias correction gives accurate moment estimates from $t=1$. - **How?** Two EMAs are computed, corrected, and combined into a single update that uses the *signal-to-noise ratio* $\frac{\hat{s}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$. ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Formula | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Gradient | $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ | Compute gradient | | 1st Moment (mean) | $s_t = \beta_1 s_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) g_t$ | Momentum-like accumulation | | 2nd Moment (var.) | $r_t = \beta_2 r_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2) g_t \odot g_t$ | RMSProp-like accumulation | | Bias-Corrected Mean | $\hat{s}_t = \dfrac{s_t}{1-\beta_1^{t}}$ | Removes initialization bias | | Bias-Corrected Variance | $\hat{r}_t = \dfrac{r_t}{1-\beta_2^{t}}$ | Removes initialization bias | | Final Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \dfrac{\hat{s}_t}{\epsilon + \sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$ | Apply final adaptive update | | Defaults | $\beta_1 = 0.9,\ \beta_2 = 0.999,\ \epsilon = 10^{-8}$ | Standard KTU/industry values | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world engineering utility:** Adam is the **default optimizer** in most modern frameworks (PyTorch `torch.optim.Adam`, TensorFlow `tf.keras.optimizers.Adam`, JAX). It is used to train Transformers (BERT, GPT), CNNs (ResNet, EfficientNet), GANs, and reinforcement learning policies. --- ## 2.4 Comparative Summary Table (Board-Exam Favorite) | Feature | AdaGrad | RMSProp | Adam | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Accumulator type | Cumulative sum | EMA of squares | Two EMAs (mean + var.) | | Has momentum? | No | No | Yes (via 1st moment) | | Bias correction? | Not needed | Not needed | Yes | | Handles non-stationary? | Poorly | Well | Very well | | Default LR | 0.01 | 0.001 | 0.001 | | Best for | Sparse gradients (NLP) | RNNs | Almost everything | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Every algebraic transition below is written explicitly. No step is skipped. This is the standard expected in KTU ESE full-mark answers. ## 3.1 Derivation of AdaGrad Update Rule We start from the vanilla SGD update and ask: *how can we scale the update based on gradient history?* **Step 1 — Vanilla SGD Update** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot g_t$$ where $g_t = \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ and $\eta$ is a single scalar. **Step 2 — Introduce a Per-Parameter Scaling Matrix** We want the $i$-th parameter to use learning rate $\frac{\eta}{\tau_i}$ where $\tau_i$ depends on gradient history. Let us define a diagonal preconditioner $G_t$ whose $i$-th diagonal entry is $\sqrt{\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau,i}^{2}}$. **Step 3 — Define the Accumulator** $$r_{t,i} = r_{t-1,i} + g_{t,i}^{2}$$ This is the running sum of squared gradients for parameter $i$. **Step 4 — Substitute into Update** $$\theta_{t+1,i} = \theta_{t,i} - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_{t,i}}} \cdot g_{t,i}$$ **Step 5 — Vectorized Form (the final KTU form)** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$$ with the convention $r_0 = 0$ and $\odot$ denoting the element-wise (Hadamard) product. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of RMSProp Update Rule AdaGrad fails because $r_t \to \infty$. The fix is to **forget old gradients**. **Step 1 — Recognize the Problem** In AdaGrad, $r_t = \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_{\tau}^{2} = g_1^{2} + g_2^{2} + \cdots + g_t^{2}$. Every past gradient contributes equally, even ancient ones. **Step 2 — Introduce Exponential Decay Weighting** We want a weighted average where recent gradients matter more. The exponential moving average of $\{x_t\}$ with decay $\beta$ is: $$EMA(x_t) = (1-\beta) x_t + \beta \cdot EMA(x_{t-1})$$ **Step 3 — Apply EMA to Squared Gradients** $$r_t = \beta \cdot r_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Expand to See the Decay** $$r_t = (1-\beta) \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta^{t-\tau} g_{\tau}^{2}$$ This shows that gradient $g_\tau$ contributes a weight $(1-\beta)\beta^{t-\tau}$ which decays geometrically. The effective horizon is $\frac{1}{1-\beta}$ steps. **Step 5 — Final RMSProp Update** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$$ with $r_0 = 0$ and $\beta \in [0,1)$ (typically 0.9). --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Adam Update Rule (Including Bias Correction) Adam combines **two** exponential moving averages and adds **bias correction**. The derivation has 6 explicit logical steps. ### Step 1 — Define the Two Moments We seek a momentum term (mean of gradients) and an adaptive term (mean of squared gradients). $$s_t \triangleq \text{EMA of } g_t \quad \text{with rate } \beta_1$$ $$r_t \triangleq \text{EMA of } g_t^{2} \quad \text{with rate } \beta_2$$ ### Step 2 — Write the Recurrence Relations $$s_t = \beta_1 s_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) g_t$$ $$r_t = \beta_2 r_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2) g_t^{2}$$ with $s_0 = r_0 = 0$. ### Step 3 — Identify the Initialization Bias Because $s_0 = r_0 = 0$, the early estimates are biased toward zero. Let us prove this for $s_t$. Unrolling the recurrence: $$s_t = (1-\beta_1) \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta_1^{t-\tau} g_\tau$$ Taking expectation, with $\mathbb{E}[g_\tau] = g$ (a constant mean): $$\mathbb{E}[s_t] = (1-\beta_1) g \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta_1^{t-\tau}$$ Using the geometric series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \beta_1^{k} = \frac{1-\beta_1^{n}}{1-\beta_1}$: $$\mathbb{E}[s_t] = g \left(1 - \beta_1^{t}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Bias Correction The actual first moment estimate is biased low by a factor $(1-\beta_1^{t})$. Dividing by this factor unbiases it: $$\hat{s}_t = \frac{s_t}{1-\beta_1^{t}}$$ Similarly for the second moment: $$\hat{r}_t = \frac{r_t}{1-\beta_2^{t}}$$ ### Step 5 — Construct the Adaptive Update Combine the corrected first moment (signal direction) with the corrected second moment (denominator scales by magnitude): $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{\hat{s}_t}{\epsilon + \sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$$ ### Step 6 — Interpretation as Signal-to-Noise Ratio Note that $\frac{\hat{s}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$ is a **dimensionless** quantity — it is essentially the gradient normalized by its recent standard deviation. This makes Adam's update **scale-invariant**, which is a key reason for its robustness. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Single Parameter) Let $\theta_0 = 1.0$, $\eta = 0.1$, $\beta = 0.9$ (RMSProp), $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$. Suppose gradients in successive steps are $g_1 = 0.5,\ g_2 = 0.3,\ g_3 = 0.8$. **RMSProp Calculation:** $$r_1 = 0.9 \cdot 0 + 0.1 \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 0.025$$ $$\theta_1 = 1.0 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{0.025 + 10^{-6}}} \cdot 0.5 \approx 1.0 - 0.316 = 0.684$$ $$r_2 = 0.9 \cdot 0.025 + 0.1 \cdot (0.3)^{2} = 0.0225 + 0.009 = 0.0315$$ $$\theta_2 \approx 0.684 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{0.0315}} \cdot 0.3 \approx 0.684 - 0.169 = 0.515$$ $$r_3 = 0.9 \cdot 0.0315 + 0.1 \cdot (0.8)^{2} = 0.02835 + 0.064 = 0.09235$$ $$\theta_3 \approx 0.515 - \frac{0.1}{\sqrt{0.09235}} \cdot 0.8 \approx 0.515 - 0.263 = 0.252$$ Notice how the learning rate *automatically shrinks* when the gradient spikes ($g_3 = 0.8$) — this is parameter-specific adaptation in action. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `N1`, `N2`) and **clean uppercase labels** without markdown formatting, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. ## 4.1 Functional Flow of Parameter-Specific Learning Rate Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD N1["INPUT GRADIENT G_T"] --> N2{"ALGORITHM
SELECTION"} N2 -->|ADA| N3["R_T EQUALS R_PREV PLUS G_T SQUARED"] N2 -->|RMS| N4["R_T EQUALS BETA TIMES R_PREV PLUS 1 MINUS BETA TIMES G_T SQUARED"] N2 -->|ADAM| N5["S_T EQUALS BETA1 TIMES S_PREV PLUS 1 MINUS BETA1 TIMES G_T"] N5 --> N6["R_T EQUALS BETA2 TIMES R_PREV PLUS 1 MINUS BETA2 TIMES G_T SQUARED"] N6 --> N7["BIAS CORRECTION
S_HAT EQUALS S DIV 1 MINUS BETA1 POWER T
R_HAT EQUALS R DIV 1 MINUS BETA2 POWER T"] N3 --> N8["STEP SIZE EQUALS ETA DIV EPS PLUS SQRT R_T"] N4 --> N8 N7 --> N9["STEP SIZE EQUALS ETA TIMES S_HAT DIV EPS PLUS SQRT R_HAT"] N8 --> N10["PARAMETER UPDATE
THETA EQUALS THETA MINUS STEP SIZE TIMES G_T"] N9 --> N10 N10 --> N11["OUTPUT NEW PARAMETERS"] ``` ## 4.2 Comparative Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BLOCK1["ADAGRAD MODULE"] A1["INPUT G_T"] --> A2["CUMULATIVE SUM R_T"] A2 --> A3["NO FORGETTING"] A3 --> A4["MONOTONIC DECAY OF LR"] end subgraph BLOCK2["RMSPROP MODULE"] B1["INPUT G_T"] --> B2["EMA R_T BETA 0.9"] B2 --> B3["FIXES VANISHING LR"] B3 --> B4["SUITABLE FOR NON STATIONARY"] end subgraph BLOCK3["ADAM MODULE"] C1["INPUT G_T"] --> C2["FIRST MOMENT S_T"] C2 --> C3["SECOND MOMENT R_T"] C3 --> C4["BIAS CORRECTION"] C4 --> C5["MOMENTUM PLUS ADAPTIVE"] end BLOCK1 --> MERGE["EVOLUTION PATH ADA TO RMS TO ADAM"] BLOCK2 --> MERGE BLOCK3 --> MERGE MERGE --> OUT["MODERN DEFAULT OPTIMIZER"] ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Optimizers (Evolution View) ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["VANILLA SGD
SINGLE LEARNING RATE"] ROOT --> A1["ADAGRAD 2011
PER PARAMETER ACCUMULATION"] A1 --> A2["RMSPROP 2012
EXPONENTIAL MOVING AVERAGE"] A2 --> A3["ADAM 2015
FIRST AND SECOND MOMENT PLUS BIAS CORRECTION"] A3 --> A4["ADAMW 2019
DECOUPLED WEIGHT DECAY"] A4 --> A5["LION 2023
SIGN BASED MOMENTUM"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > All questions below are modeled exactly after the **KTU 2024 Scheme** End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern: > - **Part A:** 3-mark short answer (typically 2 questions from each module). > - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive with **internal choice** between two sub-questions. The first sub-part (a) carries **7 marks**, the second (b) carries **7 marks**. --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Differentiate between AdaGrad and RMSProp optimizers. Why does RMSProp perform better on non-stationary loss landscapes?** **Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):** AdaGrad accumulates squared gradients as a **cumulative sum** $r_t = r_{t-1} + g_t^{2}$, which grows monotonically and forces the learning rate to decay to zero prematurely. **RMSProp** replaces this with an **exponential moving average** $r_t = \beta r_{t-1} + (1-\beta) g_t^{2}$, giving higher weight to recent gradients and forgetting older ones. Consequently, RMSProp maintains a **non-vanishing effective learning rate**, making it robust to **non-stationary** objectives such as those encountered when training RNNs on time-varying data. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **What is the role of bias correction in the Adam optimizer? Derive the corrected first moment estimate.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 50–70 words):** Because both moments $s_t$ and $r_t$ are initialized to zero, their early estimates are biased toward zero. **Bias correction** divides these estimates by $(1-\beta_1^{t})$ and $(1-\beta_2^{t})$ respectively, yielding unbiased estimates $\hat{s}_t$ and $\hat{r}_t$. The corrected first moment is: $$\hat{s}_t = \frac{s_t}{1-\beta_1^{t}}$$ where $s_t = \beta_1 s_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1)g_t$. Without this correction, the early training steps would be overly conservative. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` — *Internal Choice between A and B* ### Question A (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the update rule for the RMSProp optimizer. How does it overcome the vanishing learning rate problem of AdaGrad?** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 mark): State AdaGrad's Update** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{\sum_{\tau=1}^{t} g_\tau^{2}}} \odot g_t$$ The cumulative sum $\sum g_\tau^{2}$ grows without bound, shrinking the effective learning rate to zero. **Step 2 (2 marks): Introduce Exponential Decay** Define decay parameter $\beta \in [0,1)$. Replace the cumulative sum with an EMA: $$r_t = \beta \cdot r_{t-1} + (1-\beta) \cdot g_t^{2}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Expand and Justify** $$r_t = (1-\beta) \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta^{t-\tau} g_\tau^{2}$$ Old gradients receive exponentially smaller weights — hence they are "forgotten". The accumulator now reflects only the recent regime. **Step 4 (2 marks): Final RMSProp Update and Comparison** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$$ Unlike AdaGrad, $\sqrt{r_t}$ is bounded and adapts to recent gradient statistics, ensuring a non-vanishing effective learning rate. --- ### Question A (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the Adam optimizer with its complete update equations. Show how it integrates momentum and RMSProp, and discuss why bias correction is necessary.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 mark): Motivation** Adam = Momentum (Polyak) + RMSProp. It maintains both the **mean** and **uncentered variance** of gradients using EMAs. **Step 2 (2 marks): First and Second Moment** $$s_t = \beta_1 s_{t-1} + (1-\beta_1) g_t$$ $$r_t = \beta_2 r_{t-1} + (1-\beta_2) g_t^{2}$$ These are initialized as $s_0 = r_0 = 0$. **Step 3 (2 marks): Bias Correction Derivation** Expectation of $s_t$: $\mathbb{E}[s_t] = g(1-\beta_1^{t})$. Correction: $$\hat{s}_t = \frac{s_t}{1-\beta_1^{t}},\quad \hat{r}_t = \frac{r_t}{1-\beta_2^{t}}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks): Final Update Equation** $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{\hat{s}_t}{\epsilon + \sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$$ Defaults: $\beta_1=0.9,\ \beta_2=0.999,\ \eta=0.001,\ \epsilon=10^{-8}$. The update is scale-invariant because $\frac{\hat{s}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$ is dimensionless. --- ## 5.3 PART B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` — *Alternative Choice* ### Question B (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the AdaGrad algorithm. State its update equations and discuss its limitations in deep network training.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 mark): Definition** AdaGrad adapts the learning rate per parameter using the **historical sum of squared gradients**. **Step 2 (2 marks): Update Equations** $$r_t = r_{t-1} + g_t^{2}$$ $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \frac{\eta}{\epsilon + \sqrt{r_t}} \odot g_t$$ $$r_0 = 0$$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Intuition** Parameters with large accumulated gradients get a small effective LR; rarely-updated parameters get a large LR. This is ideal for **sparse features** (e.g., NLP embeddings). **Step 4 (2 marks): Limitation** Because $r_t$ is monotonically non-decreasing, $\frac{\eta}{\sqrt{r_t}} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Training **prematurely halts**. This is fatal for deep networks that require sustained learning across many epochs. RMSProp was designed to fix exactly this flaw. --- ### Question B (b) — 7 Marks **Derive the bias-corrected estimates used in Adam. Why are they necessary during early training?** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 mark): Unroll First Moment** $$s_t = (1-\beta_1) \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta_1^{t-\tau} g_\tau$$ **Step 2 (2 marks): Take Expectation** $$\mathbb{E}[s_t] = (1-\beta_1) \sum_{\tau=1}^{t} \beta_1^{t-\tau} \mathbb{E}[g_\tau]$$ For constant mean $g$: $$\mathbb{E}[s_t] = g \left(1-\beta_1^{t}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Bias Identification** The true first moment is $g$, but our estimator gives $g(1-\beta_1^{t})$. The bias factor is $(1-\beta_1^{t})$. **Step 4 (2 marks): Correction & Necessity** $$\hat{s}_t = \frac{s_t}{1-\beta_1^{t}}$$ Without correction, the magnitude of updates in early steps (when $\beta_1^{t} \approx 0$) would be much smaller than intended, slowing convergence. Bias correction ensures accurate moment estimates from $t=1$ onwards. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse the symbols.** In Adam, $s_t$ is the *first moment* (not $v_t$). Use the same notation as the syllabus. > 2. **Bias correction is mandatory** for Adam — omitting it costs 2 marks. RMSProp and AdaGrad do **not** use bias correction. > 3. **The square root** in the denominator is **element-wise** ($\sqrt{r_t}$, not $\sqrt{r_t \cdot r_t}$). > 4. **$\odot$ denotes the Hadamard product**, not matrix multiplication. State this explicitly. > 5. **Initialization $r_0 = 0$** must be mentioned for full marks. > 6. When asked to "compare", always include a **tabular** form — examiners reward structure. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Bullet Revision — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - **AdaGrad**: Accumulates squared gradients as a **cumulative sum**. Learning rate decays monotonically to zero. Best for **sparse data** (e.g., word embeddings). Suffers from **premature stopping**. - **RMSProp**: Replaces cumulative sum with **exponential moving average** of squared gradients. Effective horizon $\approx \frac{1}{1-\beta}$. Default $\beta = 0.9$. No bias correction needed. - **Adam**: Maintains **two moments** $s_t$ (first, mean) and $r_t$ (second, variance). Bias correction divides by $(1-\beta_1^{t})$ and $(1-\beta_2^{t})$. Final update uses **signal-to-noise ratio** $\frac{\hat{s}_t}{\sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$. Defaults: $\beta_1=0.9$, $\beta_2=0.999$, $\eta=0.001$, $\epsilon=10^{-8}$. - **Key Equation (Adam)** — memorize exactly: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \frac{\hat{s}_t}{\epsilon + \sqrt{\hat{r}_t}}$$ - **Why Adam beats RMSProp**: Adam adds **momentum**, which accelerates convergence through ravines and dampens oscillations. - **Why RMSProp beats AdaGrad**: RMSProp uses **EMA**, allowing non-vanishing learning rates for non-stationary objectives. - **Common defaults table** (must memorize for ESE): | Optimizer | Learning Rate | Decay | Other | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | AdaGrad | 0.01 | — | $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ | | RMSProp | 0.001 | $\beta = 0.9$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ | | Adam | 0.001 | $\beta_1=0.9,\ \beta_2=0.999$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$ | - **Engineering Applications**: Adam — Transformers (BERT/GPT), CNNs, GANs; RMSProp — RNNs; AdaGrad — Sparse NLP features. - **Exam Tip**: Always **derive the bias correction** explicitly (using the geometric series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \beta^k = \frac{1-\beta^n}{1-\beta}$) to earn full marks for that step.

topics.title: Regularization Techniques (L1 and L2 Regularization, Early Stopping, Dataset Augmentation, Parameter Tying and Sharing, Ensemble Methods, Dropout, Batch Normalization)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regularization Techniques (L1 and L2 Regularization, Early Stopping, Dataset Augmentation, Parameter Tying and Sharing, Ensemble Methods, Dropout, Batch Normalization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Problem: Overfitting in Deep Neural Networks

Deep neural networks, owing to their enormous parameter capacity (often millions to billions of weights), are highly susceptible to **overfitting** — a phenomenon where the model memorizes the training data (including its noise) but fails to generalize to unseen data. The hallmark symptoms are:
- **Training Error** $\rightarrow$ approaches **0%**
- **Validation Error** $\rightarrow$ starts **rising** after a certain epoch
- **Generalization Gap** = $\vert E_{\text{val}} - E_{\text{train}} \vert$ becomes large

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regularization** is the formal set of techniques used in deep learning to **reduce the generalization error (test error) without disproportionately increasing the training error**. It does this by trading a small increase in bias for a large reduction in variance.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Cramming Student)
Imagine a student preparing for an exam. One student memorizes the textbook word-for-word (overfitting). Another student understands the underlying principles and can answer *new* questions on the topic. **Regularization techniques are the "deep understanding tutors"** that prevent the neural network from being the cramming student and force it to learn *generalizable patterns*.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions of Each Technique

### A. L1 Regularization (Lasso)
L1 regularization adds the **sum of absolute values of the weights** as a penalty term to the loss function.

$$J_{\text{L1}}(\mathbf{W}) = J_{\text{original}}(\mathbf{W}) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert w_i \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect**: L1 induces **sparsity** — it drives many weights to exactly **zero**, effectively performing *automatic feature selection*. It produces compact, interpretable models.

**Analogy**: Like a strict budget — you must cut unnecessary expenses (weights) to exactly **zero**.

### B. L2 Regularization (Ridge / Weight Decay)
L2 regularization adds the **sum of squared values of the weights** as a penalty.

$$J_{\text{L2}}(\mathbf{W}) = J_{\text{original}}(\mathbf{W}) + \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Effect**: L2 forces weights to be small but **rarely exactly zero**. It distributes the "importance" across all features. It is also called **weight decay** because in gradient descent, the update rule effectively *decays* each weight by a factor.

**Analogy**: Like a balanced diet — you can have everything, but in small controlled amounts.

### C. Early Stopping
**Definition**: A regularization technique where training is halted at the point where validation error begins to increase, before the model overfits the training data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Early stopping is one of the **most widely used and computationally cheapest** regularization methods in deep learning (Goodfellow et al., *Deep Learning*, MIT Press, 2016).

**Analogy**: Like pulling your hand away from a hot stove the moment you feel the heat — stop *before* damage is done.

### D. Dataset Augmentation
**Definition**: The process of artificially expanding the size and diversity of the training set by applying label-preserving transformations to existing samples.

Common augmentations: rotation, flipping, cropping, scaling, color jittering, noise injection (for images); synonym replacement, back-translation (for text).

**Analogy**: A medical student learning to identify a disease under different lighting, angles, and noise — they become robust to irrelevant variations.

### E. Parameter Tying and Parameter Sharing
- **Parameter Tying**: Forces two separate models (or layers) to have *similar* (but not identical) weights, typically by adding a penalty $\vert W^{(1)} - W^{(2)} \vert^2$ to the loss. Used in siamese networks.
- **Parameter Sharing**: Forces the *exact same* parameters to be used in multiple parts of the network. **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** are the prime example — the same kernel slides across the entire input.

> [!NOTE]
> Parameter sharing drastically reduces the number of effective parameters and encodes the prior that *the same feature can appear in multiple locations* (translation equivariance).

### F. Ensemble Methods
**Definition**: Combining predictions from multiple independently trained models (or multiple snapshots) to produce a final, more robust prediction.

Two main deep learning variants:
1. **Model Averaging**: Train $K$ models independently and average their predictions.
2. **Snapshot Ensembles**: Save model parameters at multiple minima visited during a single cyclic learning rate schedule.

**Analogy**: Asking 10 doctors for a second opinion — the majority consensus is more reliable than one doctor's verdict.

### G. Dropout
**Definition (Srivastava et al., 2014)**: At each training iteration, **randomly "drop" (set to zero) a fraction $p$ of the neurons** in a layer, along with their connections. The remaining network is a *thinned* sub-network. At test time, all neurons are used, but their outputs are scaled by $(1-p)$ (or weights are scaled, called *inverted dropout*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dropout is the single most influential regularization innovation in modern deep learning. It approximates an **exponential ensemble** of $2^{\vert \text{neurons} \vert}$ thinned networks at near-zero additional training cost.

**Analogy**: A sports team practicing with random players missing each day — each player learns to play multiple positions, making the team robust to any absence.

### H. Batch Normalization (BN)
**Definition (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015)**: A technique that **normalizes the inputs of each layer** for every mini-batch to have approximately zero mean and unit variance, then applies a learned scale-and-shift.

> [!NOTE]
