> **This is the most-asked KTU concept:** The $1 \times 1$ convolution does **not** capture spatial information but **reduces channel depth** and adds extra non-linearity.

**Auxiliary Classifiers:**
- Two auxiliary softmax branches are attached to intermediate layers during training.
- Their loss is added (weighted by 0.3) to the main loss.
- **Purpose:** Combat vanishing gradients in this 22-layer network.

**Final Layer:**
- GoogLeNet replaces the heavy fully-connected head with **Global Average Pooling (GAP)**:
$$
\text{GAP}(x) = \frac{1}{H \times W} \sum_{i=1}^{H} \sum_{j=1}^{W} x_{i,j,c}
$$
This reduces parameters in the classifier head by a huge margin.

### 2.3 ResNet-50 — Deep Theoretical Analysis

**The Degradation Problem:**
Simply stacking more layers in a plain network causes **degradation**: training error *increases* despite over-parameterization. This is *not* overfitting — the network literally cannot optimize deeper models.

**The Residual Connection — Core Idea:**

Instead of learning the direct mapping $\mathcal{H}(x)$, the layers learn a *residual* function:
$$
\mathcal{F}(x) = \mathcal{H}(x) - x
$$
The output becomes:
$$
y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A *skip connection* (or *shortcut connection*) adds the input $x$ to the output of stacked convolutional layers. It allows gradients to flow unimpeded through the network via the chain rule.

**The Bottleneck Block (ResNet-50 uses this):**

ResNet-50 does *not* use Basic Blocks (2 convs each). It uses **Bottleneck Blocks** (3 convs each) to keep computational cost manageable:

| Conv Layer | Filter Size | Channels (In → Out) | Purpose |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Conv-BN-ReLU** | $1 \times 1$ | $256 \rightarrow 64$ | Dimension reduction |
| **Conv-BN-ReLU** | $3 \times 3$ | $64 \rightarrow 64$ | Spatial feature extraction |
| **Conv-BN** | $1 \times 1$ | $64 \rightarrow 256$ | Dimension restoration |

The shortcut performs a $1 \times 1$ convolution when input and output dimensions differ (called the *projection shortcut*).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala University Exam Tip:** These formulas appear directly in derivations and numerical problems.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| ReLU Activation | $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ | Non-saturating activation |
| ReLU Derivative | $f'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \le 0 \end{cases}$ | Used in backpropagation |
| Softmax | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | Output layer for multi-class |
| Cross-Entropy Loss | $L = -\sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | Classification loss |
| Conv Output Size | $O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $I$ = input, $K$ = kernel, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride |
| Param Count (Conv) | $K_h \cdot K_w \cdot C_{in} \cdot C_{out} + C_{out}$ | Filter size × input channels × output channels + biases |
| ResNet Residual Output | $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ | Skip connection output |
| GAP | $\text{GAP} = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{i,j} x_{i,j,c}$ | Global Average Pooling |
| Total Loss (GoogLeNet) | $L_{total} = L_{main} + 0.3 \cdot L_{aux1} + 0.3 \cdot L_{aux2}$ | With auxiliary classifiers |
| Dropout | $y = r \cdot x$ where $r \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)$ | Mask activations with probability $1-p$ |
| Inception 1×1 Reduction | $\text{FLOPs reduction ratio} = \frac{C_{in} \cdot 1^2}{C_{reduced} \cdot 1^2 + K^2 \cdot C_{reduced}}$ | Computational savings |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these architectures are deployed in industry:**
> - **AlexNet-class models** → lightweight edge devices, simple classification tasks, embedded systems.
> - **GoogLeNet-style Inception** → mobile applications (e.g., MobileNet, Xception are descendants), real-time inference with low latency.
> - **ResNet-50** → medical imaging (tumor detection), autonomous driving perception, fine-grained visual classification. The de-facto industry standard backbone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Convolutional Output Volume Dimensions

**Problem (KTU-style):** Compute the output volume of Conv1 of AlexNet given:
- Input image: $227 \times 227 \times 3$
- Filter size $K = 11$
- Stride $S = 4$
- Padding $P = 0$
- Number of filters = 96

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
O_H = \left\lfloor \frac{I_H - K_H + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Substitute the known values:

$$
O_H = \left\lfloor \frac{227 - 11 + 2(0)}{4} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

Compute the numerator first:

$$
227 - 11 + 0 = 216
$$

Divide by stride:

$$
\frac{216}{4} = 54
$$

Add 1:

$$
O_H = 54 + 1 = 55
$$

Since the input is square and filter is square, $O_W = O_H = 55$. The depth of the output is the number of filters, $C_{out} = 96$. Hence the final output volume is:

$$
\text{Output} = 55 \times 55 \times 96
$$

**Result:** The first convolutional layer of AlexNet produces a feature map of size **$55 \times 55 \times 96$**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Parameter Count of an Inception Block (Naive vs. Reduced)

**Problem:** Compare the parameter count of a naive Inception block versus the dimensionally-reduced version. Assume the input feature map has depth $C_{in} = 256$, and you want to apply:
- 64 filters of $1 \times 1$
- 128 filters of $3 \times 3$
- 32 filters of $5 \times 5$
- 32 filters of $3 \times 3$ max-pooling (followed by a $1 \times 1$ conv with 32 filters)

Assume spatial dimensions are $14 \times 14$ throughout.

#### Naive Inception Block:

**Parameters in $1 \times 1$ conv branch:**
$$
P_{1 \times 1} = (1 \cdot 1 \cdot 256 \cdot 64) + 64 = 16384 + 64 = 16448
$$

**Parameters in $3 \times 3$ conv branch (no reduction):**
$$
P_{3 \times 3} = (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 256 \cdot 128) + 128 = 294912 + 128 = 295040
$$

**Parameters in $5 \times 5$ conv branch (no reduction):**
$$
P_{5 \times 5} = (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 256 \cdot 32) + 32 = 204800 + 32 = 204832
$$

**Total Naive Parameters:**
$$
P_{\text{naive}} = 16448 + 295040 + 204832 = 516320
$$

#### Dimension-Reduced Inception Block (using $1 \times 1$ bottleneck):

Add a $1 \times 1$ conv with **16 filters** before the $3 \times 3$ and **16 filters** before the $5 \times 5$.

**New $3 \times 3$ branch:**
$$
P_{3 \times 3}' = (1 \cdot 1 \cdot 256 \cdot 16) + 16 + (3 \cdot 3 \cdot 16 \cdot 128) + 128
$$
$$
P_{3 \times 3}' = 4096 + 16 + 18432 + 128 = 22672
$$

**New $5 \times 5$ branch:**
$$
P_{5 \times 5}' = (1 \cdot 1 \cdot 256 \cdot 16) + 16 + (5 \cdot 5 \cdot 16 \cdot 32) + 32
$$
$$
P_{5 \times 5}' = 4096 + 16 + 12800 + 32 = 16944
$$

**Total Reduced Parameters:**
$$
P_{\text{reduced}} = 16448 + 22672 + 16944 = 56064
$$

**Reduction Ratio:**
$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{P_{\text{reduced}}}{P_{\text{naive}}} = \frac{56064}{516320} \approx 0.1086
$$

**Result:** The dimensionality-reduced Inception block uses only **~10.86 %** of the parameters of the naive block. This is the key reason GoogLeNet has only 4 million parameters despite being 22 layers deep.

### 3.3 Derivation: Gradient Flow Through a Residual Block

**Problem:** Show mathematically why a skip connection mitigates the vanishing gradient problem.

**Setup:** Consider a single residual block with output $y = \mathcal{F}(x, W) + x$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is the residual mapping (a stack of conv-BN-ReLU layers).

**Step 1: Forward Pass**

$$
y = \mathcal{F}(x, W) + x
$$

**Step 2: Compute the loss $L$ at the output.**

**Step 3: Backward Pass via Chain Rule**

The gradient of $L$ with respect to $x$ is:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
$$

Differentiate the residual output:
$$
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial x}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} + 1
$$

Substitute back:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} + 1 \right)
$$

**Step 4: Distribute the Terms**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

**Step 5: Analyze the Limiting Case**

If the residual function $\mathcal{F}$ learns to be small (e.g., via Batch Normalization and small initialization), then:
$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} \approx 0
$$

The gradient reduces to:
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} \approx \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

**Result:** The gradient is **preserved exactly** through the skip connection. Even in a network of 50 or 152 layers, the gradient signal neither vanishes nor explodes because the "1" acts as a highway for the gradient to flow backward. This is the **fundamental reason** ResNet can be trained at depths exceeding 100 layers, while plain networks fail beyond ~20 layers.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU Long-Answer Questions)

| Criterion | AlexNet (2012) | GoogLeNet (2014) | ResNet-50 (2015) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Architectural Unit** | Sequential Conv Pool FC | Parallel Inception Module | Residual Bottleneck Block |
| **Spatial Reduction** | Max-Pooling (overlapping) | Stride + Pooling | Strided Conv in shortcut |
| **Channel Strategy** | Doubling filters after pool | Inception parallel branches | Bottleneck (256→64→64→256) |
| **Key Trick** | ReLU + Dropout | 1×1 Conv for reduction | Identity / Projection skip |
| **Normalization** | Local Response Norm | Batch Norm (aux) | Batch Norm in every block |
| **Classifier Head** | 3 FC layers (4096, 4096, 1000) | GAP → Linear → Softmax | GAP → Linear → Softmax |
| **Trainable Params** | **~60 M** | **~4 M** | **~25 M** |
| **Computational Cost** | High (FC layers dominate) | Low (depth-wise factorized) | Moderate (heavy bottleneck) |
| **Main Limitation** | Too many params, FC bloat | Complex branching graph | Slightly over-parameterized |
| **Top-5 ImageNet Error** | 15.3 % | 6.67 % | 3.57 % |

### 3.5 Derivation: Total Loss in GoogLeNet with Auxiliary Classifiers

**Problem:** Compute the total loss given the main classifier loss $L_{main} = 2.10$, the first auxiliary loss $L_{aux1} = 2.40$, and the second auxiliary loss $L_{aux2} = 2.55$. The auxiliary classifier weight is **0.3** (as per the original GoogLeNet paper).

**Step 1:** Recall the total loss formula:

$$
L_{total} = L_{main} + 0.3 \cdot L_{aux1} + 0.3 \cdot L_{aux2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
L_{total} = 2.10 + 0.3 \cdot 2.40 + 0.3 \cdot 2.55
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the auxiliary contributions:

$$
0.3 \cdot 2.40 = 0.72
$$

$$
0.3 \cdot 2.55 = 0.765
$$

**Step 4:** Add all three terms:

$$
L_{total} = 2.10 + 0.72 + 0.765 = 3.585
$$

**Result:** The total loss used for backpropagation is **3.585**. The auxiliary classifiers add **1.485** (about **41.4 %** of the main loss) — a significant gradient signal, especially in the early training epochs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AlexNet — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputNode["INPUT 227x227x3 RGB IMAGE"]
    conv1["CONV1 11x11 stride 4 96 filters ReLU"]
    pool1["MAX POOL 3x3 stride 2"]
    conv2["CONV2 5x5 256 filters ReLU LRN"]
    pool2["MAX POOL 3x3 stride 2"]
    conv3["CONV3 3x3 384 filters ReLU"]
    conv4["CONV4 3x3 384 filters ReLU"]
    conv5["CONV5 3x3 256 filters ReLU"]
    pool5["MAX POOL 3x3 stride 2"]
    flat["FLATTEN"]
    fc6["FC6 4096 neurons DROPOUT 0.5"]
    fc7["FC7 4096 neurons DROPOUT 0.5"]
    fc8["FC8 SOFTMAX 1000 classes"]

    inputNode --> conv1 --> pool1 --> conv2 --> pool2 --> conv3 --> conv4 --> conv5 --> pool5 --> flat --> fc6 --> fc7 --> fc8
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow is purely **sequential** — one layer feeds the next. Notice the two **Dropout** nodes in the FC section. The convolutional base extracts features, and the fully-connected head acts as the classifier.

### 4.2 GoogLeNet — Inception Module Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputNode2["INPUT Feature Map HxWxC"]
    b1["BRANCH 1 1x1 Conv"]
    b2pre["1x1 Conv 16 filters DIMENSION REDUCTION"]
    b2["BRANCH 2 3x3 Conv 128 filters ReLU"]
    b3pre["1x1 Conv 16 filters DIMENSION REDUCTION"]
    b3["BRANCH 3 5x5 Conv 32 filters ReLU"]
    b4pool["3x3 Max Pool stride 1"]
    b4post["1x1 Conv 32 filters PROJECTION"]
    concat["DEPTH CONCATENATION OUTPUT HxWx256"]

    inputNode2 --> b1 --> concat
    inputNode2 --> b2pre --> b2 --> concat
    inputNode2 --> b3pre --> b3 --> concat
    inputNode2 --> b4pool --> b4post --> concat
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single Inception module has **four parallel branches** that process the same input. The two middle branches first pass through a $1 \times 1$ conv to reduce channels (this is the **key innovation**). All four outputs are concatenated depth-wise.

### 4.3 GoogLeNet — Full Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    stem["STEM 7x7 Conv stride 2 64ch MaxPool"]
    inc3a["INCEPTION 3A 256ch output"]
    inc3b["INCEPTION 3B 480ch output"]
    aux1["AUX CLASSIFIER 1 Softmax 1000 loss weight 0.3"]
    inc4a["INCEPTION 4A 512ch output"]
    inc4b["INCEPTION 4B 512ch output"]
    inc4c["INCEPTION 4C 512ch output"]
    inc4d["INCEPTION 4D 528ch output"]
    inc4e["INCEPTION 4E 832ch output"]
    aux2["AUX CLASSIFIER 2 Softmax 1000 loss weight 0.3"]
    inc5a["INCEPTION 5A 832ch output"]
    inc5b["INCEPTION 5B 1024ch output"]
    gap["GLOBAL AVERAGE POOLING"]
    drop["DROPOUT 0.4"]
    fcsoft["FC SOFTMAX 1000 classes"]

    stem --> inc3a --> inc3b --> aux1
    aux1 --> inc4a --> inc4b --> inc4c --> inc4d --> inc4e --> aux2
    aux2 --> inc5a --> inc5b --> gap --> drop --> fcsoft
```

**Reading the diagram:** The full GoogLeNet is a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** with two side branches (auxiliary classifiers) that contribute gradient signal during training but are discarded at inference time.

### 4.4 ResNet-50 — Bottleneck Block Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    skipInput["INPUT x Feature Map"]
    convA["1x1 Conv BN ReLU Channel REDUCE 256 to 64"]
    convB["3x3 Conv BN ReLU Spatial FEATURE EXTRACTION 64 to 64"]
    convC["1x1 Conv BN Channel RESTORE 64 to 256"]
    addOp["ADD skip connection x"]
    reluOut["RELU OUTPUT y"]

    skipInput --> convA --> convB --> convC --> addOp --> reluOut
    skipInput -.->|identity shortcut| addOp
```

**Reading the diagram:** A bottleneck block uses three convolutions in series. The skip connection (shown as a dashed line) bypasses the three conv layers and adds the input directly. The output is $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$.

### 4.5 ResNet-50 — Full Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    stemR["STEM 7x7 Conv stride 2 64ch MaxPool"]
    conv2x["STAGE 2 3 Bottleneck Blocks output 256ch"]
    conv3x["STAGE 3 4 Bottleneck Blocks output 512ch"]
    conv4x["STAGE 4 6 Bottleneck Blocks output 1024ch"]
    conv5x["STAGE 5 3 Bottleneck Blocks output 2048ch"]
    gapR["GLOBAL AVERAGE POOLING"]
    fcR["FC SOFTMAX 1000 classes"]

    stemR --> conv2x --> conv3x --> conv4x --> conv5x --> gapR --> fcR
```

**Reading the diagram:** ResNet-50 has **four stages** of bottleneck blocks (3 + 4 + 6 + 3 = **16 bottleneck blocks**). Each stage doubles the channel count while halving the spatial dimensions. There are **49 convolutional layers** plus **1 fully-connected layer**, totaling **50 learnable layers**.

### 4.6 Comparative Evolution Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2012["ALEXNET 2012 8 layers 60M params Problem: too many parameters FC bloat"]
    G2014["GOOGLENET 2014 22 layers 4M params Solution: Inception module 1x1 conv GAP"]
    R2015["RESNET 2015 50 layers 25M params Solution: skip connections residual learning"]
    R2015N["DEEPER RESNET 2016 101 152 layers New milestone: very deep training is stable"]

    A2012 -->|motivated| G2014 -->|motivated| R2015 -->|enabled| R2015N
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each architecture solved the *limitation* of its predecessor. AlexNet proved deep learning could win; GoogLeNet proved it could be *efficient*; ResNet proved it could be *deep* without degradation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answers)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, 3 Marks]**
> **What is the role of the $1 \times 1$ convolution in the GoogLeNet Inception module?**

**Model Answer:**
The $1 \times 1$ convolution in the GoogLeNet Inception module acts as a **dimensionality reduction** mechanism (also called a bottleneck layer). It reduces the depth (number of channels) of the input feature map before applying computationally expensive $3 \times 3$ and $5 \times 5$ convolutions. Additionally, it introduces an extra non-linearity (ReLU) at low computational cost. This dramatically reduces the number of parameters and floating-point operations (FLOPs), allowing GoogLeNet to have only **~4 million** parameters despite being 22 layers deep.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
> **Why did AlexNet use Dropout? What value of dropout probability was used?**

**Model Answer:**
AlexNet used **Dropout** to prevent overfitting in its large fully-connected layers (FC6 and FC7). Dropout randomly zeroes out a fraction $p$ of neuron activations during each training iteration, which prevents neurons from co-adapting too much and acts as an implicit ensemble of many sub-networks. AlexNet used a dropout probability of **$p = 0.5$** (i.e., 50 % of activations are zeroed during training).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Long Answers) — Module Internal Choice

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of **AlexNet** in detail. Discuss the role of ReLU activation, Dropout, and overlapping max-pooling in its success.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the output volume dimensions of all five convolutional layers of AlexNet, given an input image of $227 \times 227 \times 3$. Use stride and padding as per the original architecture. Tabulate the final output volume for each layer.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of AlexNet (7 Marks):**
- **Context:** AlexNet won ILSVRC 2012, reducing top-5 error from 26.2 % (second place) to 15.3 %. It marked the renaissance of deep learning.
- **Structure:** 5 convolutional layers + 3 fully connected layers.
- **ReLU Activation:** Solves the vanishing gradient problem (gradient of sigmoid saturates to 0 in deep networks). The function is $f(x) = \max(0, x)$, with gradient 1 for $x > 0$. This allows training to be ~6× faster than with $\tanh$.
- **Dropout (p = 0.5):** Applied to FC6 and FC7. Randomly drops 50 % of activations each iteration → reduces co-adaptation → acts as model averaging.
- **Overlapping Max-Pooling:** Uses $3 \times 3$ windows with stride 2 (i.e., overlapping). This empirically reduces top-1 error by ~0.3 %.
- **Data Augmentation:** Random crops + horizontal flips; PCA-based color jittering on RGB channels.
- **Local Response Normalization (LRN):** A form of lateral inhibition across channels.
- **GPU Implementation:** Trained on 2 GTX 580 GPUs (3 GB each) using cross-GPU parallelization.

**(b) Output Volume Computation (7 Marks):**

Using the formula:
$$
O = \left\lfloor \frac{I - K + 2P}{S} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

| Layer | Input $I$ | Filter $K$ | Stride $S$ | Padding $P$ | Output $O$ | Depth |
|-------|-----------|------------|------------|-------------|------------|-------|
| Conv1 | 227 | 11 | 4 | 0 | $\lfloor (227-11)/4 \rfloor + 1 = 55$ | 96 |
| Conv2 | 31 (post-pool) | 5 | 1 | 2 | $\lfloor (31+4-5)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 31$ | 256 |
| Conv3 | 15 (post-pool) | 3 | 1 | 1 | $\lfloor (15+2-3)/1 \rfloor + 1 = 15$ | 384 |
| Conv4 | 15 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 15 | 384 |
| Conv5 | 15 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 15 | 256 |

**Final Tabulation of Output Volumes:** $55 \times 55 \times 96$, $31 \times 31 \times 256$, $15 \times 15 \times 384$, $15 \times 15 \times 384$, $15 \times 15 \times 256$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students forget to include padding $P$ in the formula → wrong output dimension.
> 2. Students confuse $W \times H \times D$ order — depth always equals the number of filters.
> 3. Students forget to account for the max-pool layer in between, which shrinks the spatial size before the next conv.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Inception Module** of GoogLeNet. How does the use of $1 \times 1$ convolutions help in dimensionality reduction? Show with a numerical example.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] What is a **residual connection** in ResNet? Derive the gradient flow through a residual block and explain why it solves the vanishing gradient problem.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Inception Module of GoogLeNet (7 Marks):**
- **Motivation:** In a standard CNN, the designer must choose the kernel size (1×1, 3×3, 5×5) for each layer. The Inception module says: *"do them all in parallel and let the network learn which to use."*
- **Naive Inception Block:** Apply $1 \times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$ convolutions and $3 \times 3$ max-pooling in parallel on the same input. Concatenate outputs depth-wise.
- **Problem:** $5 \times 5$ conv on a 256-channel input is extremely expensive.
- **Solution:** Insert a $1 \times 1$ convolution **before** the $3 \times 3$ and $5 \times 5$ convolutions to reduce channel depth.
- **Numerical Example:** With $C_{in} = 256$, applying a $5 \times 5$ conv with 32 filters naively needs $5 \times 5 \times 256 \times 32 = 204{,}800$ parameters. By first reducing depth to 16 using a $1 \times 1$ conv, the cost becomes $(1 \times 1 \times 256 \times 16) + (5 \times 5 \times 16 \times 32) = 4096 + 12800 = 16{,}896$ parameters — a **~12× reduction**.
- **Auxiliary Classifiers:** Two side branches with weight 0.3 added to the main loss to combat vanishing gradients.
- **Final Layer:** Global Average Pooling replaces the heavy FC layers.

**(b) Residual Connection in ResNet (7 Marks):**
- **Definition:** A skip (shortcut) connection adds the input $x$ directly to the output of a stack of convolutional layers. The block therefore learns a *residual* function $\mathcal{F}(x) = \mathcal{H}(x) - x$ rather than the unreferenced mapping $\mathcal{H}(x)$.
- **Output equation:**
$$
y = \mathcal{F}(x, W) + x
$$
- **Gradient Derivation:**
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \left( \frac{\partial \mathcal{F}(x, W)}{\partial x} + 1 \right)
$$
- **Vanishing Gradient Mitigation:** When $\partial \mathcal{F}/\partial x \to 0$ (residual is small), the gradient still contains the "**+1**" term, i.e., $\partial L / \partial x \approx \partial L / \partial y$. The signal is preserved end-to-end.
- **Practical Outcome:** ResNet-50 (and even ResNet-152) can be trained from scratch without vanishing gradients, while plain networks of 30+ layers suffer the **degradation problem** (higher training error than their shallower counterparts).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students confuse the **residual function** $\mathcal{F}(x)$ with the **output** $y$. Always write $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$, not $y = x$.
> 2. Students forget to mention that the **bottleneck block** (1×1, 3×3, 1×1) is what makes ResNet-50 computationally feasible — without it, ResNet-50 would have ~110 M parameters.
> 3. For the gradient derivation, students must show the chain rule application explicitly — the "+1" term is the key.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**

- **AlexNet (2012):** 8 layers, **60M parameters**, first use of **ReLU** + **Dropout** + **GPUs** for CNN. Overlapping $3 \times 3$ max-pool with stride 2. Top-5 error: **15.3 %**.
- **GoogLeNet (2014):** 22 layers, **4M parameters**. **Inception module** = $1 \times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$, pool in parallel. **$1 \times 1$ conv = dimensionality reduction**. **Auxiliary classifiers** with weight **0.3** in the loss. **Global Average Pooling** at the end. Top-5 error: **6.67 %**.
- **ResNet-50 (2015):** 50 layers, **25M parameters**. **Residual / skip connection**: $y = \mathcal{F}(x) + x$. **Bottleneck block**: $1 \times 1 \rightarrow 3 \times 3 \rightarrow 1 \times 1$ (256→64→64→256). Gradient identity term "**+1**" prevents vanishing. Top-5 error: **3.57 %**.
- **Output volume formula:** $O = \lfloor (I - K + 2P) / S \rfloor + 1$ — applies to **every** conv layer.
- **Param count formula:** $(K_h \times K_w \times C_{in} \times C_{out}) + C_{out}$.
- **Transfer Learning flow:** Freeze convolutional base → replace final FC layer → fine-tune on target dataset.
- **Degradation problem:** Deeper plain networks have *higher* training error than shallower ones — solved by ResNet.
- **GAP formula:** $\text{GAP} = \frac{1}{H \cdot W} \sum_{i,j} x_{i,j,c}$ — replaces FC layers in modern architectures.
- **AlexNet's exact input:** $227 \times 227 \times 3$ (not 224 — a common KTU trick question).
- **Why Inception uses $5 \times 5$ at all:** Larger receptive fields capture higher-level features; the $1 \times 1$ bottleneck makes them affordable.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Pre-trained Architectures**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recurrent Neural Network Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recurrent Neural Network Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Architecture** is a class of artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns in sequences of data — such as text, speech, time-series, or video frames — by maintaining an internal **hidden state** (memory) that captures information from previous time steps. Unlike feedforward networks, RNNs share the same set of weight parameters across all time steps, enabling them to model temporal dependencies of arbitrary length.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the RNN architecture forms the foundation for advanced sequential models such as **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** and **Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)**. The architecture is characterized by three weight matrices:

- **$W_{xh}$** — Input-to-Hidden weight matrix
- **$W_{hh}$** — Hidden-to-Hidden (recurrent) weight matrix
- **$W_{hy}$** — Hidden-to-Output weight matrix

All three are **time-invariant** (shared across every time step $t$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a novel. As you read each word, you do not start with a blank mind — you remember the previous sentences, characters, and plot twists. Your understanding of the **current word** depends on everything you have read before.

An RNN operates exactly like this:
- The **hidden state** $h_t$ acts as your "reading memory."
- The **current input** $x_t$ is the new word you just read.
- The **output** $y_t$ is your reaction (e.g., understanding or prediction of the next word).
- The **shared weights** are like the consistent way you process language — you do not relearn grammar at every page.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The key innovation of RNNs is **weight sharing across time steps**, which dramatically reduces the number of parameters and allows the network to generalize across variable-length sequences.

## 1.3 Why Traditional Feedforward Networks Fail for Sequences

| Limitation of Feedforward NN | How RNN Solves It |
|---|---|
| Fixed-size input only | Accepts variable-length input sequences |
| No memory of past inputs | Maintains hidden state $h_{t-1}$ |
| Separate parameters for each position | Shared weights $W_{hh}$ across all time steps |
| Cannot model temporal order | Naturally models sequential/ordered data |
| Position-specific learning | Translation-invariant in time |

## 1.4 Types of Sequence-to-Sequence Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> RNN architectures are classified based on the correspondence between **input** and **output** sequence lengths.

1. **One-to-One** — Standard feedforward (e.g., image classification)
2. **One-to-Many** — Single input produces a sequence (e.g., image captioning)
3. **Many-to-One** — Sequence input produces single output (e.g., sentiment analysis)
4. **Many-to-Many** — Sequence in, sequence out (e.g., machine translation, video frame labeling)
5. **Encoder–Decoder (Many-to-Many with different lengths)** — Used in machine translation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unrolled Computational Graph

A recurrent network is naturally represented as an **unrolled graph** along the time axis. At each time step $t$, the network performs two computations:

### Step 1 — Hidden State Update

$$h_t = f_h\left(W_{xh} \, x_t + W_{hh} \, h_{t-1} + b_h\right)$$

where $f_h$ is typically $\tanh$ or $\text{ReLU}$, and $b_h$ is the hidden bias vector.

### Step 2 — Output Generation

$$y_t = f_y\left(W_{hy} \, h_t + b_y\right)$$

where $f_y$ is a task-specific activation (softmax for classification, linear for regression).

## 2.2 The Three Operational Modes

| Mode | Description | Activation Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Hidden State Update** | Computes new memory from current input and previous memory | $f_h = \tanh$ or ReLU |
| **Output Generation** | Maps hidden state to prediction | $f_y = \text{softmax}$, sigmoid, or linear |
| **Parameter Sharing** | Same $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ reused at all $t$ | Constraint: gradient flow |

## 2.3 Loss Function for Sequence Prediction

For a sequence of length $T$, the **total loss** is the sum of per-time-step losses:

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t\left(\hat{y}_t, y_t\right)$$

For classification tasks with softmax cross-entropy at each step:

$$L_t = -\sum_{i} y_{t,i} \log\left(\hat{y}_{t,i}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every KTU RNN question.

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden state update | $h_t = f_h(W_{xh}x_t + W_{hh}h_{t-1} + b_h)$ | Forward pass at time $t$ |
| Output computation | $\hat{y}_t = f_y(W_{hy}h_t + b_y)$ | Prediction at time $t$ |
| Total sequence loss | $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\hat{y}_t, y_t)$ | Sum over all time steps |
| Cross-entropy loss | $L_t = -\sum_i y_{t,i} \log(\hat{y}_{t,i})$ | Per-step classification loss |
| BPTT gradient (weights) | $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$ | Recurrent weight gradient |
| BPTT chain rule | $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_k} = \prod_{i=k+1}^{t} \frac{\partial h_i}{\partial h_{i-1}}$ | Gradient through time |
| Vanishing gradient | $\vert \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \vert \lt 1 \Rightarrow \text{gradient} \to 0$ | Long-term dependencies lost |
| Exploding gradient | $\vert \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}} \vert \gt 1 \Rightarrow \text{gradient} \to \infty$ | Causes NaN errors |
| Gradient clipping | $g \leftarrow \frac{\tau \cdot g}{\Vert g \Vert}$ if $\Vert g \Vert \gt \tau$ | Fixes exploding gradient |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Natural Language Processing:** Language modeling, machine translation, named entity recognition
- **Speech Recognition:** Acoustic modeling in systems like Google Assistant
- **Time-Series Forecasting:** Stock prices, weather prediction, energy demand
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein sequence classification, DNA motif detection
- **Video Analytics:** Action recognition, frame-by-frame captioning
- **Music Generation:** Symbolic composition with LSTM/RNN models

> [!NOTE]
> **Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Problem:** The repeated multiplication of the Jacobian $\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial h_{t-1}}$ along the time axis causes gradients to either shrink to zero or grow unboundedly. This is the central limitation of vanilla RNNs and the primary motivation for LSTMs and GRUs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Pass Through Time

Consider a sequence $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_T$ and an initial hidden state $h_0 = \vec{0}$. We use $\tanh$ as the hidden activation and softmax for the output.

### Step 1 — Initialization

$$h_0 = \vec{0}, \quad \hat{y}_0 = \text{undefined}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Hidden State at $t = 1$

$$h_1 = \tanh\left(W_{xh} x_1 + W_{hh} h_0 + b_h\right)$$

Since $h_0 = \vec{0}$, this simplifies to:

$$h_1 = \tanh\left(W_{xh} x_1 + b_h\right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute Output at $t = 1$

$$\hat{y}_1 = \text{softmax}\left(W_{hy} h_1 + b_y\right)$$

### Step 4 — Generalize to arbitrary $t$

$$h_t = \tanh\left(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h\right)$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}\left(W_{hy} h_t + b_y\right)$$

### Step 5 — Total Loss

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i} y_{t,i} \log\left(\hat{y}_{t,i}\right)$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)

BPTT is the application of the standard backpropagation algorithm to the unrolled computational graph. We need to compute $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{xh}}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}}, \frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}}$.

### Step 1 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Output Weights $W_{hy}$

For a single time step $t$:

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \hat{y}_t} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}_t}{\partial W_{hy}} = \left(\hat{y}_t - y_t\right) h_t^{T}$$

Summing over all time steps:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left(\hat{y}_t - y_t\right) h_t^{T}$$

### Step 2 — Gradient of Loss w.r.t. Hidden State

The hidden state $h_t$ influences the loss at the current step $L_t$ **and** all future losses $L_{t+1}, L_{t+2}, \ldots, L_T$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial h_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Recursive Term

Using the chain rule through the recurrence $h_{k} = \tanh(W_{xh} x_k + W_{hh} h_{k-1} + b_h)$:

$$\frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$$

### Step 4 — The Critical Jacobian

The term $\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is the local Jacobian. With $h_j = \tanh(z_j)$ where $z_j = W_{xh} x_j + W_{hh} h_{j-1} + b_h$:

$$\frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}$$

The diagonal matrix $\text{diag}(1 - \tanh^2(z_j))$ has entries in the range $(0, 1]$ since $\tanh^2(z) \in [0, 1)$. This means:

$$\left\Vert \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}} \right\Vert \leq \Vert W_{hh} \Vert$$

### Step 5 — The Long-Term Product (Vanishing/Exploding Source)

Over a span of $k - t$ time steps:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_t} = \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Implication:** Repeated multiplication of the same Jacobian matrix causes its eigenvalues to either decay exponentially to zero (**vanishing gradient**) or grow exponentially (**exploding gradient**). This is the **mathematical proof** of why vanilla RNNs cannot learn long-range dependencies — a question worth **14 marks** in KTU exams.

### Step 6 — Final Gradient w.r.t. $W_{hh}$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

This is the full BPTT expression. In practice, this is implemented using **Truncated BPTT** where the sum is limited to a fixed window (e.g., 50–100 time steps) to make training tractable.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Vanilla RNN | LSTM (Module 4 extension) | GRU (Module 4 extension) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gating mechanism | None | Input, Forget, Output gates | Update, Reset gates |
| Number of gates | 0 | 3 | 2 |
| Memory cell | Single $h_t$ | Separate $c_t$ and $h_t$ | Single $h_t$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Severe | Mitigated | Mitigated |
| Parameter count | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Long-term dependencies | Poor | Excellent | Good |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RNN Compact (Recurrent) Form

The left diagram shows the network as a single "looping" cell, while the right shows it unrolled across time.

```mermaid
graph LR
    xt1["x_t-1"] --> cellA[RNN CELL]
    htprev1["h_t-1"] --> cellA
    cellA --> htn["h_t"]
    htn --> yt1["y_t"]
    cellA --> cellB[RNN CELL]
    htn --> cellB
    xt2["x_t"] --> cellB
    cellB --> htn1["h_t+1"]
    htn1 --> yt2["y_t+1"]
    cellB --> cellC[RNN CELL]
    htn1 --> cellC
    xt3["x_t+1"] --> cellC
    cellC --> htn2["h_t+2"]
    htn2 --> yt3["y_t+2"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Unrolled RNN Computational Graph

This diagram shows the **shared weights** explicitly, which is a key KTU exam requirement.

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1["x_1"] --> mult1["W_xh"]
    mult1 --> sum1["SUM b_h"]
    in1 --> sum1
    h0["h_0 zero init"] --> sum1
    sum1 --> act1["tanh"]
    act1 --> h1["h_1"]
    h1 --> mult2["W_hy"]
    mult2 --> out1["y_1"]
    in2["x_2"] --> mult3["W_xh SHARED"]
    mult3 --> sum2["SUM b_h SHARED"]
    h1 --> sum2
    sum2 --> act2["tanh"]
    act2 --> h2["h_2"]
    h2 --> mult4["W_hy SHARED"]
    mult4 --> out2["y_2"]
    in3["x_3"] --> mult5["W_xh SHARED"]
    mult5 --> sum3["SUM b_h SHARED"]
    h2 --> sum3
    sum3 --> act3["tanh"]
    act3 --> h3["h_3"]
    h3 --> mult6["W_hy SHARED"]
    mult6 --> out3["y_3"]
```

## 4.3 BPTT Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below illustrates the **backpropagation through time** algorithm as a functional flow, mapping the gradient computation pipeline.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputLayer["INPUT: Sequence x_1 to x_T and target y_1 to y_T"] --> forward["FORWARD PASS Compute h_t and y_hat_t for all t"]
    forward --> loss["COMPUTE TOTAL LOSS L sum of L_t across all t"]
    loss --> gradOut["COMPUTE dL over dW_hy direct gradient"]
    gradOut --> gradHidden["COMPUTE dL over dh_t recursive across future steps"]
    gradHidden --> gradRec["COMPUTE dL over dW_hh via Jacobian product across time"]
    gradHidden --> gradIn["COMPUTE dL over dW_xh via chain rule"]
    gradHidden --> chainProd["COMPUTE PRODUCT of Jacobians partial h_j over partial h_j_minus_1"]
    chainProd --> checkNorm{"Check gradient norm"}
    checkNorm --> normHigh["NORM too large"]
    normHigh --> clip["GRADIENT CLIPPING scale to threshold tau"]
    checkNorm --> normLow["NORM too small vanishing"]
    normLow --> truncate["TRUNCATED BPTT limit window size"]
    clip --> update["UPDATE WEIGHTS using SGD or Adam"]
    truncate --> update
    gradRec --> update
    gradIn --> update
    update --> outputLayer["OUTPUT: Updated W_xh W_hh W_hy"]
```

## 4.4 Sequence-to-Sequence Architecture Variants

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph O2O["One to One"]
        A1["x"] --> B1["y"]
    end
    subgraph O2M["One to Many"]
        A2["x"] --> B2["y_1"]
        A2 --> C2["y_2"]
        A2 --> D2["y_3"]
    end
    subgraph M2O["Many to One"]
        A3["x_1"] --> B3["h"]
        A4["x_2"] --> B3
        A5["x_3"] --> B3
        B3 --> C3["y"]
    end
    subgraph M2M["Many to Many"]
        A6["x_1"] --> B6["h_1"]
        A7["x_2"] --> B6
        A8["x_3"] --> B6
        B6 --> C6["y_1"]
        B6 --> D6["y_2"]
        B6 --> E6["y_3"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. What is a Recurrent Neural Network? How does it differ from a feedforward neural network in handling sequential data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is a neural network architecture designed to process **sequential data** by maintaining a hidden state $h_t$ that captures information from previous time steps. Unlike a feedforward network, an RNN has **feedback connections** that allow the output of a neuron at time $t$ to be fed back as input at time $t+1$. The defining features are:

1. **Shared weights** across all time steps: $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ are reused.
2. **Hidden state** $h_t$ that acts as memory of past inputs.
3. Ability to handle **variable-length input sequences**.

This makes RNNs suitable for tasks like time-series prediction, language modeling, and speech recognition, where the order and context of inputs are critical.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Explain the vanishing and exploding gradient problem in RNNs with mathematical justification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The vanishing and exploding gradient problem occurs during **Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)**. The gradient of the loss w.r.t. a hidden state at time $t$ depends on products of Jacobians across many time steps:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_t} = \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}$$

If the **largest singular value** of this Jacobian product is:
- **Less than 1** → the product decays exponentially to **zero** → **vanishing gradient**, network cannot learn long-term dependencies.
- **Greater than 1** → the product grows exponentially → **exploding gradient**, causes NaN errors and instability.

Solutions include **gradient clipping** (for explosion) and **LSTM/GRU gating** (for vanishing).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

### ❓ Question A: (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw and explain the architecture of a Recurrent Neural Network. Discuss the role of weight sharing and the hidden state with suitable equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the forward pass equations of an RNN for a sequence of length $T$. Show how the total loss is computed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Architecture Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the unrolled RNN diagram.

**Key Components:**
1. **Input layer** receives $x_t$ at each time step.
2. **Hidden layer** maintains state $h_t$ computed from current input and previous state.
3. **Output layer** produces $\hat{y}_t$ from the current hidden state.

**Weight Sharing:** The same three weight matrices are used at every time step:
- $W_{xh}$: input-to-hidden
- $W_{hh}$: hidden-to-hidden (recurrent)
- $W_{hy}$: hidden-to-output

**Hidden State Update Equation:**

$$h_t = \tanh\left(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h\right)$$

**Why weight sharing matters:** It reduces the number of learnable parameters, enables the network to generalize to sequences longer than those seen during training, and ensures **translation invariance in time**.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Forward Pass Derivation:**

For $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$ with $h_0 = \vec{0}$:

$$z_t = W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h$$

$$h_t = \tanh(z_t)$$

$$o_t = W_{hy} h_t + b_y$$

$$\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t)$$

**Total Loss Computation:**

The loss at each time step using **cross-entropy**:

$$L_t = -\sum_{i} y_{t,i} \log\left(\hat{y}_{t,i}\right)$$

**Total sequence loss:**

$$L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i} y_{t,i} \log\left(\hat{y}_{t,i}\right)$$

**Per-step gradient for output layer:**

$$\frac{\partial L_t}{\partial o_t} = \hat{y}_t - y_t$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hy}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left(\hat{y}_t - y_t\right) h_t^T$$

---

### ❓ Question B: (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) algorithm in detail. Derive the gradient expressions for the recurrent weight matrix $W_{hh}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs? How does it affect learning long-term dependencies? Discuss two techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**BPTT Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Unroll** the network across all $T$ time steps.
2. **Forward pass:** Compute $h_t$ and $\hat{y}_t$ for $t = 1, \ldots, T$.
3. **Compute total loss** $L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t$.
4. **Backward pass:** Compute gradients in reverse, from $t = T$ back to $t = 1$.

**Gradient Derivation for $W_{hh}$:**

The hidden state at time $t$ depends on $W_{hh}$ both directly and through all subsequent time steps. Thus:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_t} \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

Using the chain rule and the recursive structure of the network:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{k=t}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial h_k} \cdot \left(\prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}}$$

where the local gradient is:

$$\frac{\partial h_t}{\partial W_{hh}} = \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_t)\right) h_{t-1}^T$$

This is the full BPTT expression. The inner product $\prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$ is the source of the vanishing/exploding gradient problem.

**In practice**, **Truncated BPTT** limits the sum to a window of $k$ time steps to make computation feasible.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Vanishing Gradient Definition:**

The vanishing gradient problem occurs when the gradient of the loss w.r.t. weights in early time steps becomes **exponentially small**, effectively preventing those weights from updating during training.

**Mathematical Cause:**

For a long time gap $k - t$:

$$\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial h_t} = \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \text{diag}\left(1 - \tanh^2(z_j)\right) W_{hh}$$

Since $\vert 1 - \tanh^2(z) \vert \leq 1$ and the spectral norm of $W_{hh}$ is typically less than 1, repeated multiplication drives the product to zero.

**Effect on Long-Term Dependencies:**

- The network **cannot propagate error signals** back through many time steps.
- It fails to learn relationships between events that are far apart in the sequence.
- For example, in a sentence "The clouds were dark and the sky was full of ___" with the answer "rain", the RNN may not link "clouds" to the final output.

**Two Mitigation Techniques:**

1. **Gated Architectures (LSTM/GRU):** Use multiplicative gates to control information flow. LSTMs introduce a **forget gate** that allows the cell state to retain information selectively, effectively creating a "gradient highway" that avoids the multiplicative decay.

2. **Gradient Clipping:** Caps the gradient norm to a threshold $\tau$:
$$g \leftarrow \begin{cases} g & \text{if } \Vert g \Vert \leq \tau \\ \dfrac{\tau \cdot g}{\Vert g \Vert} & \text{if } \Vert g \Vert > \tau \end{cases}$$
This primarily addresses exploding gradients but stabilizes training overall.

3. **Careful Initialization:** Initialize $W_{hh}$ as an identity matrix scaled by a small factor (e.g., 0.01) to keep eigenvalues near 1.

4. **Skip Connections / Residual RNNs:** Add direct connections between distant hidden states.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting weight sharing** — Many students draw different weight matrices at each time step. Always label them as $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ **shared across time**.
> 2. **Confusing BPTT with regular backpropagation** — BPTT is backprop on the unrolled graph; mention the **time axis** explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping the Jacobian derivation** — For vanishing gradient, you **must** show the product of Jacobians, not just state the problem.
> 4. **Forgetting the initial hidden state** — Always mention $h_0 = \vec{0}$ initialization.
> 5. **Mixing forward and backward pass notation** — Use consistent subscripts (e.g., $h_t$ for current, $h_{t-1}$ for previous).
> 6. **Missing the softmax step** — When asked for output, write both the linear layer $o_t$ and the activation $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(o_t)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for RNN Architecture:**

- ✅ RNN = Feedforward network + **recurrent hidden state** that loops back in time.
- ✅ Three weight matrices: $W_{xh}, W_{hh}, W_{hy}$ — all **shared across time steps**.
- ✅ Hidden state update: $h_t = \tanh(W_{xh} x_t + W_{hh} h_{t-1} + b_h)$.
- ✅ Output: $\hat{y}_t = \text{softmax}(W_{hy} h_t + b_y)$.
- ✅ Initial state: $h_0 = \vec{0}$.
- ✅ Total loss is **sum of per-time-step losses** over the entire sequence.
- ✅ **BPTT** = backpropagation applied to the time-unrolled graph; gradients flow backward from $T$ to $1$.
- ✅ Recurrent gradient: $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{hh}} = \sum_t \sum_k (\cdots) \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial h_j}{\partial h_{j-1}}$.
- ✅ The repeated product of Jacobians causes **vanishing** ($\to 0$) or **exploding** ($\to \infty$) gradients.
- ✅ Mitigations: **LSTM/GRU gates**, **gradient clipping**, **careful initialization**, **residual connections**.
- ✅ Sequence types: **One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-One, Many-to-Many** (and encoder-decoder).
- ✅ **Truncated BPTT** limits the time window to make training tractable.
- ✅ RNNs excel at: language modeling, time-series forecasting, speech recognition, machine translation.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot learn dependencies beyond ~10–20 time steps without gating.
- ✅ KTU frequently asks: derivation of forward pass, BPTT, and vanishing gradient explanation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Variants of RNN Architectures — KTU Module 4 Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are RNN Variants?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) Variant* is any architectural modification of the canonical Elman/Simple RNN designed to overcome specific structural limitations such as the **vanishing/exploding gradient problem**, the **inability to capture long-range temporal dependencies**, the **fixed input-output vector mismatch**, and the **lack of hierarchical/structural feature extraction**. Common variants covered in this module include **Deep RNN, Recursive Neural Network, Bidirectional RNN, Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq), LSTM, and GRU**.

In plain English, a vanilla RNN is like a person with **short-term memory loss** — they can remember the last few seconds of a conversation but forget the rest. The variants in this module are different *medical treatments* for that condition, each giving the network a different kind of "long-term memory aid" or "wider field of vision."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Memory Clinic"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Variants as Different Memory Specialists:**

| Variant | Memory Specialist Analogy | What It Solves |
|---|---|---|
| **Deep RNN** | A *tall stack of doctors*, each specializing in a different level of abstraction (low-level phonemes → high-level meaning) | Shallow representational capacity |
| **Recursive Neural Network** | A *family-tree therapist* who breaks a complex sentence into nested sub-phrases and analyzes them in a tree | Hierarchical / syntactic structure |
| **Bidirectional RNN** | A *time-traveler reader* who reads a sentence forward AND backward simultaneously | Future context dependence |
| **Encoder–Decoder** | A *bilingual translator* who first reads and summarizes a French sentence (encode) and then generates an English one (decode) | Input ≠ Output sequence length |
| **LSTM** | A *diary-writer with three locks* (forget, input, output gates) and a notebook | Long-term dependency + vanishing gradient |
| **GRU** | A *simpler notebook with two locks* (reset, update gates) — a lightweight LSTM | Same as LSTM but faster and fewer parameters |

---

### 1.3 The Six Variant Headlines

#### 1. Deep RNN (DRNN)
A **stacked RNN** where multiple recurrent hidden layers are layered vertically. Each time step $t$ passes information not just forward in time but also **upward** into deeper layers, enabling hierarchical temporal feature extraction.

#### 2. Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)
Unlike a recurrent network that follows a **chain (linear) topology** through time, a recursive network follows a **tree topology**. It applies the same set of weights recursively over a structured input (e.g., parse tree of a sentence).

#### 3. Bidirectional RNN (BiRNN)
Contains **two parallel RNN streams** — one processing the sequence from $t = 1 \rightarrow T$ (forward) and the other from $t = T \rightarrow 1$ (backward). Their hidden states are concatenated at each time step, giving every output access to **both past and future context**.

#### 4. Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture
A two-stage network: an **encoder** RNN reads the input sequence $\mathbf{x}_{1:T}$ and compresses it into a fixed-length **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$, and a **decoder** RNN generates the output sequence $\mathbf{y}_{1:T'}$ from $\mathbf{c}$. Solves problems where input and output lengths differ (e.g., machine translation).

#### 5. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
Introduced by **Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997)**. Replaces the simple hidden-state update with a **memory cell** controlled by three multiplicative gates (forget, input, output) and a protected **cell state** $\mathbf{C}_t$ that carries information across many time steps without vanishing.

#### 6. Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
Introduced by **Cho et al. (2014)**. A simplified LSTM with only **two gates** (reset and update) and a single hidden state vector (no separate cell state). Often matches LSTM performance with fewer parameters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep RNN — Stacking Recurrent Layers

**Operational Concept:**
In a standard "shallow" RNN, we have a single hidden layer that updates its state at each time step. In a **Deep RNN**, we stack $L$ such recurrent layers. The output of layer $\ell$ at time $t$ becomes the input to layer $\ell+1$ at the same time $t$.

**Why stack layers?**
- **Hierarchical temporal abstraction:** Lower layers learn short, local patterns (e.g., individual phonemes in speech); middle layers combine them into syllables/words; top layers encode semantic intent.
- **Increased representational capacity** without exploding the parameter count along the temporal axis.

**Equations (forward pass for layer $\ell$):**

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell)} = f_\ell \left( \mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(\ell)} \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{(1)} = f_1 \left( \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(1)} \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g\left( \mathbf{h}_t^{(L)} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Deep RNNs are the backbone of state-of-the-art **speech recognition (DeepSpeech), music generation, and acoustic modeling** systems where multi-scale temporal structure is essential.

---

### 2.2 Recursive Neural Network (RecNN)

**Operational Concept:**
A recursive network operates on a **tree-structured input** (usually a constituency parse tree). At each internal node, a composition function merges its two children's representations into a parent representation, using the *same shared weight matrix* at every node.

**The Composition Function (Tree-LSTM style generalized):**

$$
\mathbf{p} = f \left( \mathbf{c}_1, \mathbf{c}_2 \right) = \tanh \left( \mathbf{W} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{c}_1 \\ \mathbf{c}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \mathbf{b} \right)
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{c}_1, \mathbf{c}_2 \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ are the representations of the left and right children
- $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times 2d}$ is the shared weight matrix
- $\mathbf{p} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the parent node's representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Recurrent Network:**
> - *Recurrent* = chain topology over time.
> - *Recursive* = tree topology over structure.
> The recurrence is in the **structure of the data**, not the temporal index $t$.

**Engineering Utility:** Sentiment analysis of variable-length phrases, parse-tree-based reasoning, image parsing (e.g., scene decomposition).

---

### 2.3 Bidirectional RNN (BiRNN)

**Operational Concept:**
A BiRNN consists of two independent RNNs — a **forward RNN** ($\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$) and a **backward RNN** ($\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$). At each time step, the output hidden representation is the **concatenation** (or sum) of both:

$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = f \left( \mathbf{x}_t, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1} \right)
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = f \left( \mathbf{x}_t, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t+1} \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g \left( \mathbf{U} \left[ \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t \, ; \, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t \right] + \mathbf{c} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Limitation:** BiRNNs require the **entire input sequence to be available before any output can be computed** — they are NOT suitable for online / real-time streaming applications. This is a common KTU exam point.

**Engineering Utility:** Handwriting recognition, protein structure prediction, named entity recognition, speech recognition (post-processing complete utterance).

---

### 2.4 Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture

**Operational Concept:**
The encoder RNN reads the input sequence one symbol at a time and updates its hidden state. After the final input $\mathbf{x}_T$, the final hidden state $\mathbf{h}_T^{\text{enc}}$ is taken as a **thought vector / context vector** $\mathbf{c}$. The decoder RNN is initialized with $\mathbf{c}$ and generates the output sequence symbol-by-symbol.

**Encoder equations:**

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{enc}} = f_{\text{enc}} \left( \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\text{enc}} \right), \quad \mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T^{\text{enc}}
$$

**Decoder equations:**

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{dec}} \left( \mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\text{dec}} \right), \quad \mathbf{y}_t = g\left( \mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}} \right)
$$

with $\mathbf{h}_0^{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{c}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Bottleneck Problem:** A fixed-size $\mathbf{c}$ cannot capture all relevant details from long input sequences. This motivates the **attention mechanism** (covered in subsequent modules), where the decoder attends to *all* encoder hidden states rather than just the final one.

**Engineering Utility:** Machine translation (English→French), text summarization, image captioning, speech-to-text.

---

### 2.5 LSTM — Long Short-Term Memory

**Operational Concept:**
The LSTM introduces a **memory cell** $\mathbf{C}_t$ that is passed across time steps with only **linear interactions**, allowing gradients to flow over long distances. Three gates control the flow of information:

**The Three Gates + Cell Update:**

$$
\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma \left( \mathbf{W}_f \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_f \right) \quad \text{(Forget Gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma \left( \mathbf{W}_i \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_i \right) \quad \text{(Input Gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh \left( \mathbf{W}_C \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_C \right) \quad \text{(Candidate Cell)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t \quad \text{(Cell State Update)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma \left( \mathbf{W}_o \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_o \right) \quad \text{(Output Gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t) \quad \text{(Hidden State Output)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does LSTM beat vanishing gradient?**
> The cell state update is dominated by $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \ldots$ — there is **no squashing non-linearity** multiplying $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$, so gradients can propagate across many time steps when the forget gate is near 1.

---

### 2.6 GRU — Gated Recurrent Unit

**Operational Concept:**
GRU simplifies the LSTM by **merging the cell state and hidden state** and using only two gates.

**The Two Gates + State Update:**

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma \left( \mathbf{W}_z \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_z \right) \quad \text{(Update Gate)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma \left( \mathbf{W}_r \left[ \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_r \right) \quad \text{(Reset Gate)}
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh \left( \mathbf{W}_h \left[ \mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t \right] + \mathbf{b}_h \right) \quad \text{(Candidate Hidden)}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t \quad \text{(Hidden State Update)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Update Gate Intuition:**
> $\mathbf{z}_t$ acts as a convex combination coefficient — when $\mathbf{z}_t \approx 0$, the network **copies the previous hidden state** unchanged (long-term memory). When $\mathbf{z}_t \approx 1$, it **overwrites** with the new candidate (short-term update).

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formula Table for KTU 2024 Exam — Print and Memorize:**

| # | Variant | Key Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deep RNN | $\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell)} = f_\ell(\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(\ell)})$ | Stack layers vertically |
| 2 | Recursive NN | $\mathbf{p} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{c}_1; \mathbf{c}_2] + \mathbf{b})$ | Compose tree nodes |
| 3 | BiRNN | $\mathbf{y}_t = g(\mathbf{U}[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t] + \mathbf{c})$ | Bidirectional context |
| 4 | Encoder | $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T^{\text{enc}}$ | Compress input to context |
| 5 | Decoder | $\mathbf{y}_t = g(\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}})$ | Generate from context |
| 6 | LSTM Forget | $\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_f[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_f)$ | Decide what to discard |
| 7 | LSTM Input | $\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_i[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_i)$ | Decide what to write |
| 8 | LSTM Cell | $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ | Long-term memory |
| 9 | LSTM Output | $\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)$ | Read out memory |
| 10 | GRU Update | $\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_z[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_z)$ | Long vs. short term |
| 11 | GRU Reset | $\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma(\mathbf{W}_r[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}_r)$ | Ignore past |
| 12 | GRU Hidden | $\mathbf{h}_t = (1-\mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$ | Combine both |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, the notation $\vert x \vert$ is avoided using **square brackets and semicolons** to ensure KTU's auto-grading markdown parser does not break the table layout. Always write $\vert \cdot \vert$ as `\; ; \;` in tables.

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility Map

| Field | Preferred Variant | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Translation** | Encoder–Decoder (often with LSTM/GRU) | Variable input/output length |
| **Speech Recognition** | Deep BiLSTM | Both past & future acoustic context matter |
| **Stock Price Forecasting** | Stacked LSTM | Long-term temporal dependencies |
| **Image Captioning** | CNN Encoder + LSTM Decoder | Bridge vision & language |
| **Sentiment Analysis (sentence)** | Recursive NN / Tree-LSTM | Exploits syntactic structure |
| **Music Generation** | Stacked LSTM / GRU | Long-range melodic dependencies |
| **Real-time Streaming (rare)** | Causal LSTM / GRU | BiRNN cannot be used |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) in a Simple RNN and the Vanishing Gradient Motivation for LSTM

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** We will *derive* the gradient of the loss $L$ with respect to a weight matrix in a simple RNN and demonstrate *why* the gradient is geometrically suppressed across time — which is the exact mathematical reason LSTM's gated cell state was invented.

#### Step 1 — Define the Simple RNN Forward Pass

For a simple (Elman) RNN, the forward dynamics at time $t$ are:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \tanh \left( \mathbf{W}_{hh} \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh} \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{b}_h \right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = \mathbf{W}_{hy} \mathbf{h}_t + \mathbf{b}_y
$$

The total loss over a sequence of length $T$ is the sum of per-step losses:

$$
L = \sum_{t=1}^{T} L_t(\mathbf{y}_t, \hat{\mathbf{y}}_t)
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Gradient w.r.t. a Hidden State Using the Chain Rule

We want $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}$. By the chain rule and the recursive nature of the RNN:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \frac{\partial L_t}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} + \sum_{k=t+1}^{T} \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t}
$$

#### Step 3 — Expand the Future Dependency

By iterating the chain rule across future time steps $k > t$:

$$
\frac{\partial L_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the Single-Step Jacobian

Each $\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}}$ is a Jacobian matrix:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} = \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_j)\right) \mathbf{W}_{hh}
$$

where $\mathbf{a}_j = \mathbf{W}_{hh}\mathbf{h}_{j-1} + \mathbf{W}_{xh}\mathbf{x}_j + \mathbf{b}_h$.

#### Step 5 — Assemble the Full Gradient and Observe the Product

Combining Steps 3 and 4:

$$
\frac{\partial L_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_t} = \frac{\partial L_k}{\partial \mathbf{h}_k} \cdot \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \left[ \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_j)\right) \mathbf{W}_{hh} \right]
$$

#### Step 6 — Bound the Product (Vanishing/Exploding Gradient Theorem)

The norm of the product of $k - t$ Jacobians is bounded by:

$$
\left\| \prod_{j=t+1}^{k} \frac{\partial \mathbf{h}_j}{\partial \mathbf{h}_{j-1}} \right\| \;\leq\; \left( \lambda_{\max} \right)^{k-t}
$$

where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the spectral radius of $\mathbf{W}_{hh}$. Because each tanh derivative term is bounded in $[0, 1]$:

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \left\| \mathbf{W}_{hh} \right\|_2 \cdot \max_{j} \left\| \text{diag}\!\left(1 - \tanh^2(\mathbf{a}_j)\right) \right\|_2
$$

#### Step 7 — Conclusion: Two Failure Modes

- If $\lambda_{\max} < 1$ → gradient **vanishes** exponentially; long-term dependencies cannot be learned.
- If $\lambda_{\max} > 1$ → gradient **explodes**; training becomes numerically unstable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LSTM's Fix:** The LSTM cell update $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$ introduces a *pathwise* term $\mathbf{f}_t$ that replaces the multiplicative Jacobian. With $\mathbf{f}_t \approx \mathbf{1}$ (a learnable choice), the gradient flows through a near-constant coefficient rather than a product of diminishing tanh Jacobians — *solving the vanishing gradient problem in practice*.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of the Six Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Examiner's Most-Loved Comparison Table (memorize this for the 14-mark question):**

| Property | Deep RNN | Recursive NN | BiRNN | Encoder–Decoder | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Stacked chain | Tree | Two chains | Two chains (enc+dec) | Chain + memory cell | Chain + gated hidden |
| **Recurrence direction** | Forward time | Tree order | Forward + Backward | Forward + Auto-regressive | Forward time | Forward time |
| **Gates used** | None (plain tanh) | None | None | None | 3 (forget, input, output) | 2 (update, reset) |
| **Memory type** | Short-term hidden | Hierarchical structural | Bidirectional context | Compressed context vector | Long-term cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$ | Long-term hidden $\mathbf{h}_t$ |
| **Solves vanishing gradient?** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (via additive cell path)** | **Yes (via gated path)** |
| **Input/Output length** | Equal | Equal (tree leaves) | Equal | **Can be different** | Equal (or seq2seq) | Equal (or seq2seq) |
| **Parameter count (relative)** | Medium | Low | 2× of base RNN | Base × 2 | 4× of base RNN | 3× of base RNN |
| **Training speed (relative)** | Slow | Fast | Slow (2 streams) | Slow | Slowest | Faster than LSTM |
| **Can stream online?** | Yes | N/A | **No** | No (encoder finishes first) | Yes | Yes |
| **Typical use case** | Speech / acoustic | Sentiment on parse trees | NER, speech | Machine translation | Generic sequence | Generic sequence, faster alt |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: One LSTM Cell Time-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Trace** — assume the following toy values to validate the LSTM update:

Let the hidden dimension be 2. Suppose at time $t$:

$$
\mathbf{h}_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Assume the pre-activation (concatenation $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{W}[\mathbf{h}_{t-1};\mathbf{x}_t] + \mathbf{b}$) yields:

- Forget gate pre-activation → $\mathbf{a}_f = [0.8, 0.4]$
- Input gate pre-activation → $\mathbf{a}_i = [0.5, -0.2]$
- Candidate pre-activation → $\mathbf{a}_C = [0.3, 0.6]$
- Output gate pre-activation → $\mathbf{a}_o = [-0.1, 0.7]$
- Previous cell state → $\mathbf{C}_{t-1} = [0.4, 0.1]$

**Step 1 — Apply sigmoid to gate pre-activations:**

$$
\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma([0.8, 0.4]) = [0.690, 0.599]
$$

$$
\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma([0.5, -0.2]) = [0.622, 0.450]
$$

$$
\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma([-0.1, 0.7]) = [0.475, 0.668]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply tanh to candidate pre-activation:**

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh([0.3, 0.6]) = [0.291, 0.537]
$$

**Step 3 — Update the cell state (additive gated update):**

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = [0.690 \cdot 0.4, \; 0.599 \cdot 0.1] + [0.622 \cdot 0.291, \; 0.450 \cdot 0.537]
$$

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = [0.276, \; 0.060] + [0.181, \; 0.242] = [0.457, \; 0.302]
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new hidden state:**

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t) = [0.475, 0.668] \odot \tanh([0.457, 0.302])
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = [0.475, 0.668] \odot [0.428, 0.293] = [0.203, \; 0.196]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the *first dimension* of the hidden state changed substantially from $0.5 \rightarrow 0.203$ because the input gate allowed new information in, while the forget gate discarded a portion of the old state. This **selective forgetting and writing** is the central mechanism by which LSTM escapes the vanishing-gradient pathology of the simple RNN.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: One GRU Cell Time-Step

Let the hidden dimension be 2. Suppose at time $t$:

$$
\mathbf{h}_{t-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_t = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Assume pre-activations:

- Update gate → $\mathbf{a}_z = [0.0, 0.4]$
- Reset gate → $\mathbf{a}_r = [-0.5, 0.6]$

**Step 1 — Apply sigmoid to gates:**

$$
\mathbf{z}_t = \sigma([0.0, 0.4]) = [0.500, 0.599]
$$

$$
\mathbf{r}_t = \sigma([-0.5, 0.6]) = [0.378, 0.646]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reset-modulated previous hidden state:**

$$
\mathbf{r}_t \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} = [0.378 \cdot 0.4, \; 0.646 \cdot 0.1] = [0.151, \; 0.065]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the candidate hidden state** (assume $\mathbf{a}_h = [0.2, -0.3]$):

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t = \tanh([0.2, -0.3]) = [0.197, \; -0.291]
$$

**Step 4 — Combine via the update gate (convex combination):**

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \mathbf{z}_t) \odot \mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = [0.5 \cdot 0.4, \; 0.401 \cdot 0.1] + [0.5 \cdot 0.197, \; 0.599 \cdot (-0.291)]
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = [0.200, \; 0.040] + [0.099, \; -0.174] = [0.299, \; -0.134]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** With $\mathbf{z}_t \approx [0.5, 0.6]$, GRU *balances* the new candidate and the previous hidden state. If $\mathbf{z}_t$ had been close to 0, the hidden state would have been **copied** almost unchanged, demonstrating the long-term memory path.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Deep RNN — Stacked Recurrent Layers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["Input t-1"] --> B1["Layer 1 Hidden t-1"]
    A1["Input t"] --> B2["Layer 1 Hidden t"]
    A2["Input t+1"] --> B3["Layer 1 Hidden t+1"]
    A3["Input t+2"] --> B4["Layer 1 Hidden t+2"]
    B1 --> C1["Layer 2 Hidden t-1"]
    B2 --> C2["Layer 2 Hidden t"]
    B3 --> C3["Layer 2 Hidden t+1"]
    B4 --> C4["Layer 2 Hidden t+2"]
    C1 --> D1["Layer 3 Hidden t-1"]
    C2 --> D2["Layer 3 Hidden t"]
    C3 --> D3["Layer 3 Hidden t+1"]
    C4 --> D4["Layer 3 Hidden t+2"]
    D2 --> E1["Output y_t"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each row is a time step; each column is a stacked layer. The vertical arrows (omitted for clarity) also exist at every time step from one layer to the next above. KTU examiners often ask students to label the *direction* of information flow in such a diagram.

---

### 4.2 Recursive Neural Network — Tree Composition

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0["Root p_root"] --> N1["Left Phrase p_1"]
    N0 --> N2["Right Phrase p_2"]
    N1 --> L1["Word w_a"]
    N1 --> L2["Word w_b"]
    N2 --> L3["Word w_c"]
    N2 --> L4["Word w_d"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **recurrent** (chain over time) with **recursive** (tree over structure). The *same weight matrix* $\mathbf{W}$ is reused at every internal node — that is the recursive property.

---

### 4.3 Bidirectional RNN — Forward and Backward Streams

```mermaid
graph LR
    X1["x1"] --> F1["FWD h1"]
    X2["x2"] --> F2["FWD h2"]
    X3["x3"] --> F3["FWD h3"]
    X4["x4"] --> F4["FWD h4"]
    X1 --> B4["BWD h1"]
    X2 --> B3["BWD h2"]
    X3 --> B2["BWD h3"]
    X4 --> B1["BWD h4"]
    F1 --> Y1["y1 concat"]
    F2 --> Y2["y2 concat"]
    F3 --> Y3["y3 concat"]
    F4 --> Y4["y4 concat"]
    B4 --> Y1
    B3 --> Y2
    B2 --> Y3
    B1 --> Y4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Annotation:** The forward stream reads left→right; the backward stream reads right→left; outputs are concatenated. Hence **BiRNN cannot run online** — the entire input must be available.

---

### 4.4 Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1["x1"] --> EEnc1["Enc h1"]
    E2["x2"] --> EEnc2["Enc h2"]
    E3["x3"] --> EEnc3["Enc h3"]
    EEnc3 --> Cvec["Context Vector c"]
    Cvec --> DDec1["Dec h0"]
    DDec1 --> Dy1["y1"]
    Dy1 --> DDec2["Dec h1"]
    DDec2 --> Dy2["y2"]
    Dy2 --> DDec3["Dec h2"]
    DDec3 --> Dy3["y3"]
    Dy3 --> DDec4["Dec h3"]
    DDec4 --> Dy4["y4"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The context vector $\mathbf{c}$ is the *only* information channel from encoder to decoder. This single-vector bottleneck is the primary limitation of vanilla seq2seq and motivates the **attention mechanism** studied in later modules.

---

### 4.5 LSTM Cell — Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Xt["x_t"] --> Concat1["Concat h_{t-1} and x_t"]
    Htm1["h_{t-1}"] --> Concat1
    Concat1 --> Fgate["Forget Gate f_t = sigmoid"]
    Concat1 --> Igate["Input Gate i_t = sigmoid"]
    Concat1 --> Ogate["Output Gate o_t = sigmoid"]
    Concat1 --> Cand["Candidate tilde_C_t = tanh"]
    Ctm1["C_{t-1}"] --> MulF["Element-wise Multiply with f_t"]
    Fgate --> MulF
    MulF --> Add1["Element-wise Add"]
    Igate --> MulI["Element-wise Multiply"]
    Cand --> MulI
    MulI --> Add1
    Add1 --> Ct["C_t (New Cell State)"]
    Ct --> Tanh2["tanh of C_t"]
    Ogate --> MulO["Element-wise Multiply"]
    Tanh2 --> MulO
    MulO --> Ht["h_t (New Hidden State)"]
    Ct --> Htm1Next["h_{t-1} for next step"]
    Ht --> Htm1Next
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint:** The cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$ flows along the *top horizontal path* with minimal non-linear squashing. The hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is read out from the cell state through the output gate. This additive topology is the *key architectural innovation* of the LSTM.

---

### 4.6 GRU Cell — Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Xt2["x_t"] --> ConcatG["Concat h_{t-1} and x_t"]
    Htm12["h_{t-1}"] --> ConcatG
    ConcatG --> Zgate["Update Gate z_t = sigmoid"]
    ConcatG --> Rgate["Reset Gate r_t = sigmoid"]
    Rgate --> MulR["Multiply h_{t-1} element-wise"]
    Htm12 --> MulR
    MulR --> ConcatR["Concat with x_t"]
    Xt2 --> ConcatR
    ConcatR --> Cand2["Candidate tilde_h_t = tanh"]
    Zgate --> OneMinus["1 minus z_t"]
    OneMinus --> MulH1["Multiply h_{t-1}"]
    Htm12 --> MulH1
    Zgate --> MulH2["Multiply tilde_h_t"]
    Cand2 --> MulH2
    MulH1 --> AddG["Element-wise Add"]
    MulH2 --> AddG
    AddG --> Ht2["h_t (New Hidden State)"]
    Ht2 --> Htm12Next["h_{t-1} for next step"]
    Htm12 --> Htm12Next
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison note:** GRU has *one* state vector (no separate cell state), making the diagram simpler than LSTM's. The reset gate $\mathbf{r}_t$ decides how much of the past to *consider* when computing the new candidate; the update gate $\mathbf{z}_t$ decides how much of the past to *carry over*.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Bidirectional RNN architecture. State one limitation that prevents it from being used in real-time streaming applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Bidirectional RNN (BiRNN)** consists of two parallel recurrent networks operating on the same input sequence — a *forward RNN* that processes the sequence from $t = 1$ to $T$ and a *backward RNN* that processes it from $t = T$ to $1$. At each time step $t$, the output is computed from the concatenation of the two hidden states:

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g\!\left(\mathbf{U}\left[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t\right] + \mathbf{c}\right)
$$

**Limitation (1 mark):** BiRNN requires the *complete input sequence* to be available before any output $\mathbf{y}_t$ can be computed (since the backward stream needs to see $\mathbf{x}_{t+1} \ldots \mathbf{x}_T$). Hence it **cannot be used for online/streaming applications** such as live speech recognition or real-time control.

**Mark distribution:** Definition with formula — 2 marks. Limitation — 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between LSTM and GRU in terms of the number of gates and the type of memory state.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LSTM | GRU |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of gates** | 3 (forget $\mathbf{f}_t$, input $\mathbf{i}_t$, output $\mathbf{o}_t$) | 2 (update $\mathbf{z}_t$, reset $\mathbf{r}_t$) |
| **Memory state** | Two vectors: cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$ AND hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ | Single vector: hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ only |
| **Parameter count** | Higher (4 weight matrices) | Lower (3 weight matrices) |
| **Control mechanism** | Forget gate controls $\mathbf{C}_t$ directly | Update gate acts as convex combination coefficient |

**Mark distribution:** Gate count comparison — 1 mark. Memory state comparison — 1 mark. One additional distinguishing feature — 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
