> *(a)* Explain the **Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq) architecture** in detail. With suitable equations, describe how the input sequence is encoded into a context vector and how the decoder generates the output sequence. Mention one major limitation of this architecture. *(7 marks)*

> *(b)* Explain the **LSTM architecture** in detail. Derive the forward-pass equations for the forget gate, input gate, candidate cell, cell state update, output gate, and hidden state. Explain how the additive cell-state update solves the vanishing gradient problem. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Encoder–Decoder (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The **Encoder–Decoder (Sequence-to-Sequence)** architecture is a two-stage RNN designed to map an input sequence of variable length $T$ to an output sequence of variable length $T'$, where $T$ and $T'$ may differ. It is the foundation of modern machine translation, text summarization, and image captioning systems.

**Step 2 — Encoder equations (2 marks):**
The encoder is a standard RNN (often LSTM/GRU) that reads the input $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_T$ one symbol at a time, updating its hidden state:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{enc}} = f_{\text{enc}}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\text{enc}}\right)
$$

After consuming the entire input, the final hidden state is taken as the **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$:

$$
\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T^{\text{enc}}
$$

**Step 3 — Decoder equations (2 marks):**
The decoder is initialized with $\mathbf{h}_0^{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{c}$ and generates output symbols $\mathbf{y}_1, \mathbf{y}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_{T'}$ one at a time:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{dec}}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\text{dec}}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g\!\left(\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}}\right)
$$

The decoder uses its previous output $\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$ as the next input — an **auto-regressive** generation scheme.

**Step 4 — Limitation (2 marks):**
The **information bottleneck**: a *single fixed-length* context vector $\mathbf{c}$ must encode *all* relevant information from the entire input sequence. For long sequences (e.g., long sentences, long documents), this is insufficient. This motivates the **attention mechanism**, where the decoder can attend to *every* encoder hidden state rather than only the final one. Another practical limitation is **exposure bias** during training (teacher forcing vs. free running).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — LSTM Architecture (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
The simple RNN suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** due to repeated multiplication of Jacobian matrices during Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) solves this via a **gated memory cell**.

**Step 2 — Forget gate (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{f}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_f \left[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t\right] + \mathbf{b}_f\right)
$$

The forget gate $\mathbf{f}_t \in [0,1]^d$ decides what fraction of the previous cell state to retain.

**Step 3 — Input gate and candidate (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{i}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_i \left[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t\right] + \mathbf{b}_i\right)
$$

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t = \tanh\!\left(\mathbf{W}_C \left[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t\right] + \mathbf{b}_C\right)
$$

The input gate $\mathbf{i}_t$ scales the new candidate values $\tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$.

**Step 4 — Cell state update (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t
$$

This is the **additive gated update** — the only place in the LSTM where $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$ appears, and it is multiplied *only* by the forget gate (not by a tanh derivative).

**Step 5 — Output gate and hidden state (1 mark):**

$$
\mathbf{o}_t = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}_o \left[\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{x}_t\right] + \mathbf{b}_o\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t = \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t)
$$

**Step 6 — Why LSTM avoids vanishing gradients (2 marks):**
During BPTT, the gradient of the loss w.r.t. $\mathbf{C}_{t-1}$ flows through the cell-state path as:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{C}_t}{\partial \mathbf{C}_{t-1}} = \text{diag}(\mathbf{f}_t)
$$

This is a **diagonal matrix of forget-gate values**, *not* a product of tanh Jacobians. When the network learns $\mathbf{f}_t \approx \mathbf{1}$, the gradient flows across many time steps with magnitude ≈ 1, breaking the exponential decay that afflicts the simple RNN. Hence LSTM can capture long-range dependencies (e.g., subject–verb agreement across 50+ words).

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> *(a)* Describe the **Deep RNN architecture**. Explain how stacking multiple recurrent layers enables hierarchical temporal feature learning. State one engineering application. *(7 marks)*

> *(b)* Describe the **Bidirectional RNN** and **Encoder–Decoder architectures** with suitable equations. Compare and contrast their information-flow patterns. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Deep RNN (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
A **Deep RNN (DRNN)** is a recurrent neural network with multiple hidden recurrent layers stacked vertically. The output hidden state of layer $\ell$ at time $t$ is fed as the input to layer $\ell+1$ at the same time $t$. For a network of depth $L$:

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{(1)} = f_1\!\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(1)}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell)} = f_\ell\!\left(\mathbf{h}_t^{(\ell-1)}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{(\ell)}\right), \quad \ell = 2, 3, \ldots, L
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g\!\left(\mathbf{h}_t^{(L)}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Hierarchical feature learning (3 marks):**
Each layer learns a different *temporal abstraction* of the input:
- **Layer 1 (lowest):** local, short-range patterns. In speech, this corresponds to individual phonemes or short acoustic frames.
- **Layer 2 (middle):** mid-level temporal patterns. In speech, this corresponds to syllables or short phonetic units.
- **Layer 3 and above (highest):** global, long-range patterns. In speech, this corresponds to prosody, speaker identity, or sentence-level semantics.

The lower layers capture *fine-grained* information; the higher layers capture *coarse-grained, semantic* information. This decomposition is mathematically analogous to the hierarchical feature extraction seen in deep CNNs (edges → textures → objects), but applied along the *temporal* axis.

**Step 3 — Engineering application (2 marks):**
**Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR):** Modern ASR systems such as DeepSpeech 2 use stacked (deep) bidirectional LSTM/RNN layers to model long-range dependencies in audio. Each layer captures a different time-scale of phonemic and linguistic structure, achieving state-of-the-art word error rates on benchmarks like LibriSpeech. The combination of *depth* (hierarchical features) and *bidirectionality* (past + future context) is the standard recipe for high-accuracy ASR.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — BiRNN and Encoder–Decoder Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — BiRNN equations (2 marks):**

$$
\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = f\!\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t-1}\right)
$$

$$
\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t = f\!\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_{t+1}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_t = g\!\left(\mathbf{U}\left[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t; \overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t\right] + \mathbf{c}\right)
$$

**BiRNN information flow:** Two independent streams, one forward in time and one backward, with *no information exchange between them* at the same time step. Both streams see the *full* input.

**Step 2 — Encoder–Decoder equations (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_T^{\text{enc}}, \quad \mathbf{h}_0^{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{c}
$$

$$
\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}} = f_{\text{dec}}\!\left(\mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \mathbf{h}_{t-1}^{\text{dec}}\right), \quad \mathbf{y}_t = g\!\left(\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}}\right)
$$

**Encoder–Decoder information flow:** A *single* compressed vector $\mathbf{c}$ is the *only* bridge between encoder and decoder. The decoder is auto-regressive (each output depends on the previous output).

**Step 3 — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BiRNN | Encoder–Decoder |
|---|---|---|
| **Input/output length** | Equal ($T = T'$) | Can differ ($T \neq T'$) |
| **Information channel** | Two hidden states concatenated at every time step | One context vector $\mathbf{c}$ only |
| **Causality** | Non-causal (looks ahead) | Causal (decoder generates left-to-right) |
| **Online support** | No (needs full input) | Encoder cannot stream, decoder can |
| **Typical use** | NER, speech post-processing, handwriting | Machine translation, summarization, captioning |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse BiRNN with Encoder–Decoder.** BiRNN has *two streams over the SAME input*; Encoder–Decoder has *two different networks* (one consumes input, the other produces output). A surprising number of students swap these in the diagram and lose 3–4 marks immediately.
> 2. **Do NOT write "LSTM has 3 layers"** in answer to the "number of gates" question. LSTM has **3 gates**, not 3 layers. Confusing gates with layers is a top-3 reason for losing marks in module 4.
> 3. **Always mention the additive cell-state update** when explaining why LSTM solves vanishing gradient. Writing only "LSTM uses gates" is incomplete and will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **BiRNN is non-causal** — explicitly state this when asked. Do not write "BiRNN can be used in real-time systems" without qualification.
> 5. **Encoder–Decoder information bottleneck** is the most-tested limitation. Always state that the *single* context vector is a bottleneck for long sequences.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: RNN Variants**

**Deep RNN**
- ✓ Multiple recurrent layers stacked vertically.
- ✓ Each layer passes its hidden state to the layer above at the same time step.
- ✓ Enables hierarchical temporal feature learning (fine → coarse).
- ✓ Application: speech recognition, music generation.

**Recursive Neural Network**
- ✓ Topology is a **tree**, not a chain.
- ✓ Same weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ shared across all internal nodes.
- ✓ Composition: $\mathbf{p} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}[\mathbf{c}_1; \mathbf{c}_2] + \mathbf{b})$.
- ✓ Application: sentiment on parse trees, image scene parsing.
- ✗ Do NOT call it "recurrent" — it's "recursive."

**Bidirectional RNN**
- ✓ Two parallel streams: forward $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$ and backward $\overleftarrow{\mathbf{h}}_t$.
- ✓ Output = function of concatenated hidden states.
- ✓ Captures **both past AND future context** at each position.
- ✗ **Cannot run online / in real time** — needs full sequence.
- ✓ Application: NER, offline speech recognition, handwriting recognition.

**Encoder–Decoder (Seq2Seq)**
- ✓ Two-stage: encoder compresses to context $\mathbf{c}$, decoder generates output.
- ✓ Handles **variable-length input AND output**.
- ✓ **Auto-regressive** decoder: $\mathbf{y}_t$ depends on $\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$.
- ✗ **Information bottleneck** in $\mathbf{c}$ for long sequences.
- ✓ Application: machine translation, text summarization, image captioning.
- ✓ Foundation of attention-based models (next modules).

**LSTM**
- ✓ **3 gates**: forget $\mathbf{f}_t$, input $\mathbf{i}_t$, output $\mathbf{o}_t$.
- ✓ **2 state vectors**: cell state $\mathbf{C}_t$ (long-term) and hidden $\mathbf{h}_t$ (short-term).
- ✓ Cell update is **additive**: $\mathbf{C}_t = \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{C}}_t$.
- ✓ Solves vanishing gradient via forget-gate-protected additive cell path.
- ✓ 4 weight matrices per cell — heavier than GRU.

**GRU**
- ✓ **2 gates**: update $\mathbf{z}_t$, reset $\mathbf{r}_t$.
- ✓ **1 state vector**: hidden $\mathbf{h}_t$ (no separate cell state).
- ✓ Update gate $\mathbf{z}_t$ acts as **convex combination**: $\mathbf{h}_t = (1-\mathbf{z}_t)\odot\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \mathbf{z}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{h}}_t$.
- ✓ Reset gate decides how much past to consider in candidate.
- ✓ 3 weight matrices per cell — lighter than LSTM, often comparable accuracy.

**Cross-Cutting KTU High-Yield Distinctions**

| Question Stem | Right Answer |
|---|---|
| Which variant solves variable input/output length? | **Encoder–Decoder** |
| Which variant cannot be used in real-time? | **Bidirectional RNN** |
| Which variant uses a tree structure? | **Recursive NN** |
| Which variant has only 2 gates? | **GRU** |
| Which variant has additive cell-state update? | **LSTM** |
| Which variant captures hierarchical temporal abstraction? | **Deep RNN** |
| Foundation for the attention mechanism? | **Encoder–Decoder** |
| Invented by? | LSTM → Hochreiter & Schmidhuber (1997); GRU → Cho et al. (2014) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks for "explain the architecture" of LSTM/GRU, the examiner expects (1) the gate equations, (2) the state update equation, AND (3) a justification of *why* the design solves a problem (vanishing gradient / long-term memory). Diagrams earn at least 2 marks when labelled correctly. Always include equations in the answer — KTU board examiners award partial marks for correctly written formulas even when the prose is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoencoders and Generative Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Autoencoders and Generative Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Autoencoder — Formal Definition

An **Autoencoder (AE)** is an unsupervised neural network architecture that learns to reconstruct its input data at the output layer through a compressed intermediate representation. It consists of two coupled sub-networks: an **Encoder** $f_{\phi}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ that maps the input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to a lower-dimensional latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ (where typically $k \ll d$), and a **Decoder** $g_{\theta}: \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ that maps the latent code back to a reconstruction $\hat{x}$ in the original input space.

$$z = f_{\phi}(x) \quad ; \quad \hat{x} = g_{\theta}(z) = g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(x))$$

The parameters $\phi$ and $\theta$ are jointly optimised by minimising a **reconstruction loss** $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ such that the bottleneck layer forces the network to learn the most salient features of the data distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** An autoencoder is *not* a compression algorithm in the Shannon sense. It is **data-specific** (learns to compress only the training distribution) and **lossy**, because the latent bottleneck is regularised, not entropy-coded like ZIP/PNG.

### 1.2 Generative Model — Formal Definition

A **Generative Model** is a probabilistic model that learns the joint distribution $p_{\text{model}}(x, y)$ (or simply $p_{\text{model}}(x)$ for unsupervised tasks) over all variables in a dataset, enabling it to **sample new data instances** $x_{\text{new}} \sim p_{\text{model}}(x)$ that resemble the training distribution. This contrasts with **Discriminative Models**, which directly learn the conditional distribution $p(y \vert x)$ for classification.

In the context of deep learning, prominent generative architectures include **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)**, **Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)**, **Autoregressive Models (PixelCNN)**, and **Normalising Flows**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Art Student" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Reading the Mind of an Art Student**
> Imagine an art student who is shown **10,000 handwritten digits**. The student is then asked to reproduce them. First, the student **compresses** what they see into a mental sketch (the "essence" of curves, loops, and strokes) — this is the **Encoder** step. Then they **reproduce** the image from this mental sketch — the **Decoder** step. If the reproduction is bad, the student adjusts how they summarised the image. After many trials, the mental sketch contains the *core ingredients* of a digit (the **latent code**), and the student can draw new digits by mixing those ingredients — a **generative model**.

### 1.4 Why Bottlenecks Force Learning

The defining property of an autoencoder is the **information bottleneck**. If the network were allowed unlimited capacity (e.g., $k = d$ and the identity mapping), it would trivially copy the input. By forcing $z$ to be much smaller than $x$, we compel the network to learn a **low-dimensional manifold** that captures the data's intrinsic structure.

| Term | Mathematical Symbol | Standard Constraint |
|------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Input dimension | $d$ | $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Latent dimension | $k$ | **$k \ll d$** (undercomplete AE) |
| Reconstruction | $\hat{x}$ | $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ |
| Reconstruction Loss | $\mathcal{L}$ | Minimised during training |
| Reconstruction Error | $e$ | $e = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for the Board Exam:** An *undercomplete* autoencoder (small $k$) is regularised by architecture. A *sparse* autoencoder (large $k$, but only a few activations allowed) is regularised by a **sparsity penalty**. A *denoising* autoencoder is regularised by **input corruption**. All three are different ways to prevent the identity-mapping trivial solution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Halves Architecture — Operational Breakdown

**Step 1 — Encoder Function:**
The encoder is a stack of fully-connected or convolutional layers that progressively shrink spatial dimensions while increasing feature richness. For an input $x$, it produces a latent representation.

$$z = f_{\phi}(x) = \sigma(W_{e} x + b_{e})$$

**Step 2 — Bottleneck Layer:**
The bottleneck $z$ is the **information-locked** layer. Its dimension $k$ is a hyperparameter that controls the **compression ratio** $k/d$.

**Step 3 — Decoder Function:**
A mirror (or near-mirror) network expands $z$ back to the original dimensionality.

$$\hat{x} = g_{\theta}(z) = \sigma'(W_{d} z + b_{d})$$

**Step 4 — Loss Computation and Backprop:**
The loss is computed between $x$ and $\hat{x}$, and gradients are back-propagated through both halves. Crucially, the gradient flows **through the bottleneck**, so both encoder and decoder are updated simultaneously.

**Step 5 — Sampling (for Generative Variants):**
In a *Variational Autoencoder*, instead of producing a deterministic $z$, the encoder outputs the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ of a probability distribution from which $z$ is sampled. This makes the latent space **continuous and probabilistic**, allowing generation by simply sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | When to Use |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Encoder mapping | $z = f_{\phi}(x)$ | Forward pass of any AE |
| 2 | Decoder mapping | $\hat{x} = g_{\theta}(z)$ | Forward pass of any AE |
| 3 | MSE Reconstruction Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - \hat{x}_i \Vert^{2}$ | Continuous data (images) |
| 4 | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\sum_{i}\left[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i)\log(1-\hat{x}_i)\right]$ | Binary / pixel data in $[0,1]$ |
| 5 | L2 Regularisation Term | $\Omega = \lambda \left(\Vert W_{e} \Vert_{F}^{2} + \Vert W_{d} \Vert_{F}^{2}\right)$ | Weight-decay regularised AE |
| 6 | Sparsity Penalty (KL) | $\Omega_{s} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} KL\left(\hat{\rho}_j \Vert \rho\right)$ | Sparse autoencoder |
| 7 | Denoising Objective | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(\tilde{x})) \Vert^{2}$ | $\tilde{x}$ = corrupted input |
| 8 | Reparameterisation Trick | $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon,\ \ \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | VAE training (gradient flow) |
| 9 | VAE Loss (ELBO) | $\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\right] + KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\right)$ | Variational AE |
| 10 | KL for Two Gaussians | $KL\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1,\sigma_1^{2}) \Vert \mathcal{N}(\mu_2,\sigma_2^{2})\right) = \log\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_1^{2}+(\mu_1-\mu_2)^{2}}{2\sigma_2^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Closed-form VAE regulariser |
| 11 | GAN Value Function | $\min_{G}\max_{D} V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_{\text{data}}}\!\left[\log D(x)\right] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}\!\left[\log(1-D(G(z)))\right]$ | Adversarial training |
| 12 | Generator Loss (non-saturating) | $\mathcal{L}_{G} = -\mathbb{E}_{z}\!\left[\log D(G(z))\right]$ | Practical GAN training |
| 13 | Discriminator Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{D} = -\mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\log D(x)\right] - \mathbb{E}_{z}\!\left[\log(1-D(G(z)))\right]$ | Practical GAN training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $\Vert \cdot \Vert$:** In KTU board scripts, the norm symbol $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ is rendered as the double-pipe notation. Inside the formula table above, all absolute / norm bars are rendered as $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ to preserve LaTeX readability.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Systems

| Application Domain | Autoencoder Role | Generative Model Role |
|--------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Medical Imaging | Denoising MRI / CT scans | Synthesising training data for rare diseases |
| Finance | Anomaly / fraud detection in transactions | Stress-testing with synthetic market scenarios |
| Natural Language Processing | Sentence embeddings, semantic hashing | Dialogue generation, paraphrasing |
| Autonomous Driving | Compressing LiDAR point clouds | Simulating rare road conditions for training |
| Cybersecurity | Reconstructing normal traffic profiles | Generating adversarial samples for robustness |
| Manufacturing | Defect detection via reconstruction error | Augmenting scarce defect-image datasets |

### 2.4 Taxonomy of Autoencoders (Syllabus-Mandated)

1. **Vanilla / Undercomplete AE** — Bottleneck is the only regulariser.
2. **Sparse AE** — Adds an L1 or KL-sparsity penalty on the latent activations.
3. **Denoising AE (DAE)** — Trained to reconstruct a *clean* $x$ from a *corrupted* $\tilde{x}$.
4. **Contractive AE (CAE)** — Adds a penalty on the Jacobian $\Vert J_{f}(x) \Vert_{F}^{2}$ to make the encoder robust to small input perturbations.
5. **Variational AE (VAE)** — Replaces the deterministic $z$ with a *distribution*; trained via the **ELBO** objective.
6. **Convolutional AE** — Uses Conv/Pool layers instead of Dense layers for image data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many types?** Each variant answers a different question: *vanilla* = "compress"; *sparse* = "find sparse features"; *denoising* = "robust features"; *contractive* = "smooth manifold"; *variational* = "sample new data." Always mention *which assumption* the AE is making about the data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Mean-Squared Reconstruction Loss for Vanilla AE

**Goal:** Show how the MSE loss is derived from a maximum-likelihood perspective under a Gaussian noise assumption.

**Step 1 — Assumption:** Suppose the decoder produces a reconstruction corrupted by isotropic Gaussian noise:

$$p(x \vert z) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(x ;\, g_{\theta}(z),\, \sigma^{2} I\right)$$

**Step 2 — Log-Likelihood of a Single Sample:**

$$\log p(x \vert z) = -\dfrac{1}{2\sigma^{2}} \Vert x - g_{\theta}(z) \Vert^{2} - \dfrac{d}{2} \log(2\pi\sigma^{2})$$

**Step 3 — Maximising Log-Likelihood is Equivalent to Minimising the Negative:** The constant term and the variance $\sigma$ are independent of the network parameters, so maximising the likelihood reduces to minimising the squared-error term:

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - g_{\theta}(f_{\phi}(x_i)) \Vert^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Gradient Descent Update (Conceptual):** Gradients are computed via the chain rule through the bottleneck:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_{\theta} \mathcal{L}_{MSE}$$
$$\phi \leftarrow \phi - \eta \nabla_{\phi} \mathcal{L}_{MSE}$$

This is a single closed-form derivation showing that the *common* MSE loss is not arbitrary — it is the **MLE solution under Gaussian reconstruction noise**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for VAE

This is the most board-relevant derivation in KTU Module 4.

**Step 1 — The Marginal Likelihood Intractable:** We want to maximise $\log p_{\theta}(x)$, but:

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) = \log \int p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\, p(z)\, dz$$

This integral is **intractable** because the latent space is continuous and high-dimensional.

**Step 2 — Introduce the Variational Distribution $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$:** This is the encoder. We introduce it as a proxy for the true (intractable) posterior $p_{\theta}(z \vert x)$.

**Step 3 — Write the KL Divergence of $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ and $p_{\theta}(z \vert x)$:**

$$KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p_{\theta}(z \vert x)\right) = \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\log q_{\phi}(z \vert x) - \log p_{\theta}(z \vert x)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Conditional via Bayes' Rule:**

$$\log p_{\theta}(z \vert x) = \log p_{\theta}(x \vert z) + \log p(z) - \log p_{\theta}(x)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$KL = \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\log q_{\phi}(z \vert x) - \log p_{\theta}(x \vert z) - \log p(z)\right] + \log p_{\theta}(x)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to Isolate $\log p_{\theta}(x)$:**

$$\log p_{\theta}(x) - KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p_{\theta}(z \vert x)\right) = \mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\right] - KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\right)$$

**Step 7 — Identify the ELBO:** The right-hand side is the **Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)**, denoted $\mathcal{L}_{\theta,\phi}(x)$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(z \vert x)}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\right]}_{\text{Reconstruction Term}} - \underbrace{KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\right)}_{\text{Regularisation Term}}$$

**Step 8 — Final VAE Loss to Maximise (Minimise Negative):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q}\!\left[\log p_{\theta}(x \vert z)\right] + KL\!\left(q_{\phi}(z \vert x) \Vert p(z)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this "lower bound"?** Because $KL \geq 0$, we have $\log p_{\theta}(x) \geq \mathcal{L}_{ELBO}$. Maximising the ELBO pushes the *bound* upward, which forces the *true* $\log p_{\theta}(x)$ upward as well (and forces the encoder $q_\phi$ to approximate the true posterior).

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Reparameterisation Trick

**The Problem:** Sampling $z \sim q_{\phi}(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ is a **stochastic operation**, which means gradients cannot flow back through the sampling node during backpropagation.

**The Solution (Trick):** Express $z$ as a deterministic function of the parameters and an auxiliary noise variable $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$:

**Step 1 — Standard Normal Sample:**

$$\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

**Step 2 — Affine Transformation:**

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Verify Distribution:** $z$ is a linear transformation of a Gaussian, hence itself Gaussian with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^{2}$. The randomness is *isolated* in the $\varepsilon$ input, which has **no learnable parameters** and therefore **no gradient needed**.

**Step 4 — Gradient Flow:** Now $\frac{\partial z}{\partial \mu} = 1$ and $\frac{\partial z}{\partial \sigma} = \varepsilon$, so gradients of the loss w.r.t. $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are well-defined. This is the key engineering innovation that makes VAE training possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks *"Why is the reparameterisation trick necessary?"* the answer is **"to allow gradients to flow through the stochastic sampling node by isolating the randomness in an external noise variable with no learnable parameters."**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AE vs. VAE vs. GAN

| Property | Autoencoder (Vanilla) | Variational Autoencoder (VAE) | Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) |
|----------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Latent Space | Deterministic point $z$ | Probabilistic distribution $q(z\vert x)$ | Implicit via noise $p(z)$ |
| Generative? | **No** (needs input) | **Yes** (sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$) | **Yes** (sample $z \sim p(z)$) |
| Loss Function | MSE / BCE | Negative ELBO | Adversarial min-max |
| Training Stability | Very stable | Stable | Often unstable (mode collapse) |
| Sample Sharpness | Blurry | Blurry (mean-seeking) | **Sharp / photorealistic** |
| Inference | Encoder only | Encoder + sampling | **Generator only** (no encoder) |
| Density Estimation | Approximate | Explicit (ELBO) | **None** (implicit) |
| Architectures Used | MLP / Conv | Conv / RNN | Conv (DCGAN), ResNet (StyleGAN) |
| Best For | Denoising, compression | Smooth latent interpolation | High-fidelity image synthesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the board:** **AE learns to compress, VAE learns to compress *and* sample, GAN learns to sample *only* (no encoder, no reconstruction).**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vanilla Autoencoder — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1[Input Vector x dim d] --> enc1[Encoder Dense Layer 1]
    enc1 --> enc2[Encoder Dense Layer 2]
    enc2 --> bn1[Bottleneck Latent z dim k]
    bn1 --> dec1[Decoder Dense Layer 1]
    dec1 --> dec2[Decoder Dense Layer 2]
    dec2 --> out1[Reconstruction x hat dim d]
    out1 -.Loss MSE.- in1
    out1 -.Backprop Gradients.- enc1
    out1 -.Backprop Gradients.- dec1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The input $x$ (dimension $d$) is progressively compressed by the encoder until it reaches the **bottleneck** of dimension $k \ll d$. The decoder then mirrors this expansion to produce $\hat{x}$. The reconstruction loss is computed against the original $x$, and gradients flow through **both** the decoder and the encoder.

---

### 4.2 Variational Autoencoder — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    vIn[Input x] --> vEnc[Encoder Network f phi]
    vEnc --> vMu[Mean mu]
    vEnc --> vSig[Variance sigma squared]
    vMu --> vRep[Reparameterisation mu plus sigma times epsilon]
    vSig --> vRep
    noise1[epsilon sim Normal 0 I] --> vRep
    vRep --> vSamp[Sampled Latent z]
    vSamp --> vDec[Decoder Network g theta]
    vDec --> vOut[Reconstruction x hat]
    vOut -.Reconstruction Loss.- vIn
    vOut -.KL Divergence Regulariser.- vMu
```

**Reading the diagram:** Unlike the vanilla AE, the encoder of a VAE produces **two vectors** — the mean $\mu$ and the variance $\sigma^{2}$ of a Gaussian distribution. The **reparameterisation trick** samples $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$, which is then decoded. The loss has two parts: a reconstruction term (left feedback loop) and a KL-divergence regulariser (right feedback loop) that forces $q_{\phi}(z \vert x)$ to be close to the prior $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

---

### 4.3 Generative Adversarial Network — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nz[Noise Vector z] --> gen1[Generator G theta]
    gen1 --> fake1[Fake Image G of z]
    real1[Real Image x] --> pool1[Combine Real and Fake Batch]
    fake1 --> pool1
    pool1 --> disc1[Discriminator D phi]
    disc1 --> outR[Output Real or Fake Probability]
    outR -.Adversarial Loss.- gen1
    outR -.Adversarial Loss.- disc1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Generator** $G_\theta$ takes a random noise vector $z \sim p(z)$ and produces a fake image. The **Discriminator** $D_\phi$ receives a mixed batch of real and fake images and must classify each as real or fake. $G$ is trained to *fool* $D$, while $D$ is trained to *not be fooled*. This adversarial game drives both networks to improve until $G$ produces photorealistic samples.

---

### 4.4 Generative Model Landscape — Top-Level Overview

```mermaid
graph TB
    root1[Generative Deep Learning Models]
    root1 --> branch1[Explicit Density]
    root1 --> branch2[Implicit Density]
    branch1 --> leaf1[VAE Variational Autoencoder]
    branch1 --> leaf2[PixelCNN Autoregressive]
    branch1 --> leaf3[Normalising Flows]
    branch2 --> leaf4[GAN Generative Adversarial Network]
    branch2 --> leaf5[Energy Based Models]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Generative models are split into those that **explicitly** model the density (VAE, PixelCNN, Normalising Flows — all can compute $p(x)$) and those that **implicitly** model it (GAN, EBM — can sample but cannot compute $p(x)$ directly). This is a frequently asked KTU viva question: *"Does a GAN provide a likelihood?"* — **Answer: No, only samples.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define an autoencoder. With a neat block diagram, explain its two main components.**

**Model Answer:**

An **autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network trained to reconstruct its input $x$ at the output layer $\hat{x}$ by passing the data through a compressed latent representation $z$. It is composed of two parts:

1. **Encoder** $f_{\phi}(x)$ — maps the high-dimensional input $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to a low-dimensional latent code $z \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, where $k \ll d$.
2. **Decoder** $g_{\theta}(z)$ — reconstructs $\hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ from the latent code.

$$z = f_{\phi}(x), \quad \hat{x} = g_{\theta}(z)$$

The model is trained by minimising the reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x}) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^{2}$, forcing the bottleneck to retain only the most salient features. *Block diagram: Input → Encoder → Bottleneck $z$ → Decoder → Output $\hat{x}$, with feedback loss to both encoder and decoder.* **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the reparameterisation trick in a Variational Autoencoder? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The **reparameterisation trick** expresses the latent sample $z$ from a distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ as a deterministic function of the parameters and an external noise variable $\varepsilon$:

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

It is **necessary** because the sampling operation $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ is stochastic and blocks gradient flow during backpropagation. By isolating the randomness in the parameter-free variable $\varepsilon$, the trick makes $z$ differentiable with respect to $\mu$ and $\sigma$, enabling end-to-end training of the VAE via stochastic gradient descent. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of a vanilla autoencoder with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the bottleneck layer and the training procedure.**

### Model Solution:

**Architecture:** A vanilla autoencoder has a **symmetric** hourglass structure. Let the input be $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. The encoder applies successive linear (or convolutional) transformations with non-linear activations to compress the representation:

$$h^{(1)} = \sigma(W_{1} x + b_{1})$$
$$h^{(2)} = \sigma(W_{2} h^{(1)} + b_{2})$$
$$z = \sigma(W_{3} h^{(2)} + b_{3})$$

The decoder mirrors this:

$$\hat{h}^{(1)} = \sigma'(W'_{1} z + b'_{1})$$
$$\hat{h}^{(2)} = \sigma'(W'_{2} \hat{h}^{(1)} + b'_{2})$$
$$\hat{x} = \sigma''(W'_{3} \hat{h}^{(2)} + b'_{3})$$

**Neat Diagram (described):**
`Input x (d-dim) → Encoder (Dense/Conv) → Bottleneck z (k-dim) → Decoder (Dense/Conv) → Output x̂ (d-dim) → Loss L = ||x − x̂||² → backprop to all layers`

**Role of the Bottleneck:**
The bottleneck $z$ is the **information-locking layer**. With $k \ll d$, the network cannot simply copy $x$ to $\hat{x}$. It must learn a compact, distributed code that retains the **most informative features** for reconstruction. This forces the AE to discover the **intrinsic low-dimensional manifold** on which the data lies (manifold learning hypothesis).

**Training Procedure:**
1. Initialise weights $W, b$ using Xavier / He initialisation.
2. For each mini-batch of inputs $\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{m}$:
    a. Forward pass: compute $z^{(i)} = f_\phi(x^{(i)})$ and $\hat{x}^{(i)} = g_\theta(z^{(i)})$.
    b. Compute reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i \Vert x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)} \Vert^{2}$.
    c. Backward pass: compute $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_{e}, \partial \mathcal{L} / \partial W_{d}$ via chain rule.
    d. Update weights using SGD / Adam with learning rate $\eta$.
3. Repeat for many epochs until loss converges.
4. **Validation:** monitor reconstruction loss on a held-out validation set; an AE that generalises well will reconstruct unseen samples with low error. **(7 marks — full marks for diagram + equations + 4-step training + role of bottleneck explained)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Variational Autoencoders (VAE) in detail. Derive the VAE loss function (ELBO) and explain the reparameterisation trick.**

### Model Solution:

**Conceptual Difference from Vanilla AE:** A vanilla AE learns a deterministic mapping $x \mapsto z$, and its latent space has no enforced structure — sampling an arbitrary $z$ may produce garbage. A **VAE** instead learns a *distribution* $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_\phi(x), \sigma_\phi^{2}(x))$, so the latent space is **continuous, smooth, and probabilistic**. This allows **generation** by sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.

**Architecture:** The encoder outputs two vectors $\mu$ and $\sigma$ for each input. A latent code $z$ is sampled using the reparameterisation trick and passed to the decoder. The decoder produces $\hat{x} \sim p_\theta(x \vert z)$.

**Derivation of ELBO Loss:**
Starting from the intractable marginal log-likelihood:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x \vert z)\, p(z)\, dz$$

Introduce the variational distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z \vert x) \frac{p_\theta(x \vert z) p(z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} dz$$

By Jensen's inequality (since $\log$ is concave):

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - KL(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))$$

The right-hand side is the **ELBO**:

$$\mathcal{L}_{ELBO} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)]}_{\text{Reconstruction}} - \underbrace{KL(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))}_{\text{Regularisation}}$$

The VAE loss to **minimise** is the negative ELBO:

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] + KL(q_\phi(z \vert x) \Vert p(z))$$

**Closed-form KL (Gaussian Prior):** With $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$:

$$KL = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left(1 + \log \sigma_j^{2} - \mu_j^{2} - \sigma_j^{2}\right)$$

**Reparameterisation Trick:** To backpropagate through the stochastic sampling node, we write:

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

This isolates the randomness in the parameter-free variable $\varepsilon$, allowing gradients to flow through $\mu$ and $\sigma$.

**Training:** The total loss is backpropagated through both encoder (to tune $\mu, \sigma$) and decoder (to tune the generative distribution). At inference, sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and decode to get a new sample $\hat{x}$. **(7 marks — full marks for derivation steps + closed-form KL + trick explained + generation use-case)**

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss different types of autoencoders. Compare their regularisation strategies.**

### Model Solution:

Autoencoders come in several variants, each adding a different **regularisation** to prevent the trivial identity-mapping solution. The five principal types are:

| # | Type | Regularisation | Loss Function |
|---|------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Vanilla / Undercomplete** | Architectural (small $k$) | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - g(f(x)) \Vert^{2}$ |
| 2 | **Sparse** | Sparsity penalty on latent activations | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert^{2} + \beta \sum_{j} KL(\hat\rho_j \Vert \rho)$ |
| 3 | **Denoising** | Input corruption $\tilde{x} = x + \text{noise}$ | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - g(f(\tilde{x})) \Vert^{2}$ |
| 4 | **Contractive** | Jacobian penalty (local smoothness) | $\mathcal{L} = \Vert x - g(f(x)) \Vert^{2} + \lambda \Vert J_f(x) \Vert_F^{2}$ |
| 5 | **Variational** | KL regularisation on $q_\phi(z \vert x)$ | $\mathcal{L} = -\mathbb{E}_q[\log p(x \vert z)] + KL(q \Vert p)$ |

**Detailed Discussion:**

1. **Vanilla AE:** Bottleneck size $k \ll d$ is the *only* regulariser. Simple, but the latent codes are not constrained to be useful for generation.

2. **Sparse AE:** Allows a *large* $k$, but adds a KL-based penalty to enforce that the average activation $\hat\rho_j$ of each latent unit stays close to a small target $\rho$ (e.g., 0.05). This forces **distributed sparse representations**, similar to the V1 cortex.

3. **Denoising AE:** Corrupts the input via $\tilde{x} \sim \mathcal{C}(\tilde{x} \vert x)$ (e.g., masking 25% of pixels in an image) and trains the network to reconstruct the **clean** $x$. This forces the encoder to learn a *robust* manifold and is highly effective for feature learning.

4. **Contractive AE:** Adds a penalty on the Frobenius norm of the encoder's Jacobian $\Vert J_f(x) \Vert_F^{2}$, encouraging the encoder to be **insensitive to small input perturbations**. This produces a smoother latent manifold.

5. **Variational AE (VAE):** Replaces the deterministic latent with a probabilistic one and adds the KL term. Enables **generation** of new samples.

**Comparison Summary:** All five share the same reconstruction objective but differ in *what additional constraint* they impose. Sparse penalises activations, Denoising penalises input sensitivity empirically, Contractive penalises input sensitivity analytically, and VAE penalises distributional deviation from the prior. **(7 marks — full marks for table + brief explanation of each + comparative statement)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare discriminative and generative models. Explain the architecture and training of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN).**

### Model Solution:

**Discriminative vs. Generative Models:**

| Aspect | Discriminative Model | Generative Model |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| What it learns | $p(y \vert x)$ — boundary | $p(x)$ — full distribution |
| Goal | Classify / regress | Sample new data |
| Examples | Logistic Regression, CNN, RNN | VAE, GAN, PixelCNN |
| Density Estimation | Not required | Required (explicit) or implicit |

**GAN Architecture:**
A **Generative Adversarial Network** (Goodfellow et al., 2014) consists of two networks trained in opposition:

- **Generator** $G_\theta$: Takes a noise vector $z \sim p_z$ (e.g., $\mathcal{N}(0, I)$) and produces a fake sample $G(z)$.
- **Discriminator** $D_\phi$: Takes a sample (real or fake) and outputs a probability that it is real, $D(\cdot) \in [0, 1]$.

**Value Function (Min-Max Game):**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim p_z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$$

**Training Procedure:**
1. Sample a mini-batch of $m$ real images $\{x^{(1)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\}$ from the dataset.
2. Sample $m$ noise vectors $\{z^{(1)}, \ldots, z^{(m)}\}$ from $p_z$.
3. **Update the Discriminator:** Freeze $G$. Compute:

$$\nabla_\phi \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\log D(x^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))\right]$$

   and take an ascent step on $D$.

4. **Update the Generator:** Freeze $D$. Compute:

$$\nabla_\theta \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log(1 - D(G(z^{(i)})))$$

   (or the non-saturating surrogate $-\log D(G(z))$) and take a descent step on $G$.

5. Repeat for many iterations.

**Convergence:** Theoretically, the game reaches a **Nash equilibrium** where $D$ cannot distinguish real from fake ($D(x) = 0.5$ everywhere) and $G$ perfectly models $p_{\text{data}}$.

**Challenges:** Mode collapse, training instability, vanishing gradients for the generator early in training. Mitigations: feature matching, mini-batch discrimination, Wasserstein loss (WGAN), spectral normalisation.

**Applications:** Image synthesis (StyleGAN), super-resolution (SRGAN), image-to-image translation (CycleGAN, Pix2Pix), data augmentation. **(7 marks — full marks for comparison table + GAN architecture + min-max equation + 5-step training procedure + one challenge mentioned + one application)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the AE loss with the VAE loss.** Vanilla AE has *only* reconstruction loss; VAE has *reconstruction + KL regulariser*. Many students write the MSE for both and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **The reparameterisation trick is the most-tested 2-mark sub-question in Module 4.** Memorise the exact equation $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ and the *reason* for using it (gradient flow through stochastic node).
> 3. **In the ELBO derivation**, do not skip Jensen's inequality. Examiners specifically test if students can identify *why* the bound is a *lower* bound — the answer is "$KL \geq 0$".
> 4. **In GAN questions**, the min-max equation must be written correctly: $\min_G \max_D$. Reversing the order is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Do not write code in theory exams.** KTU 2024 Scheme values *equations, diagrams, and derivations* over code. Pseudocode is acceptable; Python syntax is not.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Autoencoders and Generative Models)**

- [ ] **Autoencoder** = Encoder + Bottleneck + Decoder, trained to reconstruct input via MSE / BCE loss.
- [ ] The **bottleneck** forces the network to learn a compressed, salient representation; without it, the AE collapses to the identity mapping.
- [ ] **Five main variants**: Vanilla, Sparse, Denoising, Contractive, Variational — each uses a *different regulariser*.
- [ ] **Sparse AE** regularises by penalising latent activations (KL-divergence from a sparsity target $\rho$).
- [ ] **Denoising AE** trains on corrupted input $\tilde{x}$ to reconstruct clean $x$ — learns robust features.
- [ ] **Contractive AE** adds a Jacobian penalty $\Vert J_f(x) \Vert_F^2$ to enforce local smoothness in the latent manifold.
- [ ] **Variational AE (VAE)** replaces deterministic $z$ with a distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, enabling **generation**.
- [ ] **VAE Loss = Reconstruction Loss + KL Divergence Regulariser** (the negative ELBO).
- [ ] The **ELBO** is a lower bound on $\log p_\theta(x)$ because $KL(q \Vert p) \geq 0$ (Jensen's inequality).
- [ ] The **reparameterisation trick** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \varepsilon$ isolates randomness in a parameter-free variable, allowing backpropagation through the sampling node.
- [ ] **GAN** consists of a Generator and a Discriminator trained in a **min-max** adversarial game.
- [ ] **GAN value function**: $\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}_{x}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))]$.
- [ ] GANs provide **only samples**, not a likelihood — they are *implicit* density models.
- [ ] VAEs provide **blurry but stable** samples; GANs provide **sharp but unstable** samples.
- [ ] **Applications**: denoising, anomaly detection, dimensionality reduction (AEs); image synthesis, super-resolution, data augmentation (VAEs / GANs).
- [ ] **Discriminative models** learn $p(y \vert x)$; **Generative models** learn $p(x)$ or $p(x, y)$.
- [ ] The KL divergence between two Gaussians $\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $\mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ has a **closed form**: $\log(\sigma_2/\sigma_1) + (\sigma_1^2 + (\mu_1-\mu_2)^2)/(2\sigma_2^2) - 1/2$.
- [ ] **Mode collapse** is a famous GAN failure mode: the generator produces only a few distinct samples.
- [ ] **Nash equilibrium** in GANs corresponds to $p_G = p_{\text{data}}$ and $D(x) = 0.5$ for all $x$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Autoencoders (Variational Autoencoder, Undercomplete Autoencoder, Stochastic Encoder, Denoising Encoder)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Autoencoders (Variational Autoencoder, Undercomplete Autoencoder, Stochastic Encoder, Denoising Encoder)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Autoencoder?
An **Autoencoder (AE)** is a special class of unsupervised neural networks designed to learn a compressed, distributed representation (encoding) of input data and reconstruct it (decoding) with minimal reconstruction error. It consists of two mirrored sub-networks:
- **Encoder**: $f_\phi: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ — maps input $x$ to latent code $z$.
- **Decoder**: $g_\theta: \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ — reconstructs $\hat{x}$ from $z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCADT601, Module 4):** Autoencoders are neural networks trained to copy their input to their output, with a bottleneck layer that forces a compressed representation. Variants differ in **how the latent space is structured** and **what noise/regularization is imposed**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Luggage Compression" Metaphor
Imagine packing clothes into a suitcase for a flight:
- The **encoder** is the act of folding and compressing clothes (high-dimensional wardrobe → compact suitcase).
- The **bottleneck** is the limited suitcase volume.
- The **decoder** is unpacking at your destination.
- The **types of autoencoders** differ in *what rules* you follow while packing:
  - *Undercomplete*: You MUST use a small suitcase — the loss of detail is the goal.
  - *Denoising*: You pack deliberately ignoring a few stains (robust features).
  - *Stochastic*: You randomly pick which clothes to pack.
  - *Variational*: You don't pack specific clothes — you pack a *probability distribution* of outfits, from which the decoder samples.

### 1.3 Classification Overview
| **Autoencoder Type** | **Core Idea** | **Latent Space Property** |
|---|---|---|
| Undercomplete | Bottleneck smaller than input | Deterministic, low-dimensional |
| Stochastic | Random sampling in hidden layer | Probabilistic forward pass |
| Denoising | Reconstruct clean $x$ from corrupted $\tilde{x}$ | Robust, noise-invariant |
| Variational | Encoder outputs distribution parameters ($\mu, \sigma^2$) | Continuous, smooth, generative |

> [!NOTE]
> All four variants share the **same encoder–decoder skeleton**; they differ in the **objective function** and **sampling strategy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Undercomplete Autoencoder
An autoencoder is called **undercomplete** when the dimensionality of the latent code $z$ is strictly less than the input dimensionality $d$.

$$\dim(\mathcal{Z}) \ll \dim(\mathcal{X})$$

**Why does it work?**
- Forces the network to learn only the **most salient features**.
- Without additional regularization, it would simply learn the identity function.
- To prevent trivial learning, the network is forced (architecturally) through the bottleneck.

**Objective Function (Standard MSE Loss):**

$$\mathcal{L}_{AE}(\theta, \phi) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| x^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(x^{(i)})) \right\|^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sparsity regularized** versions (adding $\Omega(z) = \sum_j \vert h_j \vert$) are also called *Sparse Autoencoders* — frequently asked in KTU exams.

### 2.2 Stochastic Encoder / Decoder Autoencoder
In **stochastic encoders** and **stochastic decoders**, the forward pass involves **sampling** rather than deterministic mapping.

**Encoder:**

$$z \sim q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu_\phi(x), \sigma_\phi^2(x))$$

**Decoder:**

$$\hat{x} \sim p_\theta(x \vert z)$$

The "stochasticity" can be injected as Bernoulli dropout on activations, or as Gaussian noise on latent variables. The key idea: even for the **same input**, the latent code $z$ can vary across forward passes.

### 2.3 Denoising Autoencoder (DAE)
Proposed by **Vincent et al. (2008)** as a way to learn **robust features** without explicit regularization.

**Procedure:**
1. Corrupt the clean input: $\tilde{x} \sim q(\tilde{x} \vert x)$ (e.g., Gaussian noise, masking noise, salt-and-pepper).
2. Encode: $z = f_\phi(\tilde{x})$.
3. Decode: $\hat{x} = g_\theta(z)$.
4. Loss penalizes the difference between reconstruction and the **clean** input $x$, not the corrupted $\tilde{x}$.

**Objective Function:**

$$\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\| x^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}^{(i)})) \right\|^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The model **never sees the clean input during encoding** — only during loss computation. This forces the encoder to extract features that are **invariant to the noise process**.

### 2.4 Variational Autoencoder (VAE)
Proposed by **Kingma & Welling (2013)**. The VAE is a **generative model** with a principled probabilistic framework.

**Two core distributions:**
- **Encoder (Recognition/Inference model):** $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu, \sigma^2 I)$
- **Decoder (Generative model):** $p_\theta(x \vert z)$

**The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO):**

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$$

**The Reparameterization Trick (to make sampling differentiable):**

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Relevant AE Type** |
|---|---|---|
| $x$ | Input vector | All |
| $\hat{x}$ | Reconstructed input | All |
| $z$ | Latent code | All |
| $\mu, \sigma^2$ | Mean & variance of latent distribution | VAE |
| $\epsilon$ | Standard Gaussian noise sample | VAE |
| $\tilde{x}$ | Corrupted input | Denoising AE |
| $D_{KL}$ | Kullback–Leibler divergence | VAE |
| $\mathcal{L}_{rec}$ | Reconstruction loss (MSE or BCE) | All |
| $\mathcal{L}_{KL}$ | Regularization term | VAE |
| $\beta$ | Weight on KL term (β-VAE) | VAE variant |

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent KTU Mistake:** Confusing $D_{KL}(q \| p)$ with $D_{KL}(p \| q)$. The order **matters** — the VAE uses $D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \| p(z))$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Undercomplete AE**: Feature extraction for downstream classifiers (e.g., industrial defect detection in Kerala MSMEs).
- **Denoising AE**: Medical image denoising (MRI/CT scans), removing sensor noise in IoT.
- **Stochastic AE**: Bayesian uncertainty estimation, reinforcement learning policies.
- **VAE**: Synthetic data generation, drug discovery, anomaly detection in time-series.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the VAE Loss Function (ELBO)
The VAE maximizes the log-likelihood of data. Starting from the marginal log-likelihood:

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x, z) \, dz$$

**Step 1: Introduce the variational distribution $q_\phi(z \vert x)$**

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int q_\phi(z \vert x) \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \, dz$$

**Step 2: Apply Jensen's Inequality** (since $\log$ is concave):

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \int q_\phi(z \vert x) \log \frac{p_\theta(x, z)}{q_\phi(z \vert x)} \, dz = \text{ELBO}$$

**Step 3: Expand the joint probability** $p_\theta(x, z) = p_\theta(x \vert z) p(z)$:

$$\text{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z \vert x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$$

**Step 4: Reparameterization Trick** — replace sampling $z \sim q_\phi(z \vert x)$ with a deterministic function of $\phi(x)$ and noise $\epsilon$:

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon$$

**Step 5: Closed-form KL divergence** for diagonal Gaussian $q_\phi$ and standard Gaussian prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \right)$$

**Final VAE Loss** (to minimize, hence negative ELBO):

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Denoising Autoencoder Gradient
The DAE minimizes reconstruction of $x$ from a corrupted version $\tilde{x}$. The gradient with respect to decoder parameters $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{DAE} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( x^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}^{(i)})) \right) \cdot \nabla_\theta g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}^{(i)}))$$

**Intuition:** Because $\tilde{x}$ is the input, the gradient flows through corrupted features, but the target is the clean $x$. This forces the encoder to learn a **denoising manifold**.

### 3.3 Stochastic Encoder Update Rule
For a stochastic encoder with Gaussian output $q_\phi(z \vert x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$:

$$\mu \leftarrow \mu - \eta \cdot \nabla_\mu \mathcal{L}, \quad \sigma \leftarrow \sigma - \eta \cdot \nabla_\sigma \mathcal{L}$$

The encoder outputs a **distribution**, not a point — increasing robustness to input perturbations.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix
| **Property** | **Undercomplete AE** | **Stochastic AE** | **Denoising AE** | **Variational AE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input fed to encoder | $x$ | $x$ | $\tilde{x}$ (corrupted) | $x$ |
| Latent representation | Deterministic vector | Sampled from $q$ | Deterministic | $\mu, \sigma^2$ parameters |
| Loss includes KL term | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Generative capability | Weak | Weak | Weak | **Strong** |
| Regularization mechanism | Architectural (bottleneck) | Sampling noise | Input corruption | KL divergence |
| Typical activation of latent | Sigmoid/ReLU | ReLU + noise | ReLU/Sigmoid | Linear (μ), Softplus (σ) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Unified Autoencoder Family Architecture
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputA["Input x"] --> EncoderA["Encoder f_phi"]
    EncoderA --> BottleneckA["Latent z"]
    BottleneckA --> DecoderA["Decoder g_theta"]
    DecoderA --> OutputA["Reconstruction x_hat"]
    InputA -.-> LossA["MSE / BCE Loss"]
    OutputA -.-> LossA

    InputB["Input x"] --> Corrupt["Corruption q_tilde_x_given_x"]
    Corrupt --> EncoderB["Encoder f_phi"]
    EncoderB --> BottleneckB["Latent z"]
    BottleneckB --> DecoderB["Decoder g_theta"]
    DecoderB --> OutputB["Reconstruction x_hat"]
    InputB -.-> LossB["Denoising Loss"]
    OutputB -.-> LossB

    InputC["Input x"] --> EncoderC["Encoder f_phi"]
    EncoderC --> Mu["Mean mu"]
    EncoderC --> Sigma["Variance sigma_sq"]
    Mu --> Sample["Reparameterization: z = mu + sigma * epsilon"]
    Sigma --> Sample
    Sample --> DecoderC["Decoder g_theta"]
    DecoderC --> OutputC["Reconstruction x_hat"]
    Mu --> KLLoss["KL Divergence Term"]
    Sigma --> KLLoss
    InputC -.-> RecLoss["Reconstruction Loss"]
    OutputC -.-> RecLoss
    RecLoss --> Total["Total VAE Loss"]
    KLLoss --> Total

    InputD["Input x"] --> EncoderD["Encoder f_phi"]
    EncoderD --> SampleD["Sample z from q_phi_z_x"]
    SampleD --> DecoderD["Decoder g_theta"]
    DecoderD --> OutputD["Reconstruction x_hat"]
    InputD -.-> LossD["Stochastic Loss"]
    OutputD -.-> LossD
```

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — How Each Variant Modifies the Base AE
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base["Base Autoencoder"] --> UC["Undercomplete AE"]
    Base --> SC["Stochastic AE"]
    Base --> DC["Denoising AE"]
    Base --> VC["Variational AE"]

    UC --> ModUC["Modification: Bottleneck dim less than input dim"]
    SC --> ModSC["Modification: Random sampling on z"]
    DC --> ModDC["Modification: Corrupt input x to tilde_x before encoding"]
    VC --> ModVC["Modification: Encoder outputs mu and sigma; KL loss added"]
```

### 4.3 Training Loop Flow (Generic)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start Training"] --> LoadData["Load Batch of x"]
    LoadData --> Preprocess["Optional Corruption or Noise Injection"]
    Preprocess --> Encode["Forward Pass: Encoder f_phi"]
    Encode --> Latent["Sample or Compute z"]
    Latent --> Decode["Forward Pass: Decoder g_theta"]
    Decode --> Recon["Compute Reconstruction x_hat"]
    Recon --> Loss["Compute Loss L"]
    Loss --> Backprop["Backpropagation: Compute Gradients"]
    Backprop --> Update["Update phi and theta"]
    Update --> Check["Convergence Reached?"]
    Check -->|No| LoadData
    Check -->|Yes| End["Return Trained Model"]
```

### 4.4 KTU Exam-Friendly Schematic — Loss Decomposition for VAE
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TotalLoss["Total VAE Loss L_VAE"] --> RecPart["Reconstruction Term: minus E_q log p_theta x given z"]
    TotalLoss --> RegPart["Regularization Term: D_KL q_phi z given x || p_z"]
    RecPart --> MSE["Equivalent to MSE for Gaussian decoder"]
    RegPart --> ClosedForm["Closed form for Gaussian q and p"]
    MSE --> PixelWise["Per-pixel reconstruction error"]
    ClosedForm --> Penalize["Penalizes divergence from prior p z equals N zero I"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, schematic diagrams carry high marks in 14-mark questions. Practice drawing these blocks clearly with labeled arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an Undercomplete Autoencoder and a Sparse Autoencoder.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Undercomplete AE**: Regularization is achieved **architecturally** by forcing $\dim(\mathcal{Z}) < \dim(\mathcal{X})$.
- **Sparse AE**: The latent dimension may even be *larger* than input, but a **sparsity penalty** $\Omega(h) = \sum_j \vert h_j \vert$ is added to the loss to keep most activations near zero.
- Undercomplete = "smaller bottleneck"; Sparse = "bottleneck with sparsity constraint".
- Both prevent identity learning, but through different mechanisms. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the reparameterization trick in a Variational Autoencoder?**

**Model Answer:**
The reparameterization trick expresses the latent sample as a deterministic function of learnable parameters and external noise:

$$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

This allows gradients to flow through the sampling step during backpropagation, making the VAE end-to-end trainable. Without it, stochastic sampling would block gradient flow. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often (1) confuse the two KL terms in the ELBO, (2) forget the reparameterization trick in VAE derivations, and (3) describe denoising AE without specifying the **corruption process**. Always state $q(\tilde{x} \vert x)$ explicitly.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. Derive the ELBO objective function and show the closed-form KL divergence term for Gaussian latent variables.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare a **Denoising Autoencoder** with a **Stochastic Autoencoder** with respect to their objectives, training inputs, and applications. Provide a labeled diagram.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) VAE Architecture and ELBO Derivation:**

**Architecture:**
The VAE has an encoder that outputs **two vectors** — $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\log \sigma_\phi^2(x)$ — instead of a single latent code. A latent sample $z$ is drawn, and a decoder reconstructs $\hat{x}$.

**Step 1: Marginal log-likelihood**

$$\log p_\theta(x) = \log \int p_\theta(x \vert z) p(z) \, dz$$

**Step 2: Variational lower bound using Jensen's inequality**

$$\log p_\theta(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \| p(z))$$

**Step 3: Closed-form KL** with $q = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $p = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$:

$$D_{KL} = -\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left( 1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2 \right)$$

**Step 4: Reparameterization**

$$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$

**Step 5: Final loss to minimize**

$$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z \vert x)}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{J} \left( \mu_j^2 + \sigma_j^2 - \log \sigma_j^2 - 1 \right)$$

The first term = reconstruction loss (e.g., MSE). The second term = regularization toward standard Gaussian prior. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Denoising vs. Stochastic Autoencoder:**

| **Aspect** | **Denoising AE** | **Stochastic AE** |
|---|---|---|
| Input to encoder | Corrupted $\tilde{x}$ | Clean $x$ |
| Source of stochasticity | Corruption process $q(\tilde{x} \vert x)$ | Sampling in latent layer |
| Target for loss | Clean $x$ | Clean $x$ |
| Latent representation | Deterministic | Sampled from $q(z \vert x)$ |
| Primary goal | Learn noise-invariant features | Regularize via stochastic forward pass |
| Application | Image denoising, inpainting | Bayesian inference, RL policies |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DAE["Denoising Autoencoder"]
        X1["x"] --> Noise["Add Noise: tilde_x = x + n"]
        Noise --> Enc1["Encoder"]
        Enc1 --> Z1["Latent z"]
        Z1 --> Dec1["Decoder"]
        Dec1 --> Xhat1["x_hat"]
        X1 -.-> Loss1["Loss: || x - x_hat ||^2"]
    end

    subgraph SAE["Stochastic Autoencoder"]
        X2["x"] --> Enc2["Encoder"]
        Enc2 --> Dist["Output: mu sigma"]
        Dist --> Sample["Sample: z = mu + sigma * epsilon"]
        Sample --> Dec2["Decoder"]
        Dec2 --> Xhat2["x_hat"]
        X2 -.-> Loss2["Loss: || x - x_hat ||^2"]
    end
```

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Undercomplete Autoencoder** in detail. Explain why a simple undercomplete AE can still learn the identity function, and how this is mitigated in practice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Variational Autoencoder** and discuss its advantages over a standard undercomplete autoencoder. Mention any two real-world applications.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Undercomplete Autoencoder:**

- An AE is *undercomplete* if the size of the hidden layer $h$ is smaller than the input $x$: $\dim(h) < \dim(x)$.
- Mathematical formulation:

$$z = f_\phi(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e), \quad \hat{x} = g_\theta(z) = \sigma(W_d z + b_d)$$

- The loss is the standard reconstruction loss:

$$\mathcal{L} = \| x - \hat{x} \|^2$$

- **Problem — Identity Learning**: If the encoder/decoder have enough capacity (e.g., non-linear activations in deep layers) and no constraint, the network can learn to **simply copy** $x$ to $\hat{x}$ by learning a near-identity mapping, even through the bottleneck. For instance, if the encoder is non-linear, the latent space might be a non-linear manifold that still preserves full information.
- **Mitigation strategies**:
  1. **Linear encoder/decoder** with squared loss → forces projection onto a linear subspace (PCA-like).
  2. **Sparsity regularization** $\Omega(h) = \lambda \sum_j \vert h_j \vert$.
  3. **Denoising**: feed corrupted input.
  4. **Contractive penalty**: $\lambda \left\| \nabla_x f_\phi(x) \right\|_F^2$.
  5. **Variational regularization** → leads to VAE. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) VAE Block Diagram and Advantages:**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x"] --> EncE["Encoder Network"]
    EncE --> Mu["mu_phi x"]
    EncE --> Sig["log_sigma_sq_phi x"]
    Mu --> Reparam["Reparameterization z = mu + sigma * epsilon"]
    Sig --> Reparam
    Reparam --> DecD["Decoder Network"]
    DecD --> Xhat["Reconstruction x_hat"]
    Mu --> KL["D_KL divergence with N 0 I"]
    Sig --> KL
    Xhat --> ReconL["Reconstruction Loss MSE or BCE"]
    KL --> TotalL["Total VAE Loss L_VAE"]
    ReconL --> TotalL
```

**Advantages over Undercomplete AE:**
1. **Generative model** — can sample new data by drawing $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
2. **Smooth, continuous latent space** — interpolation between points yields meaningful outputs.
3. **Probabilistic interpretation** — provides uncertainty estimates.
4. **Regularized by construction** — KL term prevents overfitting and trivial identity learning.

**Real-world Applications:**
1. **Anomaly detection** in IoT sensor streams (e.g., Kerala Power System anomaly alarms).
2. **Synthetic medical image generation** for augmenting scarce datasets (e.g., X-ray augmentation for hospital AI labs).
3. **Drug molecule design** in computational chemistry.
4. **Face generation/editing** in computer vision. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- An **autoencoder** has two parts: encoder $f_\phi$ and decoder $g_\theta$, trained to minimize $\|x - \hat{x}\|^2$.
- **Undercomplete AE**: bottleneck dimension smaller than input — regularization by design.
- **Sparse AE**: hidden layer sparse via L1 penalty $\Omega(h) = \lambda \sum \vert h_j \vert$.
- **Stochastic AE**: encoder outputs a **distribution** $q_\phi(z \vert x)$; latent $z$ is sampled.
- **Denoising AE**: trained to reconstruct clean $x$ from corrupted $\tilde{x}$; learns robust features.
- **VAE**: probabilistic encoder outputting $(\mu, \sigma^2)$; uses **ELBO** and **reparameterization trick**.
- **Reparameterization**: $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, where $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$.
- **ELBO**: $\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p_\theta(x \vert z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z \vert x) \| p(z))$.
- **Closed-form KL** (Gaussian VAE): $-\frac{1}{2} \sum_j (1 + \log \sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2)$.
- **VAE loss = reconstruction loss + KL regularization term**.
- **Denoising ≠ Stochastic**: DAE corrupts input; Stochastic AE samples latent.
- **VAE is generative**; Undercomplete/Denoising/Stochastic are not inherently generative.
- **Common confusion**: $D_{KL}(q \| p)$ vs $D_{KL}(p \| q)$ — VAE uses the **first** form.
- **Practical tip**: In KTU derivations, always show Jensen's inequality step explicitly — examiners reward it.
- **Diagram**: Always draw encoder → (μ, σ) → reparameterization → decoder for VAE questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Autoencoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of Autoencoders ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Autoencoder (AE):** An unsupervised neural network trained to copy its input to its output via a **bottleneck** hidden layer, forcing a compressed latent representation. The network comprises two coupled functions — an **encoder** $f_\phi : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Z}$ and a **decoder** $g_\theta : \mathcal{Z} \rightarrow \mathcal{X}$ — such that $g_\theta(f_\phi(x)) \approx x$. An **Application of an Autoencoder** is any downstream engineering or scientific task that exploits the learned low-dimensional latent space $\mathcal{Z}$ or the reconstruction behaviour of $g_\theta \circ f_\phi$ to perform useful inference (compression, denoising, generation, anomaly detection, etc.) on data $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are asked to fax a detailed map of Kerala. You can't send the original (too much ink, too slow). So you: 1. **Compress** the map by tracing only the major roads, rivers, and district boundaries — this is the **encoder**. 2. The receiver gets a small simplified map (the **latent code**). 3. They then **re-draw** a detailed version from your notes — this is the **decoder**. If you train someone to keep doing this until the reconstructed map looks like the original, they have learned the **most important features** of the map. That learned "summary skill" can now be repurposed for many tasks — spotting an incorrect district boundary (anomaly detection), filling in a missing river (inpainting), or generating a brand-new plausible map (generation). This is precisely how applications of autoencoders work. ### 1.3 Why Autoencoders Enable So Many Applications > [!IMPORTANT] > The autoencoder is **not** designed to do any one task. Its real power is that it learns a **compact, semantically meaningful representation** of data. Almost every application is just a clever re-use of either: > - the **latent vector** $z = f_\phi(x)$, OR > - the **reconstruction** $\hat{x} = g_\theta(z)$, OR > - the **reconstruction error** $\mathcal{L}(x, \hat{x})$ used as a scoring signal. ### 1.4 Categorisation of Applications | Category | What is reused | Typical Task | |----------|---------------|--------------| | **Representation Learning** | Latent vector $z$ | Dimensionality reduction, clustering, classification | | **Generative Modelling** | Decoder $g_\theta$ | Image synthesis (VAE), drug discovery | | **Signal Restoration** | Reconstruction $\hat{x}$ | Denoising, inpainting, super-resolution, colorization | | **Density/Anomaly Modelling** | Reconstruction error | Fraud detection, fault diagnosis, medical imaging | | **Compression** | Compressed $z$ + decoder | Image/file compression, communication | --- ### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used Across Applications > [!NOTE] > - **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** $L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum \|x_i - \hat{x}_i\|^2$ — used for reconstruction quality. > - **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** measure in **decibels (dB)**, higher is better. > - **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index):** bounded in $[-1, 1]$, closer to **1** means perceptually closer images. > - **FID (Fréchet Inception Distance):** used for generative models; **lower** is better. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Generic Autoencoder Recap The encoder compresses an input $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into a latent vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^p$ (where $p \ll d$ in the undercomplete case): $$z = f_\phi(x) = \sigma(W x + b)$$ The decoder attempts to reconstruct the input: $$\hat{x} = g_\theta(z) = \sigma'(W' z + b')$$ The parameters $\phi = \{W, b\}$ and $\theta = \{W', b'\}$ are jointly trained to minimise a reconstruction loss: $$\phi^*, \theta^* = \arg\min_{\phi, \theta} \, \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}} \, \mathcal{L}\!\left(x,\, g_\theta(f_\phi(x))\right)$$ ### 2.2 The Variants That Power Specific Applications Each application arises because we modify the loss, the architecture, or the latent-space distribution. This is the **why** behind every downstream use case. #### 2.2.1 Undercomplete AE → Dimensionality Reduction / Feature Extraction - Constraint: $p < d$. - Forces the model to keep only the most informative features. - **Application:** Replace PCA with a nonlinear manifold learner for visualisation (t-SNE/UMAP) and clustering. #### 2.2.2 Denoising AE (DAE) → Image / Signal Denoising - Train on corrupted input $\tilde{x} \sim \mathcal{C}(x)$, reconstruct the clean $x$. - Loss becomes: $$\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \|x - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}))\|^2$$ - **Application:** Remove sensor noise, MRI noise reduction, speech enhancement. #### 2.2.3 Sparse AE → Anomaly / Feature Selection - Add a sparsity penalty on the hidden activations $\hat{\rho}_j$ versus a target $\rho$: $$\mathcal{L}_{Sparse} = \mathcal{L}(x,\hat{x}) + \beta \sum_{j} KL\!\left(\rho \,\|\, \hat{\rho}_j\right)$$ - Forces only a few neurons to fire — useful for **interpretable features**. #### 2.2.4 Contractive AE → Robust Feature Extraction - Adds penalty on the Jacobian $\|\nabla_x f_\phi(x)\|_F^2$ to make the encoder insensitive to small input perturbations. #### 2.2.5 Variational AE (VAE) → Generative Modelling - Treats $z$ as a random variable; encoder outputs parameters $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ of $q(z\mid x)$. - Maximises the **ELBO**: $$\mathcal{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log p(x\mid z)\right] - D_{KL}\!\left(q(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$$ - **Application:** Image synthesis, drug discovery, audio generation, data augmentation. #### 2.2.6 Convolutional AE → Image-domain Restoration - Uses Conv layers instead of Dense layers to preserve spatial structure. - **Application:** Super-resolution, inpainting, colorisation, compression. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise the following equations — they appear frequently in KTU 2024 scheme questions for Module 4. | # | Equation | Meaning | Application | |---|----------|---------|-------------| | 1 | $z = \sigma(W x + b)$ | Encoder forward pass | All AEs | | 2 | $\hat{x} = \sigma'(W' z + b')$ | Decoder forward pass | All AEs | | 3 | $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\|x_i - \hat{x}_i\|^2$ | Reconstruction loss | Basic/DAE/Sparse | | 4 | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\sum_i \left[x_i \log \hat{x}_i + (1-x_i)\log(1-\hat{x}_i)\right]$ | Binary cross-entropy | Image AEs with pixels in $[0,1]$ | | 5 | $\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \|x - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x}))\|^2$ | Denoising loss | Denoising | | 6 | $\mathcal{L}_{Sparse} = \mathcal{L}(x,\hat{x}) + \beta \sum_j \rho \log\frac{\rho}{\hat{\rho}_j} + (1-\rho)\log\frac{1-\rho}{1-\hat{\rho}_j}$ | Sparsity penalty | Sparse AE | | 7 | $\mathcal{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z\mid x)}[\log p(x\mid z)] - D_{KL}\!\left(q(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$ | VAE objective | Generation | | 8 | $D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)\,\|\,\mathcal{N}(0,1)\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_j\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2\right)$ | Closed-form KL for VAE | Generation | | 9 | $s(x) = \|x - g_\theta(f_\phi(x))\|^2$ | Anomaly score | Anomaly detection | | 10 | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right)$ **dB** | Reconstruction quality | Image restoration | > [!WARNING] > Do not confuse **anomaly score** $s(x)$ with classification loss. Autoencoders for anomaly detection are **one-class** — they only learn from normal data and flag anything with high $s(x)$. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Real-world use | |---|---| | Anomaly detection in manufacturing | Detect defective PCBs by monitoring high reconstruction error | | Medical imaging | Denoise low-dose CT/MRI scans before diagnosis | | Generative drug design | VAEs propose novel molecular structures for pharma R\&D | | Recommendation systems | Collaborative filtering via AutoRec — reconstruct user-item ratings | | Image compression | Transmit compact $z$, reconstruct at the edge (IoT) | | Cybersecurity | Detect zero-day attacks by reconstruction-error spikes | | Self-supervised pretraining | Use AE latent features for downstream classification when labels are scarce | --- ## 3.
