Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reconstruction Loss of the Vanilla AE (MSE) We wish to find parameters that minimise the squared reconstruction error over the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$. **Step 1 — Define the model output.** $$\hat{x}^{(i)} = g_\theta\!\left(f_\phi(x^{(i)})\right)$$ **Step 2 — Define the per-sample squared error.** $$L^{(i)} = \left\|x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)}\right\|^2$$ **Step 3 — Average over the dataset (empirical risk).** $$\mathcal{L}(\phi, \theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\|x^{(i)} - g_\theta(f_\phi(x^{(i)}))\right\|^2$$ **Step 4 — Expand the norm.** $$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \left(x_j^{(i)} - \hat{x}_j^{(i)}\right)^2$$ **Step 5 — Gradient w.r.t. decoder weights $W'$.** Using chain rule: $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W'} = \frac{2}{N}\sum_i \left(\hat{x}^{(i)} - x^{(i)}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{x}^{(i)}}{\partial W'}$$ **Step 6 — Update via gradient descent** with learning rate $\eta$: $$W' \leftarrow W' - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W'}$$ This derivation directly justifies how AE training generalises to **all** MSE-based applications: denoising, anomaly detection, compression. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Denoising Autoencoder Objective **Step 1 — Define a corruption process.** $$\tilde{x} \sim \mathcal{C}(x) \quad \text{e.g., Gaussian noise: } \tilde{x} = x + \epsilon, \; \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$$ **Step 2 — The model receives $\tilde{x}$, but must reconstruct the clean $x$.** $$z = f_\phi(\tilde{x}), \qquad \hat{x} = g_\theta(z)$$ **Step 3 — Loss is now computed against the clean target, not the input.** $$\mathcal{L}_{DAE} = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \mathcal{D}} \, \mathbb{E}_{\tilde{x} \sim \mathcal{C}(x)} \left\| x - g_\theta(f_\phi(\tilde{x})) \right\|^2$$ **Step 4 — Connection to score matching** (advanced; for context): The optimal denoiser $g^*$ satisfies $g^*(x) = x + \sigma^2 \nabla_x \log p_{\text{data}}(x)$, showing that learning to denoise **implicitly learns the data distribution's gradient**, which is why DAEs are powerful feature learners. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Anomaly Score Thresholding In anomaly detection, we model only "normal" data and flag $x$ as anomalous if reconstruction error exceeds a threshold $\tau$. **Step 1 — Train the AE on normal samples only.** $$\theta^*, \phi^* = \arg\min \frac{1}{N_{norm}}\sum_{i \in normal} \left\|x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)}\right\|^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute reconstruction errors on a validation set.** $$s^{(i)} = \|x^{(i)} - \hat{x}^{(i)}\|^2$$ **Step 3 — Choose $\tau$ at a desired false-positive rate (FPR).** Suppose errors on validation are $\{s_1, \ldots, s_M\}$ sorted ascending. For FPR $= \alpha$: $$\tau = s_{\lceil (1-\alpha) M \rceil}$$ **Step 4 — Inference.** $$\text{label}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{anomaly}, & s(x) > \tau \\ \text{normal}, & s(x) \le \tau \end{cases}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — VAE ELBO Closed Form (Reparameterisation Trick) **Step 1 — Encoder outputs distribution parameters.** $$q_\phi(z\mid x) = \mathcal{N}\!\left(z;\, \mu_\phi(x),\, \text{diag}(\sigma_\phi^2(x))\right)$$ **Step 2 — Reparameterise to allow backprop.** $$z = \mu_\phi(x) + \sigma_\phi(x) \odot \epsilon, \qquad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$$ **Step 3 — Write the ELBO.** $$\mathcal{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)\right] - D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right)$$ **Step 4 — Closed form of the KL term** with $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$: $$\begin{aligned} D_{KL}\!\left(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z)\right) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Reconstruction term via Monte-Carlo sampling** (often a single sample $z$): $$\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}\!\left[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)\right] \approx \log p_\theta(x\mid z)$$ **Step 6 — Final VAE loss** (negative ELBO, to be minimised): $$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\log p_\theta(x\mid z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2\right)$$ This loss is what allows **generative** applications: after training, sample $z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ and decode via $g_\theta(z)$ to get novel samples. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Generic Autoencoder Architecture & Application Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input x in R^d"]:::input --> B["Encoder f_phi"] B --> C["Latent z in R^p"]:::latent C --> D["Decoder g_theta"] D --> E["Output x_hat in R^d"]:::output C --> F["Dimensionality Reduction"]:::app C --> G["Feature Extraction"]:::app C --> H["Clustering"]:::app E --> I["Denoising"]:::app E --> J["Super Resolution"]:::app E --> K["Colorization"]:::app E --> L["Inpainting"]:::app M["Reconstruction Error"]:::err --> N["Anomaly Detection"]:::app D --> O["Generative VAE Sampling"]:::app classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef latent fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef app fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef err fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram clearly shows the three "reuse points" of an autoencoder — the latent vector, the reconstruction, and the reconstruction error — each spawning a different family of applications. --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow — VAE-based Generative Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["Real Image x"]:::input --> B1["Encoder q_phi z|x"] B1 --> C1["mu_phi x"]:::latent B1 --> C2["sigma_phi x"]:::latent C1 --> R["Reparameterisation
z = mu + sigma . epsilon"] C2 --> R EPS["epsilon ~ N(0,I)"]:::noise --> R R --> D1["Decoder g_theta z"] D1 --> A2["Reconstruction x_hat"]:::output A2 --> L1["Reconstruction Loss
MSE or BCE"] C1 --> L2["KL Divergence Loss"] C2 --> L2 L1 --> T["Total Loss = Recon + KL"] L2 --> T T --> U["Backprop + Update phi, theta"] U --> B1 SAMP["Sample z ~ N(0,I)"]:::noise --> D2["Decoder g_theta z"] D2 --> A3["Generated Image x_new"]:::gen classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef latent fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef noise fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef gen fill:#e0cffc,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow — Anomaly Detection Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Normal Training Data"]:::input --> B["Train Autoencoder"] B --> C["Learned f_phi, g_theta"]:::model C --> D["Inference Stage"] X["New Sample x_test"]:::input --> D D --> R["Reconstruct x_hat"] R --> S["Error s = ||x - x_hat||^2"]:::score V["Validation Set
Normal Only"]:::input --> TH["Choose Threshold tau
at FPR = alpha"] TH --> CMP{"s(x) greater than tau?"} S --> CMP CMP -->|"Yes"| AN["Flag as Anomaly"]:::alert CMP -->|"No"| NO["Predict Normal"]:::ok classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef model fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef score fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef alert fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000 classDef ok fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any three applications of autoencoders and state which part of the AE (encoder output, decoder output, or reconstruction error) each application uses.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Dimensionality Reduction** — uses the **encoder output (latent vector $z$)** for a compressed representation of $x$. 2. **Image Denoising** — uses the **decoder output (reconstruction $\hat{x}$)** after training on noisy–clean pairs. 3. **Anomaly Detection** — uses the **reconstruction error** $\|x - \hat{x}\|^2$ as an anomaly score; high error flags outliers. *(Each correctly matched pair: 1 mark. Full marks for any three valid application–component pairs.)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain why a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) is considered a *generative* model, whereas a vanilla autoencoder is not.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A vanilla AE learns a deterministic mapping $f_\phi, g_\theta$ and its latent space has no enforced structure — points sampled randomly from $\mathcal{Z}$ usually decode to garbage. A VAE, in contrast, regularises the latent distribution $q_\phi(z\mid x)$ to be close to a known prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ via the KL-divergence term. Because the prior covers a continuous region of latent space, we can sample $z \sim p(z)$ and pass it through the decoder to obtain novel, plausible samples. This ability to **sample** is what makes the VAE generative. *(2 marks for KL regularisation, 1 mark for sampling argument.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an autoencoder. Explain the role of the encoder, the latent (bottleneck) layer, and the decoder. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss in detail how autoencoders are applied to **dimensionality reduction** and **anomaly detection**. For anomaly detection, derive the threshold $\tau$ at a given false-positive rate. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution — (a) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Architecture.** An autoencoder consists of three parts: **encoder** $f_\phi : \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^p$, **latent/bottleneck layer** $z \in \mathbb{R}^p$ with $p \ll d$ (undercomplete case), and **decoder** $g_\theta : \mathbb{R}^p \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^d$. A symmetric layered network (e.g., 784-256-64-256-784 for MNIST) is typical. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Encoder.** Maps input to latent code: $z = \sigma(W x + b)$. It compresses, retaining only the most salient features; non-linearity $\sigma$ enables nonlinear manifold learning (unlike PCA). **Step 3 (1 mark) — Latent layer.** The bottleneck forces information compression, preventing identity mapping and forcing generalisation. **Step 4 (2 marks) — Decoder.** Reconstructs: $\hat{x} = \sigma'(W' z + b')$. Trained to minimise $\mathcal{L} = \|x - \hat{x}\|^2$. The reconstruction quality directly measures how informative $z$ is. *(Block diagram: include the same Mermaid as Section 4.1.)* ##### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Dimensionality Reduction.** The latent $z$ is a **nonlinear** alternative to PCA. It can capture curved manifolds (e.g., the Swiss roll), which linear PCA cannot. Downstream tasks — KNN, K-means, classification — are performed on $z$. Compared to PCA, AE has lower reconstruction error on complex data. **Step 2 (1 mark) — Anomaly Detection Principle.** Train AE **only on normal data**. Anomalies will not reconstruct well → high error. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Anomaly Score.** $$s(x) = \|x - g_\theta(f_\phi(x))\|^2$$ **Step 4 (2 marks) — Threshold derivation (already shown in Section 3.3).** Sort validation errors $s_{(1)} \le s_{(2)} \le \ldots \le s_{(M)}$; pick $\tau = s_{\lceil(1-\alpha)M\rceil}$ at FPR $=\alpha$. Flag $x$ as anomaly if $s(x) > \tau$. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** Explain the architecture and training objective of a **Variational Autoencoder (VAE)**. State the ELBO and show the closed-form KL term for a Gaussian prior. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the applications of **convolutional autoencoders** in image denoising and super-resolution. Mention the loss functions and metrics used for evaluation. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution — (a) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Architecture.** Unlike a vanilla AE, the VAE encoder outputs two vectors $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$, parameterising $q_\phi(z\mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2 I)$. A latent sample is drawn via the **reparameterisation trick** $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$, then passed through the decoder. **Step 2 (2 marks) — ELBO.** $$\mathcal{ELBO} = \mathbb{E}_{q_\phi(z\mid x)}[\log p_\theta(x\mid z)] - D_{KL}(q_\phi(z\mid x) \,\|\, p(z))$$ First term → reconstruction; second term → regularisation. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Closed-form KL** with $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)$: $$D_{KL} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{p}\!\left(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2\right)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark) — Final training loss:** $$\mathcal{L}_{VAE} = -\log p_\theta(x\mid z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{j}(\sigma_j^2 + \mu_j^2 - 1 - \log\sigma_j^2)$$ ##### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1 (2 marks) — Convolutional AE architecture.** Replaces Dense layers with **Conv2D** + **MaxPooling** (encoder) and **Conv2DTranspose / UpSampling** (decoder). Preserves spatial locality — critical for image tasks. **Step 2 (2 marks) — Denoising application.** Train on $(x_{\text{noisy}}, x_{\text{clean}})$ pairs. Loss: $\mathcal{L} = \|x_{\text{clean}} - g_\theta(f_\phi(x_{\text{noisy}}))\|^2$. Used in MRI denoising, low-light photography. **Step 3 (2 marks) — Super-resolution application.** Input is low-res $x_{LR}$, output is high-res $\hat{x}_{HR}$. Loss combines pixel MSE with **perceptual loss** (VGG feature distance). Metrics: **PSNR (dB)** and **SSIM**. **Step 4 (1 mark) — Why convolutional?** Weight sharing captures translation-invariant features; far fewer parameters than a Dense AE for the same image size. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students lose marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing the autoencoder with a classifier.** It is unsupervised; do not add a softmax unless you are doing a supervised variant. > 2. **Forgetting the reparameterisation trick.** In VAEs, simply sampling $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ blocks gradients; you must write $z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$. > 3. **Mixing up anomaly detection with classification accuracy.** AE-based anomaly detection uses **reconstruction error**, not accuracy. > 4. **Wrong loss for image data.** Pixels in $[0,1]$ → use **binary cross-entropy**, not MSE, unless you have a strong reason. > 5. **Sampling $z$ from a vanilla AE's latent space** and expecting realistic outputs — this only works for VAEs (or after explicit regularisation). > 6. **Neglecting to mention the bottleneck dimension** $p$ when asked about dimensionality reduction. > 7. **Oversizing the bottleneck** — if $p \ge d$, the AE becomes an identity function and is useless. > 8. **Forgetting units in PSNR** — always mention **dB**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] An autoencoder has two halves: **encoder** $f_\phi$ and **decoder** $g_\theta$, trained with a **reconstruction loss**. - [x] The **bottleneck** $z \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the heart of all applications. - [x] **Undercomplete AE** ($p < d$) → dimensionality reduction, feature extraction. - [x] **Denoising AE** (trained on corrupted $\tilde{x}$, reconstruct clean $x$) → image/signal denoising. - [x] **Sparse AE** (KL penalty on activations) → interpretable feature selection. - [x] **Contractive AE** (Jacobian penalty) → robust feature extraction. - [x] **VAE** (KL regularised latent prior) → **generative** modelling; uses the reparameterisation trick. - [x] **Convolutional AE** → image-domain restoration (denoising, super-resolution, colorisation, inpainting). - [x] **Anomaly detection** uses reconstruction error $\|x - \hat{x}\|^2$ with a threshold $\tau$ chosen at a desired FPR. - [x] Key metrics: **MSE, PSNR (dB), SSIM ∈ [-1, 1], FID** (generative). - [x] Real-world use cases: medical imaging, drug discovery, manufacturing QC, cybersecurity, recommendation systems, IoT compression. - [x] KTU hot topics: derive the MSE loss, derive the threshold $\tau$, derive the closed-form KL for VAE, and explain the reparameterisation trick.

topics.title: Generative Models (Boltzmann Machines, Deep Belief Networks, Generative Adversarial Networks)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADT601 - DEEP LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generative Models (Boltzmann Machines, Deep Belief Networks, Generative Adversarial Networks)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Generative Models?

A **Generative Model** is a class of machine learning models that learn the underlying probability distribution $P(\mathbf{x})$ of the training data so that it can **sample (generate) new data points** that resemble the original dataset. Unlike discriminative models (which learn $P(y \vert \mathbf{x})$), generative models learn $P(\mathbf{x})$ or $P(\mathbf{x}, y)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Generative models are probabilistic models capable of synthesizing novel data samples that match the statistical properties of a given training distribution. They form the backbone of unsupervised representation learning.

## 1.2 The Three Key Architectures in this Module

### A. Boltzmann Machine (BM)
A **Boltzmann Machine** is a stochastic, recurrent neural network based on the principles of statistical mechanics (specifically, the Ising model). It consists of binary stochastic units (neurons) connected symmetrically, governed by an **energy function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-Based View:** Every configuration $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ of visible and hidden units is assigned a scalar **energy**. The network prefers low-energy states. The probability of any state is the Boltzmann distribution:
> 
> $$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z}$$
> 
> where $Z$ is the **partition function** (normalizing constant).

### B. Deep Belief Network (DBN)
A **Deep Belief Network** is a deep, hierarchical generative model composed of a **stack of Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs)**. The hidden layer of one RBM becomes the visible layer of the next, enabling layer-wise unsupervised pre-training followed by supervised fine-tuning.

### C. Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)
A **Generative Adversarial Network** is a framework that pits two neural networks against each other in a zero-sum game: a **Generator** $G$ that maps random noise to fake data, and a **Discriminator** $D$ that distinguishes real data from fake data. They are trained simultaneously until $G$ produces realistic samples.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

| Model | Real-World Analogy | Plain English Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| **Boltzmann Machine** | A vibrating molecular lattice | Imagine atoms in a crystal. Each atom wiggles (is 0 or 1). The atoms prefer configurations that minimize the system's total energy. The Boltzmann Machine does the same — learning which neuron patterns "click" together (low energy) vs. clash (high energy). |
| **Deep Belief Network** | A multi-floor factory assembly line | Each floor (RBM) takes crude parts from the floor below and refines them into higher-level features. Floor 1 sees pixels → edges → corners → objects → concepts. The product (representation) becomes more abstract and meaningful as you go up. |
| **Generative Adversarial Network** | A counterfeiter vs. a detective | The **counterfeiter (Generator)** tries to forge currency so real-looking the detective can't tell. The **detective (Discriminator)** studies both real and fake bills to spot fakes. As training progresses, the counterfeiter becomes so skilled that the forgeries are indistinguishable from genuine currency. |

## 1.4 Why Study These Models?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance in KTU 2024 Context:**
> - **Image synthesis** & super-resolution (GANs)
> - **Drug discovery** (generative molecular design)
> - **Speech & music generation** (RBMs/DBNs)
> - **Anomaly detection** in cybersecurity
> - **Data augmentation** when training data is scarce

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Generative Models Covered

```
Generative Models
├── Energy-Based
│   ├── Boltzmann Machine (BM)
│   ├── Restricted BM (RBM)
│   └── Deep Belief Network (DBN)  ← Stack of RBMs
│
└── Adversarial-Based
    ├── Vanilla GAN
    ├── Conditional GAN (cGAN)
    └── Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boltzmann Machine — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Architecture
A Boltzmann Machine has:
- **Visible units** $\mathbf{v} \in \{0,1\}^{n_v}$: observable data
- **Hidden units** $\mathbf{h} \in \{0,1\}^{n_h}$: latent features
- **Symmetric weights** $W$ between all pairs of units
- **Biases** $\mathbf{b}$ (visible) and $\mathbf{c}$ (hidden)

> [!NOTE]
> **Unrestricted BM Problem:** When hidden-to-hidden and visible-to-visible connections exist, inference becomes **intractable** (exponential time). This motivates the *Restricted* Boltzmann Machine.

### 2.1.2 The Energy Function

The energy of a joint state $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ is:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$$

- $W \in \mathbb{R}^{n_v \times n_h}$ : weight matrix (visible-to-hidden)
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_v}$ : visible bias vector
- $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_h}$ : hidden bias vector

### 2.1.3 Joint Probability Distribution

Using the Boltzmann distribution from statistical mechanics:

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z} = \frac{e^{\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v} + \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h} + \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}}}{Z}$$

The **partition function** normalizes over all $2^{n_v+n_h}$ states:

$$Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Trap:** Many students forget that computing $Z$ requires summing over **all possible configurations**, which is exponential. This is the central computational challenge of BMs.

## 2.2 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) — The Tractable Special Case

### 2.2.1 Structural Restriction
In an RBM, **there are no intra-layer connections**:
- No visible-to-visible edges
- No hidden-to-hidden edges
- Only bipartite connections between $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{h}$

This makes the graph a **bipartite graph**, enabling efficient inference.

### 2.2.2 The Factorization Theorem

Because of the bipartite structure, conditional distributions **factorize** into independent units:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \prod_{j=1}^{n_h} P(h_j \mid \mathbf{v})$$

$$P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n_v} P(v_i \mid \mathbf{h})$$

The closed-form conditional probability for a binary unit:

$$P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(c_j + \sum_{i=1}^{n_v} W_{ij}\, v_i\right)$$

$$P(v_i = 1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(b_i + \sum_{j=1}^{n_h} W_{ij}\, h_j\right)$$

where $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ is the **logistic sigmoid function**.

## 2.3 Deep Belief Network (DBN) — Theory

### 2.3.1 Architecture
A DBN is a stack of RBMs where the hidden layer $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ of layer $l$ serves as the visible layer for layer $l+1$:

$$P(\mathbf{h}^{(l)} \mid \mathbf{h}^{(l-1)}) \text{ is given by RBM-}l$$

### 2.3.2 Greedy Layer-Wise Training (Hinton, 2006)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why layer-wise pre-training works:** It avoids poor local minima in deep networks by training each layer as an RBM first (unsupervised), then fine-tuning the whole network with backpropagation. This was a breakthrough that enabled **deep learning** to be practical pre-2012.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Train RBM-1 on raw input $\mathbf{x} \rightarrow$ produces $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$
2. Treat $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ as input; train RBM-2 $\rightarrow$ produces $\mathbf{h}^{(2)}$
3. Repeat until all layers are trained
4. Add a final supervised layer (e.g., softmax classifier)
5. Fine-tune end-to-end using **backpropagation** with labeled data

## 2.4 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) — Theory

### 2.4.1 The Two Players

- **Generator** $G(\mathbf{z}; \theta_g)$: maps noise $\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})$ (e.g., Gaussian) to fake data $\mathbf{x}_{\text{fake}} = G(\mathbf{z})$
- **Discriminator** $D(\mathbf{x}; \theta_d)$: outputs $P(\text{real} \mid \mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]$

### 2.4.2 The Value Function (Min-Max Game)

$$\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}\,[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}\,[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**Interpretation:**
- $D$ wants to **maximize** the objective: classify real as real ($D(\mathbf{x}) \to 1$) and fake as fake ($D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 0$)
- $G$ wants to **minimize** the objective: fool $D$ by making $D(G(\mathbf{z})) \to 1$

### 2.4.3 Optimal Discriminator

At optimum, $D$ is fixed first. Differentiating $V(D, G)$ w.r.t. $D(\mathbf{x})$:

$$D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_{g}(\mathbf{x})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $p_{g} = p_{\text{data}}$, we get $D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ — the discriminator can no longer distinguish real from fake. The model has **converged** to the true data distribution.

## 2.5 Training Challenges of GANs

| Problem | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode Collapse** | $G$ produces only a few samples (lacks diversity) | Mini-batch discrimination, unrolled GANs |
| **Vanishing Gradients** | $D$ becomes too strong; $G$ receives no learning signal | Use $\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ alternative loss |
| **Training Instability** | Oscillating losses, non-convergence | Spectral normalization, two-timescale update rule (TTUR) |
| **Evaluation** | Hard to quantitatively measure sample quality | **Inception Score (IS)**, **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BM Energy | $E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$ | Scalar energy of joint state |
| 2 | BM Joint Prob. | $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \dfrac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z}$ | Boltzmann distribution |
| 3 | Partition Function | $Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$ | Normalizing constant |
| 4 | Marginal (data) | $P(\mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z}$ | Distribution over visible units |
| 5 | RBM Hidden Cond. | $P(h_j=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(c_j + \sum_i W_{ij} v_i\right)$ | Independent hidden units given $\mathbf{v}$ |
| 6 | RBM Visible Cond. | $P(v_i=1 \mid \mathbf{h}) = \sigma\!\left(b_i + \sum_j W_{ij} h_j\right)$ | Independent visible units given $\mathbf{h}$ |
| 7 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$ | Logistic activation |
| 8 | CD-1 Update | $\Delta W_{ij} = \varepsilon\left(\langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{model}}\right)$ | Contrastive divergence weight update |
| 9 | CD-k | Run Gibbs chain for $k$ steps starting from data | Approximation to gradient |
| 10 | GAN Value | $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$ | Min-max objective |
| 11 | Optimal $D$ | $D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_{g}(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes-optimal discriminator |
| 12 | Generator Loss (alt.) | $J^{(G)} = -\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))]$ | Non-saturating generator loss |
| 13 | Inception Score | $\text{IS} = \exp\!\left(\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}\left[D_{KL}(p(y \mid \mathbf{x}) \,\|\, p(y))\right]\right)$ | Sample quality metric |
| 14 | FID | $\text{FID} = \vert \mu_r - \mu_g \vert^{2} + \text{Tr}\!\left(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}\right)$ | Fréchet distance between feature Gaussians |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RBMs**: Collaborative filtering (Netflix Prize), feature learning, dimensionality reduction
- **DBNs**: Speech recognition (acoustic modeling), image recognition pre-2012
- **GANs**: Image-to-image translation (Pix2Pix, CycleGAN), super-resolution (SRGAN), medical image synthesis, deepfakes detection research

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Energy to Conditional Probability in RBM

We want to derive the closed-form conditional probability $P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v})$ for a single hidden unit in an RBM.

### Step 1: Write the joint probability

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z}$$

### Step 2: Compute the conditional $P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}{P(\mathbf{v})} = \frac{P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}')}$$

Since the **partition function $Z$ and $P(\mathbf{v})$** are constants w.r.t. the numerator sum, we have:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}')}}$$

### Step 3: Expand the energy function

Substitute the energy function:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}$$

$$e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})} = e^{\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v}}\, e^{\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h}}\, e^{\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}}$$

### Step 4: Factor out visible-dependent terms

The term $e^{\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v}}$ does not depend on $\mathbf{h}$ and can be taken out of the sum:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v}}\, e^{\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h}}\, e^{\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}}}{e^{\mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{v}} \sum_{\mathbf{h}'} e^{\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h}'}\, e^{\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}'}} = \frac{e^{\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h}}\, e^{\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} e^{\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h}'}\, e^{\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h}'}}$$

### Step 5: Recognize the exponent as a sum over units

Since $\mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{h} = \sum_{j} c_j h_j$ and $\mathbf{v}^{T}W\mathbf{h} = \sum_{j}(\sum_i W_{ij} v_i) h_j = \sum_j a_j h_j$ where $a_j = \sum_i W_{ij} v_i$:

$$P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\prod_{j} e^{(c_j + a_j) h_j}}{\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} \prod_{j} e^{(c_j + a_j) h_j'}}$$

### Step 6: Show the factorization

The sum over $\mathbf{h}'$ factorizes into a product of $2 \times 1$ sums because $h_j \in \{0,1\}$:

$$\sum_{\mathbf{h}'} \prod_{j} e^{(c_j + a_j) h_j'} = \prod_{j} \sum_{h_j' \in \{0,1\}} e^{(c_j + a_j) h_j'} = \prod_{j} \left(1 + e^{c_j + a_j}\right)$$

### Step 7: Compute the marginal for a single unit

For unit $h_j$:

$$P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \frac{e^{c_j + a_j}}{1 + e^{c_j + a_j}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(c_j + a_j)}}$$

### Step 8: Final closed form

$$\boxed{P(h_j = 1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma\!\left(c_j + \sum_{i=1}^{n_v} W_{ij}\, v_i\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This is why RBMs are tractable. The bipartite structure means hidden units are **conditionally independent** given $\mathbf{v}$, allowing parallel Gibbs sampling rather than exponential enumeration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Learning Rule for RBM Weights

The log-likelihood of a single training example $\mathbf{v}^{(t)}$ is:

$$\log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)}) = \log \sum_{\mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}^{(t)}, \mathbf{h})} - \log Z$$

### Step 1: Take the gradient w.r.t. $W_{ij}$

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)})}{\partial W_{ij}} = -\sum_{\mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(t)}) \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}} - \left(-\sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}) \frac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}}\right)$$

### Step 2: Note that $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial W_{ij}} = -v_i h_j$

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \sum_{\mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(t)})\, v_i h_j - \sum_{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h}} P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})\, v_i h_j$$

### Step 3: Write the gradient as expectations

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \mathbb{E}_{P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v}^{(t)})}[v_i h_j] - \mathbb{E}_{P(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})}[v_i h_j]$$

Equivalently:

$$\frac{\partial \log P(\mathbf{v}^{(t)})}{\partial W_{ij}} = \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$$

### Step 4: The learning rule

$$\boxed{\Delta W_{ij} = \varepsilon\left(\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Trap:** The second term $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$ requires sampling from the full joint distribution, which is intractable. Students often write this formula but forget to mention the **intractability issue**, which is precisely why **Contrastive Divergence (CD-k)** is needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) Approximation

### Step 1: Identify the bottleneck
Computing $\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{model}}$ requires running a Markov chain to equilibrium — extremely slow.

### Step 2: Hinton's Trick
Hinton (2002) proposed: instead of running the chain to equilibrium, start it from the **training data** and run only **$k$ Gibbs steps** (typically $k=1$).

**One Gibbs step** consists of:
- Sample $\mathbf{h} \sim P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$
- Sample $\mathbf{v}' \sim P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h})$
- (CD-1 stops here; CD-k repeats)

### Step 3: The CD-k update rule

$$\Delta W_{ij} \approx \varepsilon\left(\langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j \rangle_{\text{recon}}\right)$$

where the second term uses samples after $k$ Gibbs steps. This is a **biased but fast** estimator of the true gradient.

### Step 4: Why CD-1 works empirically
The bias is small in practice because:
1. The data distribution is close to the model distribution (especially after some training)
2. The chain starting from data quickly moves to a region of high model probability

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Optimal Discriminator of a GAN

### Step 1: Set up the objective

$$V(D, G) = \int p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} + \int p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z}) \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))\, d\mathbf{z}$$

### Step 2: Change variable in the second integral

Let $\mathbf{x} = G(\mathbf{z})$, with the induced generator density $p_g(\mathbf{x})$:

$$V(D, G) = \int p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} + \int p_{g}(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x}))\, d\mathbf{x}$$

$$V(D, G) = \int \left[p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x}) + p_{g}(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x}))\right] d\mathbf{x}$$

### Step 3: Maximize pointwise

For fixed $G$, the integrand is a function of $D(\mathbf{x})$ alone. Differentiate w.r.t. $D(\mathbf{x})$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial D(\mathbf{x})}\left[p_{\text{data}} \log D + p_{g} \log(1 - D)\right] = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{D} - \frac{p_{g}}{1 - D} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $D$

$$p_{\text{data}}(1 - D) = p_{g} D \;\Longrightarrow\; p_{\text{data}} - p_{\text{data}} D = p_{g} D \;\Longrightarrow\; D = \frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_{g}}$$

### Step 5: Final form

$$\boxed{D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_{g}(\mathbf{x})}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Global Optimum of GAN Training

### Step 1: Substitute $D^{*}$ back into $V(D^{*}, G)$

$$V(D^{*}, G) = \int p_{\text{data}} \log\frac{p_{\text{data}}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}\, d\mathbf{x} + \int p_{g} \log\frac{p_{g}}{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}\, d\mathbf{x}$$

### Step 2: Recognize the KL divergence

$$V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + D_{KL}\!\left(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right) + D_{KL}\!\left(p_{g} \,\|\, \tfrac{p_{\text{data}} + p_g}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3: Apply the JSD identity

The sum of two KL divergences is the **Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD)** up to a constant:

$$V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{JS}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_{g})$$

### Step 4: Minimize over $G$

Since $D_{JS} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_{\text{data}} = p_g$:

$$\min_{G} V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 \quad \text{at} \quad p_{g} = p_{\text{data}}$$

This proves the **global optimum** of the GAN objective corresponds to the generator perfectly modeling the data distribution.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| Aspect | RBM | DBN | GAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training** | Contrastive Divergence (CD-k) | Greedy layer-wise + backprop | Alternating SGD on $D$ and $G$ |
| **Sampling** | Gibbs sampling | Top-down ancestral sampling | Forward pass through $G$ |
| **Likelihood** | Tractable up to $Z$ | Approximate | Not explicitly modeled |
| **Inference** | Easy ($P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$ analytic) | Hard (variational needed) | Implicit via $G$ |
| **Pros** | Principled, probabilistic | Deep representation learning | Sharp, realistic samples |
| **Cons** | $Z$ intractable | Slow training | Mode collapse, unstable |
| **Key Hyperparams** | $k$ in CD-k, $\varepsilon$, $\mathbf{v} \in \{0,1\}$ | # layers, RBM types | $n_{\text{noise}}$, batch size, $D$ updates per $G$ update |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) Architecture

The RBM has a bipartite structure with **visible** and **hidden** layers, with no intra-layer connections.

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1["VISIBLE UNIT V1"] --- h1["HIDDEN UNIT H1"]
    v1 --- h2["HIDDEN UNIT H2"]
    v1 --- h3["HIDDEN UNIT H3"]
    v1 --- h4["HIDDEN UNIT H4"]
    v2["VISIBLE UNIT V2"] --- h1
    v2 --- h2
    v2 --- h3
    v2 --- h4
    v3["VISIBLE UNIT V3"] --- h1
    v3 --- h2
    v3 --- h3
    v3 --- h4
    v4["VISIBLE UNIT V4"] --- h1
    v4 --- h2
    v4 --- h3
    v4 --- h4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each edge has an associated weight $W_{ij}$. Note the **absence of edges within** the visible layer (e.g., v1–v2) and within the hidden layer (e.g., h1–h2). This bipartite restriction is what makes inference tractable.

## 4.2 Deep Belief Network (DBN) — Stack of RBMs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["RAW INPUT PIXELS LAYER 0"] --> B["RBM 1 EDGES AND TEXTURES"]
    B --> C["RBM 2 PARTS AND MOTIFS"]
    C --> D["RBM 3 OBJECTS AND CONCEPTS"]
    D --> E["SOFTMAX LABEL LAYER 4"]
    B -.->|UNTIE WEIGHTS| F["UNSUPERVISED PRETRAINING"]
    F -.-> G["SUPERVISED FINE TUNING"]
    E --> G
```

**Layer-wise training process:**

1. **RBM-1** trained on raw input $\mathbf{x}$ using CD-1
2. **RBM-2** trained on hidden activations $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ from RBM-1
3. **RBM-3** trained on $\mathbf{h}^{(2)}$ from RBM-2
4. **Softmax classifier** added on top, all weights fine-tuned via backpropagation

## 4.3 Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    Z["RANDOM NOISE Z PRIOR PZ"] --> G["GENERATOR G"]
    G --> XG["FAKE DATA GZ"]
    XR["REAL DATA X FROM PDATA"] --> D["DISCRIMINATOR D"]
    XG --> D
    D --> OUT["PROBABILITY REAL OR FAKE"]
    G -.->|MINIMAX UPDATE| D
    D -.->|FEEDBACK| G
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow:**

| Stage | Component | Input | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noise Sampler | Seed | Draw $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ | Latent vector |
| 2 | Generator $G$ | $\mathbf{z}$ | Deep deconv / MLP forward pass | Fake image $\mathbf{x}_{\text{fake}}$ |
| 3 | Real Data Loader | Dataset index | Sample $\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}$ | Real image $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 4 | Discriminator $D$ | $\mathbf{x}$ or $\mathbf{x}_{\text{fake}}$ | Convolutional scoring network | $D(\mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]$ |
| 5 | Loss Computer | $D(\mathbf{x}), D(G(\mathbf{z}))$ | BCE loss for $D$; non-saturating for $G$ | Scalar loss values |
| 6 | Optimizer (Adam) | Loss, parameters | Update $\theta_d$ then $\theta_g$ | Updated weights |

## 4.4 GAN Training Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["STEP 1 SAMPLE MINI BATCH FROM DATA"] --> S2["STEP 2 SAMPLE NOISE VECTOR Z"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 GENERATE FAKE XFAKE GZ"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 TRAIN DISCRIMINATOR D FIX G"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 COMPUTE DLOSS REAL LOG D X FAKE LOG 1 D GZ"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 BACKPROP AND UPDATE D"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7 TRAIN GENERATOR G FIX D"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8 COMPUTE GLOSS FAKE LOG D GZ NON SATURATING"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9 BACKPROP AND UPDATE G"]
    S9 --> S10{"STEP 10 CONVERGED CHECK"}
    S10 -->|NO| S1
    S10 -->|YES| S11["STEP 11 OUTPUT TRAINED G FOR INFERENCE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical ratio:** In practice, $D$ is updated $k_d$ times (often $k_d=1$) for every single update of $G$ to prevent $D$ from becoming too strong too early.

## 4.5 Comparison: DBN vs GAN Data Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DBN
        A1["INPUT X"] --> A2["RBM 1"] --> A3["H1"] --> A4["RBM 2"] --> A5["H2"] --> A6["SOFTMAX"]
    end
    subgraph GAN
        B1["NOISE Z"] --> B2["G"] --> B3["FAKE X"]
        B4["REAL X"] --> B5["D"]
        B3 --> B5
        B5 --> B6["REAL FAKE LABEL"]
    end
```

**Architectural philosophy:**

- **DBN** is **bottom-up inference, top-down generation**, with explicit layer-wise probabilistic structure
- **GAN** is **end-to-end adversarial**, with implicit density modeling and direct feedforward generation

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a Restricted Boltzmann Machine. How does it differ from a general Boltzmann Machine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) is a two-layer stochastic neural network with a visible layer $\mathbf{v}$ and a hidden layer $\mathbf{h}$, having a **bipartite** connection structure — no visible-to-visible or hidden-to-hidden connections.
- **Difference 1 (1 mark):** In a general BM, every pair of units is connected, making inference intractable ($Z$ hard to compute and conditional distributions don't factorize). In an RBM, the bipartite structure allows conditional independence: $P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h})$ factorize over units.
- **Difference 2 (1 mark):** As a result, RBM allows efficient Gibbs sampling and closed-form conditional probabilities $P(h_j=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(c_j + \sum_i W_{ij} v_i)$, whereas general BM requires expensive MCMC.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is mode collapse in GANs? Mention one technique to mitigate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Mode collapse is a failure mode of GAN training where the **Generator** maps many different input noise vectors to the same (or very similar) output samples. The generator effectively "collapses" to producing only a few modes of the data distribution, ignoring other regions of $p_{\text{data}}$. This happens because the generator finds a small set of outputs that consistently fool the discriminator.
- **Mitigation (1 mark):** **Mini-batch discrimination** — feed the discriminator features from an entire batch, allowing it to detect lack of diversity in the generator's outputs; OR use **unrolled GANs**, where the generator sees the discriminator's gradient through $k$ future update steps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - When answering BM/RBM derivations, **always write the energy function first**, then the joint probability, and then the conditional. Skipping the energy step loses 2–3 marks.
> - For GANs, do **not** confuse the value function with a standard loss; emphasize the min-max structure.
> - In DBNs, students often forget to mention **why** greedy layer-wise training helps (avoids local minima, leverages RBMs' tractability).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Choose either A or B]

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the architecture and training procedure of a Deep Belief Network. How does greedy layer-wise pre-training help in training deep networks?

**Model Solution:**

**1. DBN Architecture (3 marks):**
A Deep Belief Network is a deep probabilistic generative model composed of a stack of $L$ Restricted Boltzmann Machines. The visible layer $\mathbf{v}^{(0)}$ represents input data, and each subsequent layer's hidden units $\mathbf{h}^{(l)}$ are connected to the layer above through a symmetric weight matrix $W^{(l)}$.

Mathematically, the joint distribution of the top two layers is an RBM:

$$P(\mathbf{h}^{(L-1)}, \mathbf{h}^{(L)}) \propto e^{\mathbf{b}^{(L-1)T}\mathbf{h}^{(L-1)} + \mathbf{c}^{(L)T}\mathbf{h}^{(L)} + \mathbf{h}^{(L-1)T}W^{(L)}\mathbf{h}^{(L)}}$$

and lower layers are conditionally modeled as sigmoid belief nets:

$$P(\mathbf{h}^{(l-1)} \mid \mathbf{h}^{(l)}) = \prod_i \sigma\!\left(b_i^{(l-1)} + \sum_j W_{ij}^{(l)}\, h_j^{(l)}\right)$$

**2. Greedy Layer-Wise Pre-training (3 marks):**
- Train RBM-1 using **Contrastive Divergence (CD-1)** on raw input $\mathbf{x}$. The trained RBM-1 maps $\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{h}^{(1)}$.
- Freeze $W^{(1)}$ and treat $\mathbf{h}^{(1)}$ as input to train RBM-2 using CD-1 again. Obtain $\mathbf{h}^{(2)}$.
- Repeat for $L$ layers.
- Each layer learns to reconstruct its input (the previous layer's activations) rather than the original data, building increasingly abstract features.

**3. Why It Helps (1 mark):**
- Pre-training initializes weights in a **good region of parameter space** (near a good local minimum), so backpropagation fine-tuning converges faster and to better solutions.
- It acts as an **unsupervised regularizer**, especially valuable when labeled data is scarce.
- It avoids the **vanishing gradient problem** in deep networks, which was a major obstacle before 2006.

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Derive the optimal discriminator $D^{*}(\mathbf{x})$ for a GAN. Show that the global minimum of the GAN objective is achieved when $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$.

**Model Solution:**

**1. GAN Objective (1 mark):**

$$V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**2. Change of Variables (2 marks):**
Let $\mathbf{x} = G(\mathbf{z})$. The induced generator distribution is $p_g(\mathbf{x})$. Then:

$$V(D, G) = \int p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) \log D(\mathbf{x})\, d\mathbf{x} + \int p_g(\mathbf{x}) \log(1 - D(\mathbf{x}))\, d\mathbf{x}$$

**3. Pointwise Maximization (2 marks):**
Differentiate the integrand w.r.t. $D(\mathbf{x})$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{D(\mathbf{x})} - \frac{p_g(\mathbf{x})}{1 - D(\mathbf{x})} = 0$$

Solving:

$$D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x})}{p_{\text{data}}(\mathbf{x}) + p_g(\mathbf{x})}$$

**4. Global Minimum (2 marks):**
Substitute $D^{*}$ back:

$$V(D^{*}, G) = -\log 4 + 2 \cdot D_{JS}(p_{\text{data}} \,\|\, p_g)$$

Since $D_{JS} \geq 0$ with equality iff $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$:

$$V(D^{*}, G) \big|_{p_g = p_{\text{data}}} = -\log 4$$

This is the **global minimum**, and at this point $D^{*}(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}$ everywhere — the discriminator cannot tell real from fake.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Discuss the architecture, energy function, and probability distribution of a Boltzmann Machine. Explain why general Boltzmann Machines are intractable and how the Restricted Boltzmann Machine solves this problem.

**Model Solution:**

**1. BM Architecture (1 mark):**
A BM consists of stochastic binary units split into visible units $\mathbf{v}$ and hidden units $\mathbf{h}$. Every unit pair is symmetrically connected by weights $W_{ij}$, with no directionality (unlike feedforward networks). The state evolves stochastically based on energy minimization.

**2. Energy Function (2 marks):**
The energy of a joint state $(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})$ is:

$$E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\sum_i b_i v_i - \sum_j c_j h_j - \sum_{i,j} W_{ij} v_i h_j - \sum_{i \neq i'} W_{ii'} v_i v_{i'} - \sum_{j \neq j'} W_{jj'} h_j h_{j'}$$

The first two terms are biases; the rest are pair-wise unit interactions (visible-visible, hidden-hidden, and visible-hidden).

**3. Joint Probability (1 mark):**

$$P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = \frac{e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}}{Z}, \quad Z = \sum_{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}} e^{-E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h})}$$

**4. Intractability of General BM (2 marks):**
- The **partition function $Z$** requires summation over $2^{n_v + n_h}$ states — exponential.
- The **conditional probability** $P(h_j \mid \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}_{\neq j})$ does not factorize because each $h_j$ is connected to other hidden units.
- Exact inference (computing marginals) is therefore **computationally intractable** for all but trivial sizes.

**5. RBM Solution (1 mark):**
The Restricted Boltzmann Machine removes all intra-layer connections (visible-to-visible and hidden-to-hidden). This bipartite structure makes $P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h})$ **factorize** over units, enabling:
- Closed-form conditional probabilities (sigmoid activations)
- Block Gibbs sampling (parallel updates)
- Tractable inference in $O(n_v n_h)$ per step

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Explain the training procedure of a GAN with the value function. Describe the generator and discriminator update rules, and explain the role of non-saturating loss for the generator.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Value Function (2 marks):**

$$\min_G \max_D V(D, G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim p_{\text{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim p_{\mathbf{z}}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

**2. Discriminator Update (2 marks):**
For fixed $G$, train $D$ to maximize $V(D, G)$. The discriminator loss (to minimize) is:

$$J^{(D)} = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\log D(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)})))\right]$$

Using stochastic gradient descent with mini-batches of $m$ real and $m$ fake examples.

**3. Generator Update (1.5 marks):**
Naïvely, $G$ minimizes $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$. However, early in training, $D$ easily rejects fake samples, so $\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))$ **saturates** (gradient → 0). The standard fix is the **non-saturating loss**:

$$J^{(G)} = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \log D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))$$

This gives strong gradients when $D$ rejects $G$'s output, especially early in training.

**4. Training Algorithm Outline (1.5 marks):**
- **For** each training iteration:
  - Sample $m$ noise samples $\{\mathbf{z}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{z}^{(m)}\}$ from $p_{\mathbf{z}}$
  - Sample $m$ real examples $\{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(m)}\}$ from dataset
  - Update $D$ by ascending its stochastic gradient: $\nabla_{\theta_d} \tfrac{1}{m} \sum_i [\log D(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) + \log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)})))]$
  - Sample $m$ fresh noise samples
  - Update $G$ by **descending** the non-saturating gradient: $\nabla_{\theta_g} \tfrac{1}{m} \sum_i \log D(G(\mathbf{z}^{(i)}))$
- **Use** Adam optimizer with separate learning rates; $\beta_1 = 0.5$ works well in practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Generative Models (Module 4)**

### A. Boltzmann Machines
- ☐ BM = energy-based stochastic network; learns $P(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = e^{-E}/Z$
- ☐ Energy: $E(\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{h}) = -\mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{h} - \mathbf{v}^T W \mathbf{h}$ (for RBM form)
- ☐ General BM is **intractable** due to $Z$ and non-factorizing conditionals
- ☐ RBM restricts to bipartite graph → tractable inference via **sigmoid conditionals**
- ☐ Conditional: $P(h_j=1 \mid \mathbf{v}) = \sigma(c_j + \sum_i W_{ij} v_i)$
- ☐ Training: Maximum Likelihood → gradient = $\langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{model}}$

### B. Contrastive Divergence
- ☐ CD-k approximates the model expectation using only $k$ Gibbs steps from data
- ☐ CD-1: One positive phase + one negative (reconstruction) phase
- ☐ Biased but fast estimator; works well empirically
- ☐ Update rule: $\Delta W_{ij} = \varepsilon(\langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{data}} - \langle v_i h_j\rangle_{\text{recon}})$

### C. Deep Belief Networks
- ☐ DBN = **Stack of RBMs** + final classifier layer
- ☐ Greedy layer-wise pre-training: train each RBM in turn using CD
- ☐ Final fine-tuning via backpropagation with labels
- ☐ DBN breakthrough by Hinton (2006) revived deep learning
- ☐ Applications: speech recognition, image classification pre-AlexNet

### D. Generative Adversarial Networks
- ☐ GAN = **Generator $G$** vs **Discriminator $D$** in a min-max game
- ☐ Value: $V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log(1 - D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$
- ☐ Optimal $D$: $D^*(\mathbf{x}) = p_{\text{data}} / (p_{\text{data}} + p_g)$
- ☐ Global optimum at $p_g = p_{\text{data}}$, with $V = -\log 4$
- ☐ Non-saturating generator loss: $J^{(G)} = -\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}}[\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))]$
- ☐ Common problems: **mode collapse**, **vanishing gradients**, **training instability**
- ☐ Evaluation metrics: **Inception Score (IS)** ↑, **Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)** ↓

### E. Numerical Constants / Values to Remember
- ☐ Generator loss at global optimum: $V(D^*, G^*) = -\log 4 \approx -1.386$
- ☐ Inception Score: higher is better; typical good GANs achieve IS > 8 on CIFAR-10
- ☐ FID: lower is better; SOTA diffusion models achieve FID < 3 on CIFAR-10

### F. Key Comparisons to Remember
- ☐ **RBM vs Autoencoder:** RBM is **stochastic & probabilistic**; autoencoder is **deterministic** reconstruction
- ☐ **DBN vs Deep NN:** DBN has **unsupervised pre-training** layer-wise; pure deep NN trained end-to-end
- ☐ **GAN vs VAE:** GAN is **implicit density** + sharp samples; VAE is **explicit** density + blurry samples

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing the **partition function $Z$** with the loss function — $Z$ is a normalization constant.
> 2. Writing $D(\mathbf{x}) = 1$ for fake data — it should be $0$.
> 3. Forgetting that in RBM, $P(\mathbf{h} \mid \mathbf{v})$ and $P(\mathbf{v} \mid \mathbf{h})$ are **independently sampled per unit**.
> 4. Stating GAN training is "minimizing a loss" — it is a **min-max game**, not a minimization.
> 5. In DBN diagrams, drawing connections between hidden units of the **same** layer — the RBM restriction forbids this.
